Now classify them by what the research actually IS:

Categories:
- basic_research: Understanding mechanisms, pathways, disease processes (knowledge output)
- biotools: Developing research tools, assays, methods, platforms (tool output)
- therapeutics: Developing drugs, treatments, therapies (treatment output)
- diagnostics: Developing clinical tests, biomarker panels (diagnostic test output)
- medical_device: Developing physical devices for patients (device output)
- digital_health: Patient-facing software, apps, clinical decision support
- other: Health services, behavioral interventions, epidemiology

application_id: 10998020
activity_code: F31
title: Randomized controlled trial of a single-session mechanism-focused intervention for suicidal thoughts and behaviors
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF KENTUCKY
phr: Suicide is a leading cause of death in the U.S. and worldwide, with an even larger number of people experiencing suicidal thoughts and behaviors (STBs); however, extant (brief and traditional) treatments appear to have a limited impact on suicidal ideation. Focusing on the core mechanisms that give rise to suicidal ideation, as an “upstream” target that may have an impact on suicide attempts and deaths “downstream,” may represent a more potent and parsimonious approach to care. The proposed project will address the critical need of integrating established suicide-specific mechanisms that maintain suicidal ideation into a disseminable intervention for STBs.

application_id: 10997925
activity_code: F31
title: Plasticity of Layer 6 corticothalamic pyramidal cell circuits in the adult primary auditory cortex following visual deprivation
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Cochlear implants effectively restore hearing, but their efficacy diminishes with age due to adults having less neural plasticity than children. Research shows that dark exposure in adult mice can renew auditory plasticity, with corticothalamic neurons in the auditory cortex potentially playing a key role. Therefore, understanding the impact of dark exposure on these neurons could improve hearing loss treatments in adults and provide insights into the brain's adaptation to significant input loss, like in stroke and neurodegenerative diseases.

application_id: 11112150
activity_code: K01
title: The Ano4 channel in the AP is required for orexigenic action of asprosin.
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Extensive research efforts have revealed significant insights into how asprosin controls eating behavior and
blood glucose levels, but the role of Ano4, a calcium-activated chloride channel protein, in the mechanism has
yet to be examined. I will investigate the function of Ano4 in the area postrema, mediating asprosin’s action on
feeding behavior and glucose homeostasis. These studies could unravel the neurobiological mechanism of
asprosin and further identify Ano4 as a novel target for treating metabolic disorders in human.

application_id: 10863012
activity_code: F32
title: Leveraging Choroid Plexus M-CSF Signaling to Prevent Inflammatory-Driven Hydrocephalus
org_name: BOSTON CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Pediatric hydrocephalus is a life-threatening condition that is primarily caused by in-utero and perinatal infections and intraventricular hemorrhage. The underlying molecular pathology of how neuroinflammation from infection or hemorrhage during critical developmental periods leads to hydrocephalus is poorly understood. This proposal implements single-cell transcriptomics combined with core methods for studying the choroid plexus (ChP) to uncover the role that the ChP has in mediating neuroinflammation that can be leveraged for diagnostics and therapeutics to prevent the progression of inflammatory-driven hydrocephalus.

title: The roles of Hsd3b7 and Foxp2 in Lower Jaw Development
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF SOUTHERN CALIFORNIA
phr: Project Narrative Craniofacial birth defects commonly arise due to altered patterning of the cranial neural crest-derived cells that form the jaw skeleton and connective tissues. This project investigates two novel jaw-specific mechanisms that regulate the proportion of skeletal and connective tissue cell types in the developing face. Findings from these studies will inform how altered Hedgehog signaling and chromatin accessibility contribute to a variety of birth defects affecting the jaw.

org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Parkinson’s disease is a complex neurodegenerative disorder with a large unexplained genetic component and the role of rare noncoding variation remains poorly understood. This research proposal aims to improve our understanding of the genetic architecture of Parkinson’s disease by utilizing deep learning and functional genomic approaches to characterize the impact on putatively functional rare noncoding variants on gene regulation. This project will improve our understanding of noncoding variation found in Parkinson’s disease patients, providing a step towards improved understanding of disease pathogenesis and patient prognostication.

application_id: 11142580
activity_code: T32
title: Training Program in Molecular Hematology
org_name: BRIGHAM AND WOMEN'S HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative
Research in hematology has been at the cutting edge of medical advances for decades,
providing novel paradigms for the study of human disease. Nevertheless, hematologic diseases
remain major public health problems, and the number of physician-scientists dedicated to the
study of hematology is declining. This grant is focused on clinician scientists and aims to
support a laboratory-based training program to nurture the next generation of hematologists
focused on research in molecular hematology.

application_id: 11145159
activity_code: T32
title: Translational Research Training in Sleep and Circadian Science
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Sleep and circadian (24-hour) rhythms are fundamental functions of human beings that promote physical,
mental, and cognitive health, but show substantial disparities across the population. Given the recent explosion
of scientific knowledge about sleep and circadian rhythms, the scientific workforce in these areas remains small
and insufficiently diverse. The aim of this grant is to train a new and diverse generation of sleep and circadian
rhythms researchers, including doctoral, predoctoral, and short-term scholars.

org_name: CALIFORNIA STATE UNIVERSITY LONG BEACH
phr: Project Narrative
The Bridges to the Doctorate Training Program at California State University, Long Beach will partner with
University of California, Irvine to form a seamless bridge to support biomedical Master’s degree students

title: Variability in the Perinatal Hormone Environment and Risk of Autism Spectrum Disorder
org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Autism spectrum disorder (ASD) prevalence is increasing, yet its etiology is largely unknown. It is important to explore possible causes of ASD that occur in the perinatal period, as early identification leads to better outcomes. The proposed analysis uses the Study to Explore Early Develop (SEED) to determine if variability in the perinatal hormonal environment is associated with ASD. The proposed aims align with NICHD’s strategic goal “setting the foundation for healthy pregnancies and lifelong wellness”.

phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Spinal cord injury (SCI) is a devastating condition that affects about 250,000 Americans with 17,700 new cases

shape the future of SCI research. T32 funding will enable this successful program to grow, training more

application_id: 10952552
activity_code: K01
title: Manipulation of MHC expression using TGF-beta2 to reduce mesenchymal stem cell immunogenicity
org_name: UNIV OF MARYLAND, COLLEGE PARK
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Allogeneic mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) are a promising cell source for treating inflammatory and immune- mediated diseases, but are targeted for death by the recipient immune system due to mismatched-major histocompatibility complex (MHC) molecules expressed on the cell surface. The aim of this proposal is to analyze how transforming growth factor-β2 (TGF-β2) regulates MHC expression in MSCs and how the recipient immune system responds to TGF-β2-treated MSCs in vivo. Results from the completion of these studies will support the generation of allogeneic donor MSCs that can be used “off-the-shelf” manner for safe and efficacious treatment.

application_id: 10852853
activity_code: F30
title: Microbe dependent mechanisms that antagonize intestinal injury repair
org_name: CASE WESTERN RESERVE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Some patients with Crohn Disease have a yeast called Debaryomyces hansenii present within their ulcers, and mouse models of intestinal injury show that this yeast impairs intestinal healing. The research described here will help understand the mechanisms through which this yeast persists within the intestine and how it blocks injury repair. This project will increase our understanding of how microbes that are associated with Crohn Disease can affect disease course, with the goal of identifying new targets for future therapeutic development to benefit Crohn Disease patients with D. hansenii infection.

title: Training Grant in Precision Cancer Control
org_name: RESEARCH INST OF FOX CHASE CAN CTR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The ability to precisely target individuals with cancer and at-risk populations with evidence-based tools to address
individual, sociocultural, or environmental factors is likely to lead to the greatest improvements in cancer

title: Characterization of Longitudinal EEG Biomarkers in Chronic Low Back Pain
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Prognostic biomarkers support precision-based treatments yet are lacking for patients with chronic lower back pain. Here, I aim to use advanced electroencephalogram-based neuroimaging strategies to identify and characterize biomarkers of dysfunctional brain networks (resting-state, cognitive, and emotional) in patients with chronic low back pain across time. application_id: 11165522
activity_code: F31
title: Gene regulatory programs underlying differences in neural crest developmental potential in the head and neck
org_name: CALIFORNIA INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY
phr: Project Narrative
The neural crest of the head and neck contributes to many essential tissues including the craniofacial
skeleton, parts of the cardiac mesenchyme, and the enteric nervous system. The ability of the neural
crest to form ectomesenchymal tissue, such as craniofacial cartilage, is lost in lower parts of the body.
Further study of the gene regulatory differences that establish these differential cell fates will inform
our understanding of the neural crest while carrying potential implications for underlying craniofacial
birth defects and regenerative therapies targeted at generation of ectomesenchyme tissue.

activity_code: F31
title: Investigation of Gastrointestinal Interoception in the Maintenance of Disordered Eating Behaviors
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF SOUTH FLORIDA
phr: Heightened gastrointestinal (GI) visceral sensitivity likely maintains restrictive eating disorders, yet this has never been tested. The proposed research tests whether heighted GI visceral sensitivity maintains restrictive eating disorders (i.e., anorexia nervosa [AN], atypical AN, and avoidant restrictive food intake disorder [ARFID]) and examines possible mechanisms underlying altered GI experience in individuals with restrictive eating disorders (i.e., altered GI physiological functioning, GI interoception). Findings have implications for treatment of restrictive eating disorders, such as shifting treatment targets to directly address heightened visceral sensitivity to increase treatment retention and efficacy.

title: Antifibrotic effects of Syndecan-2 and CD148 in Rheumatoid Arthritis Interstitial Lung Disease
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: Project Narrative: Rheumatoid arthritis (RA) is a systemic inflammatory disorder affecting approximately 3 million adults in the United States; interstitial lung disease (ILD), particularly pulmonary fibrosis, is the most common lung complication of RA and is associated with increased mortality. This study aims to define how syndecan-2 (SDC2), an antifibrotic proteoglycan, affects the development of lung fibrosis in the setting of arthritis. The results of this work will provide a better understanding of the molecular mechanisms driving the pathogenesis of RA-associated lung fibrosis and identify potential therapeutic targets to treat patients with this progressive disease.

title: Inhibition of CD33-sialic acid binding in Late-Onset Alzheimer's Disease
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE While AD pathogenesis is still poorly understood, genetic studies have clearly indicated a causal role for microglia, the innate immune cells of the CNS. This project will investigate the effects of competitive inhibition of CD33, a genetically-associated risk factor for Late- Onset Alzheimer’s disease, by sialic acid binding peptides on downstream microglial function in an in vitro model of human AD. This proposal will provide insights to not only the mechanism of action of these peptides, but to also help to clarify the role of CD33 in microglial activation and immune signaling pathways in the progression of Alzheimer’s disease and further development of CD33 targeted therapies.

application_id: 11198992
activity_code: F31
title: The Role of Platelet-Derived Transforming Growth Factor Beta in Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CINCINNATI
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA) is characterized by progressive deterioration of the aortic wall and
catastrophic aortic failure resulting in sudden death in >80% of acute ruptures leading to the death of at least
15,000 Americans every year. Unfortunately, there are no pharmacological treatments available for the
prevention of AAA progression and the only option for these patients is surgical intervention. Our proposal
seeks to evaluate platelets as the source of protective transforming growth factor β (TGFβ) and adventitial
fibroblasts as the target cell type mediating this protection.

application_id: 11235817
activity_code: F32
title: Understanding the link between stressful life events and cardiovascular disease risk: A health neuroscience approach
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: Project Narrative
Stressful life events can increase risk of cardiovascular disease. This study examines neural and behavioral
measures of negative affect and cognitive reappraisal as modifiers of subclinical CVD biomarkers (e.g., carotid
artery intima media thickness, systemic inflammation, metabolic syndrome) following exposure to negative life
events. By identifying biopsychosocial patterns that may buffer or facilitate early CVD pathophysiology, this
study will help clarify individual differences in the relationship between stressful life events and cardiovascular
disease and inform research on CVD prevention.

activity_code: T32
title: Research Training in Global Non-Communicable Diseases
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: Non-communicable diseases account for 71% of all deaths globally, including in the United States, yet training opportunities for research in global cardiovascular, cardiometabolic, pulmonary, and hematologic diseases are limited. This research training program is designed to fill this gap and provide pathways for post-doctoral research fellows to contribute to these critical fields. The program will provide fellows with in-depth research training, professional development, and high-quality mentorship; emphasis will be placed on relevance for non-communicable diseases and health promotion in the United States.

org_name: CINCINNATI CHILDRENS HOSP MED CTR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE This project aims to investigate the relationship between obesity, cardiovascular disease, and lipid metabolism by investigating the role of the novel microprotein Nrac within the cardiovascular system. The primary focus of this research plan is to elucidate the functional role of Nrac and define its involvement in lipid droplet biogenesis and metabolism within cardiomyocytes. This study is essential for understanding the role of lipid abnormalities in obesity and related cardiovascular disease with the potential to reveal new targets for mitigating these diseases.

application_id: 11237088
activity_code: F32
title: Interrogating Dynamic Regulation of Pre-Synaptic Release Probability In Vivo
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative:
 Release probability (P(r)) of neurotransmitter containing vesicles is a critical component of information
transfer between neurons and is dynamically regulated by a variety of molecular and circuit mechanisms. Here
I combine an array of novel imaging, electrophysiological, behavioral, and pharmacological methods to
interrogate the regulation of P(r) in vivo at multiple different scales. This will lead to a novel understanding of
how individual pre-synaptic sites are regulated and bridge a wealth of literature in reduced preparations to the
awake, behaving brain.

title: A novel role for 14-3-3-ζ in regulating islet cell crosstalk
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA AT DAVIS
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The global prevalence of type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM) has now reached epidemic proportions, making it a pressing global health concern. application_id: 11002666
activity_code: K23
title: Discovering immune drivers of fibroblast polarization andrecovery in systemic sclerosis
org_name: HOSPITAL FOR SPECIAL SURGERY
phr: Despite multiple clinical trials of immunosuppressive and anti-fibrotic agents, there are no FDA-approved drugs to treat the severe skin fibrosis caused by systemic sclerosis (SSc). Preliminary studies suggest there is a subset of patients who harbor activated fibroblasts and immune cells in their skin, and those patients may be most likely to benefit from immunosuppression. The overarching study goal is to unravel immune cell-fibroblast interactions in SSc and Very Early Onset of Systemic Sclerosis (VEDOSS) to map disease phases where treatment may be of most benefit, test a histology-derived model for classifying patients most likely to improve, and yield insights into the very earliest phases of SSc.

application_id: 11144313
activity_code: K23
title: Clearing the Fog: Characterization of the Phenotypic and Neurophysiological Features of Cognitive Dysfunction in Fibromyalgia
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: Project Narrative
The proposed project will characterize the phenotypic nature of cognitive dysfunction in fibromyalgia (FM)
using a combination of self-report and objective measures of cognition, complemented by neurophysiological
(EEG) and functional magnetic resonance (fMRI) outcomes. Cognitive symptoms are one of the most
debilitating aspects of FM, are poorly understood and thus poorly managed, and are present in a wide range of
chronic pain conditions beyond FM. Therefore, this project addresses a critical practical challenge that has
applications for the improved healthcare management of a broad range of patients suffering from chronic pain.

activity_code: T32
title: T32 Training Program in Pediatric Immuno-Oncology and Immunotherapy
org_name: ST. JUDE CHILDREN'S RESEARCH HOSPITAL
phr: NARRATIVE
Immunotherapy is a promising treatment for pediatric cancer that can improve outcomes while minimizing toxicity
and the long-term adverse effects associated with other types of therapy. The success of this treatment requires
more discovery-based researchers who aim to improve understanding of how pediatric cancer cells interact with
the immune system, and translational researchers to refine immunotherapeutic approaches for use in early
phase clinical studies. The Pediatric Immuno-Oncology and Immunotherapy training program is designed to
provide integrated training in advanced research approaches in a clinical context to foster the development of
the next generation of independent scientists leading the field.

application_id: 10998152
activity_code: F32
title: Performance Shaping Features of Intermediate Care Nursing Workload
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Intermediate care (i.e., step-down or progressive care) is prevalent in the United States, but the organization and scope of this level of care has changed over time with more severely ill patients needing services previously limited to the intensive care unit being admitted to intermediate care. There is growing concern that nursing workload in some models of intermediate care is excessive, which can lead to staff demoralization, burnout, and poor patient outcomes. In this proposal, we will use a mixed methods approach to (1) identify performance shaping features of intermediate care nursing work and (2) characterize the direction (amplify or decrease) and strength of association with perceived nursing workload.

activity_code: T32
title: CBT@EmTech - CardioVascular Biomechanics Training Program at Emory and GaTech
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative 
This interdisciplinary Program in Cardiovascular Biomechanics at Emory University and Georgia Tech (CBT@EmTech) will train the next generation of biomedical engineers to study how biomechanics affects cardiovascular diseases. Trainees will receive formal coursework, research training, and professional development that will enable them to apply an integration of engineering, vascular biology, data sciences, and biosciences to unsolved problems in cardiovascular medicine. We expect these trainees to be thought leaders in the diagnosis and treatment of cardiovascular disease.

application_id: 11173800
activity_code: K23
title: Early and Personalized Cardiovascular Disease Preventive Care in Lupus Nephritis (EPiC-LN)
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WISCONSIN-MADISON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Patients with lupus nephritis (LN) frequently suffer from life-threatening clinical atherosclerotic cardiovascular
disease (ASCVD) events, that can lead to devastating health consequences, such as stroke, lifelong paralysis,
heart failure, and premature mortality; yet ASCVD preventive strategies are underutilized in LN. Therefore, the
proposed work will develop two ASCVD preventive interventions: 1) an implementation guide to help clinicians
tailor and start ASCVD preventive strategies by individual patient’s risk; 2) a shared decision-making tool to
support patients and clinicians in decisions to start lifesaving ASCVD preventive strategies in LN. Finally, a pilot
test will be done to examine the effectiveness and adoption of these ASCVD interventions during LN visits to...

title: Insulin regulates aging-associated genes through chromatin topology
org_name: JOSLIN DIABETES CENTER

phr: Project Narrative The long-term efficacy of adeno-associated virus (AAV) based therapies have been limited in their utility to treat chronic neurological diseases. title: Molecular Immunology and Tumor Biology
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Cancer remains a major cause of morbidity and mortality in the United States, and new
approaches to the diagnosis and treatment of cancer are critically needed. This T32
program focuses predominantly on the training of physician-scientists who plan research
careers that will improve our understanding of the molecular basis of human cancers.

department and Cancer Center, and thus creates a dynamic platform to educate
physician-scientists and fundamental researchers in the study of cancer biology.

application_id: 11167767
activity_code: F99
title: Metabolic Reprogramming of Myeloid Cells to Enhance Cancer Immunotherapy
org_name: ROSWELL PARK CANCER INSTITUTE CORP
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
A major mechanism of immunotherapeutic resistance is immune suppression and major cell types that drive
immune suppression are myeloid-derived suppressor cells (MDSCs), which compromise anti-cancer immunity.
We identified a targeted metabolic vulnerability in MDSCs rendering them less suppressive, thus, raising tumor
susceptibility to novel immunotherapy regimens. Here, we are investigating strategies to optimize this
therapeutic paradigm in solid cancer models and the underlying immune mechanisms, which will have
important translational implications in cancer patients with high MDSC burden.

title: A Bio-orthogonal Targeting System for Precision Drug Delivery to Vascular Endothelium in Transplanted Organs
org_name: BOSTON UNIVERSITY (CHARLES RIVER CAMPUS)
phr: Project Narrative This proposal describes the training and mentoring of Ms. Aria Pearlman Morales and her research project which develops a novel bio-orthogonal targeting system capable of delivering novel nucleic acid therapies that traffic directly to the organ via systemic dosing. Using this system, she describes a method for preventing systemic immunosuppression and associated drug toxicity, paving a new way for the induction of organ specific immune tolerance. The capability of this system to allow for donor organ specific drug delivery and minimization of off- target toxicity will be evaluated.

application_id: 11238891
activity_code: F32
title: Neurobiology of Paternal Care
org_name: PRINCETON UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Caring fathers can directly support child physical health and behavioral development while abusive and
neglectful fathers can prove harmful. It remains largely unknown at the neural level why some adult males
demonstrate care while others demonstrate abuse or neglect, and there is little information on how these
variable paternal phenotypes may be shaped by social and environmental experience in earlier development.
The proposed research will use a biparental rodent model species to elucidate the neural activity and
molecular mechanisms associated with natural variation in paternal care.

application_id: 11239749
activity_code: F32
title: Investigating a RORgt-expressing antigen presenting cell required for peripheral Treg responses to gut microbiota
org_name: NEW YORK UNIVERSITY SCHOOL OF MEDICINE
phr: Project Narrative:
This research aims to study how the body develops tolerance towards the expansive host of microbial species
residing within our gut. By determining the profile and function of a newly discovered type of white blood cell
observed to dampen self-harming immune responses to microbes in our intestines, targeted therapeutic
strategies can be developed to prevent or reduce imbalanced inflammatory responses in the intestinal
environment. Ultimately, results from our work to define the essential cells involved in this process will expand
our understanding of the relationship between host and its microbiota, while spurring new approaches to
preventing and treating auto-inflammatory diseases such as inflammatory bowel disease (IBD).

activity_code: F99
title: Metabolic Reprogramming of Myeloid Cells to Enhance Cancer Immunotherapy
org_name: ROSWELL PARK CANCER INSTITUTE CORP
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE A major mechanism of immunotherapeutic resistance is immune suppression and major cell types that drive immune suppression are myeloid-derived suppressor cells (MDSCs), which compromise anti-cancer immunity. We identified a targeted metabolic vulnerability in MDSCs rendering them less suppressive, thus, raising tumor susceptibility to novel immunotherapy regimens. Here, we are investigating strategies to optimize this therapeutic paradigm in solid cancer models and the underlying immune mechanisms, which will have important translational implications in cancer patients with high MDSC burden.

application_id: 10669586
activity_code: F30
title: Investigation of neural ensembles underlying chronic pain
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA-IRVINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Opioid overdose has claimed the lives of over 450,000 adults in the United States since the turn of the century, an epidemic fueled in large part by physicians’ reliance upon opioids for chronic pain management. Successful mitigation of the opioid crisis is therefore dependent upon the identification of novel, non-opioid analgesics. By identifying key neural circuits involved in pain chronification, this project will pave the way for targeted, circuit- based therapies that effectively mitigate pain without the deleterious effects of long-term opioid use.

application_id: 10997934
activity_code: F31
title: Using a novel model system to explore the causes and consequences of altered sleep need during pregnancy
org_name: HARVARD UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Pregnancy is a particularly vulnerable state in which inadequate sleep is linked to negative health outcomes, and this might explain why women are reported to sleep more during their first trimester of pregnancy. I developed a novel paradigm in Drosophila melanogaster to model increased sleep drive during gravidity with the goal of understanding its causes and consequences. Further, studying the mechanisms that drive this form of increased sleep need will provide a potential avenue for studying how sleep need is measured, regulated, and fulfilled in general, as is my long term goal as a career scientist.

application_id: 11174461
activity_code: F99
title: PD-1 blockade effects on bone strength and metastatic progression
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs) are less effective at reducing tumor burden in bone metastatic patients and
coincide with musculoskeletal toxicities including bone loss and fractures. Interactions between the tumor, bone,
and immune microenvironments that might be contributing to this clinical observation are not well understood.
This proposal investigates the novel concept that ICIs suppress bone formation and enhance bone resorption,
increasing patient fracture risk, and therefore have the potential to transform clinical care and outcomes for
patients receiving ICIs.

title: Wnt-driven adhesion and extracellular matrix dynamics in breast cancer metastasis
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: Project Narrative Triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC) is an aggressive breast cancer subtype that disproportionally affects Hispanic and African-American women. We hypothesize that changes in Wnt signaling drive the rewiring of the extracellular matrix (ECM), facilitating micrometastases and macrometastases in the lungs. Understanding how Wnt signaling plasticity facilitates metastatic progression will provide insights into how Wnt-driven ECM alterations confer colonization competence to metastatic TNBC cells and contribute to identifying mechanisms for perturbing these changes.

application_id: 11142989
activity_code: T32
title: Multidisciplinary Training Program in Pediatric Lung Diseases
org_name: CHILDREN'S HOSP OF PHILADELPHIA
phr: Narrative
Disorders and diseases of the lung account for much of the morbidity and mortality that occurs during
childhood, and the onset of lung disease in childhood is a strong predictor for respiratory disease in later life.
The prevention and/or treatment of childhood respiratory diseases also has the potential to significantly reduce
disease and medical costs associated with disease throughout the lifespan. Recognizing the paucity of
researchers and physician scientists who focus on basic, clinical and translational research centered on
pediatric lung diseases, the proposed training program will address the substantial need for academic pediatric
pulmonologists and pulmonary investigators who will establish research programs and train leaders of
tomorrow.

title: Acute Kidney Injury with Immune Checkpoint Inhibitors and Beta-Lactam Antibiotics
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICI) are a revolutionary highly effective anti-cancer treatment and are associated with immune mediated acute kidney injury (AKI) through unclear mechanisms. ICI mediated immune system activation may lead to excessive immune responses to medications such as beta-lactam antibiotics, which themselves cause AKI via acute interstitial nephritis. This study aims to estimate the comparative risk and treatment effect heterogeneity of AKI in patients on ICIs concomitantly exposed to beta-lactam versus fluroquinolone antibiotics, which are widely used alternatives to beta-lactams.

title: Neural dynamics of freely-moving, naturalistic behavior
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative  Movement is essential to our everyday lives, giving us the freedom to interact with a rich, complex world around us. This proposal seeks to understand how motor cortical dynamics generalize from supporting artificially constrained behavior to freely-moving, naturalistic behavior, allowing for more real-world applications to be trans- lated from the basic scientific findings in the motor systems field. This work has applications for understanding how the cortical control of natural, everyday motor functions can be disrupted in people with stroke or traumatic brain injury and can help optimize the decoding of naturalistic movements for assistive neurotechnology, such as neural prosthetics, for those with motor disabilities to restore the freedom of movement.

title: Multidimensional Features of Socioeconomic Status, Brain Age, and the Potential Mediating Role of Cardiometabolic Health Among Mid and Late Life Adults
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: Project Narrative Lower socioeconomic status (SES) has been linked to reduced cardiometabolic health, which may explain the why inequalities in SES may confer accelerated brain aging - a highly predictive biomarker of risk for Alzheimer's disease and related dementias. However, the extent to which SES relates to brain age, and whether cardiometabolic health accounts for the relationship between SES and brain age has yet to be conclusively determined. This proposal seeks to elucidate the connections between SES, brain age, and cardiometabolic health among midlife adults while also investigating whether these associations extend to older adults.

activity_code: T32
title: Cardiology and Pulmonary Clinical Research Training Program
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
There is a national shortage of qualified clinician-scientists able to conduct the rigorous clinical research needed to address cardiovascular and pulmonary diseases. This training program proposes to continue to address this shortage through the efforts of a distinguished, experienced, and committed training program faculty. Training will continue to be provided to those who desire careers as clinician-scientists focusing on cardiology and pulmonary clinical research through a program that provides trainees with the skills needed to design, conduct, and complete epidemiologic studies that will address the most vexing population-based questions in cardiovascular and pulmonary medicine.

activity_code: F99
title: Synapse to behavior, interrogating pathways underlying the control of food intake
org_name: WASHINGTON STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Ingestion of a meal can elicit both positive (rewarding) and negative (aversion/nausea) feelings, and maladaptive
changes in these feelings could have broad implications, not only for the overconsumption of food that can lead
to obesity, but also for emotional disorders. It is therefore critical to understand the neuronal circuitry underlying
the positive and negative valences associated with food intake. The goal of the F99 portion of this proposal is to
investigate the cellular mechanisms underlying divergent activation of hindbrain neurons by positively and
negatively associated signals, while the goal of the K00 portion is to learn manipulation and recording methods
to interrogate neuronal pathways in-vivo.

title: In vitro Reconstitution of the C. elegans Synaptonemal Complex
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Sexually reproducing organisms rely on meiosis, a specialized cell division that generates haploid gametes to ensure the correct inheritance of genomes from parent to offspring. Defects in this process result in gametes with an incorrect number of chromosomes or aneuploidy, which is the primary cause of age-related infertility, miscarriages, and genetic disorders, such as Down Syndrome. My proposed research will elucidate regulatory mechanisms that ensure the faithful transmission of genetic information and will broadly impact the field of reproductive biology and the design of therapeutic interventions.

title: Astrocyte experience-dependent regulation of synapse stability in the adult mouse visual cortex

phr: Project Narrative Elucidating the dynamic relationship between increased experience-dependent neuronal activity and astrocyte transcriptional responses and their subsequent regulation of synaptic plasticity will offer a greater understanding of brain homeostasis. application_id: 10902811
activity_code: F31
title: Characterizing Ahcy as a redox regulated enzyme in the Drosophila eye under oxidative stress
org_name: PURDUE UNIVERSITY
phr: Narrative Oxidative stress contributes to the development and progression of ocular diseases including cataracts, glaucoma, age-related macular degeneration, and diabetic retinopathy. One-carbon metabolism influences the response to oxidative stress via antioxidant biosynthesis; however, this pathway is also perturbed by oxidative stress in the eye. In these studies, I propose that oxidative stress leads to the oxidation of Adenosylhomocysteinase (Ahcy) inhibiting its activity, resulting in increased SAH and decreased methylation capacity.

application_id: 11383350
activity_code: F30
title: Place and Time Processing of Pitch in the Context of Cochlear Dysfunction
org_name: INDIANA UNIVERSITY INDIANAPOLIS
phr: Project Narrative Our perception of pitch is not only important for listening to and creating music, but also for communication and processing the complex sounds that surround us. However, our neurophysiological understanding of pitch processing and how it is degraded with hearing impairment is limited. The proposed study utilizes a cross-species approach to characterize deficits in pitch coding and perception that occur due to various forms of cochlear damage, which will advance our understanding of the physiological bases for individual differences in the perception of every-day sounds with sensorineural hearing impairment.

application_id: 11192919
activity_code: F99
title: Mapping neural targets and advancing neuromodulation techniques for disorders of consciousness
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Utilizing advanced neuroimaging and machine learning to investigate subcortical arousal in auditory
perception holds promise for improved detection of perceptual awareness, laying the groundwork for
potential therapeutic interventions in severe brain disorders and anesthesia, when explicit reporting is
challenging. Exploring non- invasive neuromodulation strategies for disorders of consciousness contributes
to the development of novel therapeutic approaches, holding the potential to enhance health by offering a
widely applicable intervention for patients with limited options for behavioral recovery following severe brain
injuries.

application_id: 11001630
activity_code: F99
title: Investigating novel gene regulatory networks for development of myeloid cells
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF NOTRE DAME
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Constructing the brain requires the coordination of several different cell types that permanently reside within the central nervous system. One of these cell types, microglia, is the resident immune cell of the CNS and must be produced during embryogenesis to maintain brain homeostasis throughout life. We are studying gene regulatory networks governing microglia production during embryogenesis to help identify novel genes that can expand the knowledge of genetic risk factors and therapeutic targets for diseases where microglia are the cause.

application_id: 11195647
activity_code: F99
title: Neurofibromin 1 as a novel regulator of lipid metabolism
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
High grade gliomas are aggressive brain tumors that occur in children and, with limited therapeutic options, are
highly lethal. We have discovered a novel function for a protein commonly mutated in pediatric high grade
gliomas, which leads to distinct changes in metabolism of the neural progenitor and tumor cells. Our proposed
study seeks to understand how this protein regulates lipid metabolism specifically, and whether this distinct
change can be targeted to improve therapeutic intervention.

application_id: 11072367
activity_code: F99
title: Effects of Internal State on Social and Non-Social Decision Making in the Non-Human Primate Prefrontal Cortex
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ARIZONA
phr: Project Narrative: Various physiological and psychological variables influence our decision making, but little is known about how the brain represents factors like heart rate or emotional state, or the neural mechanisms by which these factors bias the decision-making process. Here I propose to evaluate these mechanisms by recording from the non- human primate prefrontal cortex while they perform social and non-social decision-making tasks and either visceral or brain physiology is manipulated. The research described herein will provide a strong backbone upon which I will receive training in experimental and computational neurophysiology, as well as in the use of causal genetic manipulations in non-human primates.

application_id: 11096070
activity_code: K08
title: Induction of Broad Sarbecovirus Immunity Via A Vaccine Engineered From SARS-CoV-2 Heptad Repeat 1
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
COVID-19 vaccines are unable to prevent widespread infection with new viral variants, as even the updated
vaccine tends to boost pre-existing antibodies to old strains instead of causing new antibodies to form against
current strains. We designed a vaccine that causes antibodies to form to only one area on the virus: a weak
spot that is identical in all strains. We will test and optimize this vaccine in mice, with the ultimate goal of using
it in humans as a more effective booster vaccine against all viral strains.

application_id: 11192941
activity_code: F99
title: Persistent Impacts of Adolescent THC on Microglial Function and Opioid Addiction Risk
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA-IRVINE
phr: Project Narrative
Early cannabis use is associated with later use of harder drugs like opioids in humans, and rodent studies have
suggested that THC may cause changes in opioid addiction-relevant brain circuits that could underlie this
association. Microglia, the brains’ immune cells, contribute significantly to brain development by influencing
neural circuit remodeling and rewiring, and they respond to developmental insults like THC as well as to
adulthood opioid drugs. Here we study the causal roles of microglia in long-lasting pro-opioid effects, help
uncover novel microglial biology with relevance to addiction, and attain training in RNA sequencing and
bioinformatics, writing, and career development positioning me well for a career in addiction neuroimmunology.

application_id: 10782009
activity_code: K00
title: Cis regulatory control of avian bill morphogenesis
org_name: PRINCETON UNIVERSITY
phr: The neural crest gives rise to most of the skeletal elements of the face and is implicated in many congenital birth defects. Expanding our understanding of molecular programs controlling neural crest formation will provide insight into the genetic basis of craniofacial disease. Novel mechanisms uncovered in this work may have important implications for the diagnosis and treatment of craniofacial defects.

title: Cellular and molecular changes in the spinal cord that cause motor deficits in old age
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: The ability to carry out voluntary motor commands diminishes with age, reducing the quality of life and lifespan of individuals. Understanding the changes α-motor neurons undergo during aging is crucial for the future development of treatments that preserve motor function into old age.

application_id: 10754846
activity_code: F31
title: Characterization of Dopamine Neuron Axonal Mitochondria Specialization and its Relevance to Parkinson’s Disease
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY
phr: Project Narrative  Parkinson’s disease is an irreversible neurodegenerative disease that is among the fastest growing neurological diseases in prevalence, associated disability, and death. Because cell death is irreversible, identifying early disease-events is critical for developing treatments that act before the brain is damaged beyond repair. The loss of axons of dopamine neurons in the Substantia Nigra pars compacta is one such early event, and the goals of this project are to better understand how subcellular molecular and functional specialization of mitochondria contribute to axon survival, and whether these processes are altered in mouse models of Parkinson’s disease.

phr: Project Narrative
This project will study two long non-coding RNAs (lncRNAs), that affect cellular function directly as RNAs, without

a tumor suppressor DRAIC. In the F99 phase, I will focus on structure-function studies of the lncRNAs that will
identify the minimal regions of these RNAs important for their action and thus identify how these lncRNAs
regulate glioma progression, while in parallel familiarizing myself with animal models and histopathology of
gliomas. In the K00 phase, I will focus on two strategies for therapy of gliomas based on the knowledge obtained
during the F99: methods of targeting/delivering lncRNAs in gliomas and screening for small chemicals that will

activity_code: DP2
title: Brewing anti-toxin drugs using probiotic yeast
org_name: NORTH CAROLINA STATE UNIVERSITY RALEIGH
phr: Narrative
We propose to identify drugs that “disarm” pathogenic bacteria by neutralizing their toxins using a new, rapid
approach that harnesses baker’s yeast. These drugs will be more specific to pathogens than normal antibiotics
and can also be used to treat antibiotic-resistant infections. To inexpensively deliver these drugs to the site of
disease, we will engineer probiotic yeast to produce them directly in the gut.

title: Decoding the language of inflammation between central nervous system resident immune cells
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA BERKELEY
phr: NARRATIVE Central nervous system (CNS)-resident astrocytes and microglia interact to regulate pro-inflammatory programs that drive neurodegenerative diseases, but the pathways and molecules involved are largely unknown. application_id: 10702064
activity_code: K00
title: Sleep Based Memory Consolidation in Juvenile Zebra Finch
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: Project Narrative Although the role of sleep has been studied in many different contexts, little effort has been dedicated to understanding the role of sleep in the acquisition of communicativebehaviors. With a well-defined neural circuitry underlying a robust and stereotyped learned behavior, along with evidence pointing to changes in song structure during sleep, the songbird is exceptionally suited for examining sleep-based learning mechanisms. Byidentifying circuits mechanisms involved in memory consolidation we will stand better equipped to help those affected by sleep disturbances.

org_name: JACKSON LABORATORY
phr: Project Narrative Chronic kidney disease (CKD), which affects 37 million Americans, progresses with the loss of important proteins such as Klotho, leading to severe endocrine disease, as well as increased renal oxidative stress that further damages the kidneys. application_id: 10860020
activity_code: DP5
title: Harnessing Network Science to Personalize Scalable Interventions for Adolescent Depression
org_name: NORTHWESTERN UNIVERSITY AT CHICAGO
phr: Project Narrative
The goal of this project is to integrate advances in network analysis and intervention science—specifically,
research on single-session interventions (SSIs)—to identify potent, accessible strategies for reducing
depression in adolescents. The first goal is to establish guidelines for characterizing adolescents' depression
symptom structures, using experience sampling method data to compare two approaches for computing
personalized symptom networks; the second goal is to test whether parameters from these personalized
symptom networks predict adolescents' clinical response to evidence-based SSIs targeting distinct features of
depression. Results may identify a novel, powerful approach to matching adolescents to targeted SSIs based
on personalized clinical need.

title: SC Biomedical Informatics & Data Science for Health Impact (SC BIDS4Health)
org_name: MEDICAL UNIVERSITY OF SOUTH CAROLINA
phr: The South Carolina Biomedical Informatics & Data Science for Health Impact (SC BIDS4Health) training program combines a rigorous curriculum in biomedical informatics, data science, population, health systems and policy, and domain biology based on the existing joint PhD degree in Biomedical Data Science and Informatics with coursework and practical experiences in understanding factors that affect health outcomes and improving career readiness skills. The training program includes predoctoral and postdoctoral components and faculty mentors are nationally and internationally recognized leaders in these fields.

application_id: 10904169
activity_code: R36
title: Functional and molecular organization of lateral septum in threat processing
org_name: HARVARD MEDICAL SCHOOL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE To avoid potential harm, we are equipped with brain circuits that allow detecting, learning, and responding to perceived threats in our environment. Dysregulation in these processes often denotes psychopathological conditions such as post-traumatic stress and anxiety disorders. This study examines how specific neuronal populations process perceived threats to guide behavioral defenses, thus providing novel insights into the neural mechanisms underlying threat processing in both health and disease.

application_id: 11146501
activity_code: DP1
title: Intracellular Electrophysiology: An electrochemical atlas of organelles
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CHICAGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Increasingly, organelles are been found to signal to each other via intimate contacts. This new mode of signaling,
whereby information is transferred without the organelles fusing, has wide-ranging impacts on health and disease.
I propose to create an electrochemical atlas of organelles, apply it to study aberrant inter-organelle contacts in
neurodegenerative disease and thereby identify common pathways one can target to restore contacts.

application_id: 11171841
activity_code: DP2
title: New classes of small, cell permeable genome regulators to expand the age of genomic medicine
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: Project Narrative
Genetic disorders caused by repetitive DNA expansions, such as Friedreich ataxia, remain challenging to
treat due to limitations in current genome-editing technologies. The proposed research aims to develop a
novel class of small, cell-permeable genome editors based on pyrrole-imidazole polyamides that can be
rationally designed to target and edit specific genomic regions. This innovative approach has the potential to
overcome delivery and immunogenicity challenges associated with protein-based editors, potentially
leading to more effective treatments for a wide range of genetic disorders and expanding the toolkit for
precision medicine.

title: Innovations in Personalizing Treatment for Eating Disorders Using Idiographic Methods and the Impact of Personalization on Psychological, Physical, and Sociodemographic Outcomes
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF LOUISVILLE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
This project will build a new precision treatment for eating disorders and identify how individual
treatment mechanisms relate to public health outcomes, individual disease burden, medical
comorbidities, and social determinants of health. The proposed research has significant public
health relevance, as it will directly lead to a highly accessible and deployable personalized
treatment that can improve treatment response rates and be applied to all psychiatric illnesses.
The proposed research is relevant to NIH’s strategic aim 3.3b, as it will lead to the creation of a
personalized treatment consistent with NIH’s precision treatment initiative.

application_id: 10686547
activity_code: DP2
title: Mapping and Therapeutic Hijacking of Lysosomal Transport
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Macromolecular trafficking between the cell surface and lysosomes is an essential cellular process with proven
therapeutic promise, yet the identities and functions of proteins which transit between these two spaces have
only been profiled on a limited basis. Here, we develop an innovative, tissue-specific approach to discover the
identities of proteins which traffic to lysosomes in vivo, characterize these proteins’ trafficking ability, and to
harness this lysosomal transport for targeted protein degradation of extracellular proteins and therapeutic
delivery. Our work will directly provide translational avenues for context-specific drug delivery and establish an
approach to remove deleterious proteins from the extracellular space.

application_id: 11095817
activity_code: DP1
title: Ending HIV: Bringing Integrated Prevention and Treatment Services to People Who Use Drugs Where They Live
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative:
Through this Avant-Garde Award, I plan to fill in the gaps of the United States Ending the HIV Plan for persons
who use drugs (PWUD). My pioneer award vision is to develop a mobile hub and spoke model that has mobile
pharmacies and clinics on wheels that deploy a rapid response sustainable workforce of trained community
health workers and pharmacists that literally meet the community where they live. They will provide rapid HIV
testing, opioid use disorder (OUD) diagnosis, same day deployment of medication treatment for OUD, PrEP
and ART, and retain them on treatment where they live to advance the goal of ending HIV in PWUD.

application_id: 11194280
activity_code: DP1
title: Neural control of rhythmic orofacial movements
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: Research Narrative
Disorganization of orofacial rhythmic movements leads to severe behavioral consequences. For example,
discoordination of swallowing and breathing produces choking, which is the fourth leading cause of unintentional
death among preschool children and elderly and the leading cause of death among infants. This project aims to
shed light on why these life-threatening symptoms occur in many forms neurological malfunctions by mapping
and dissecting the brainstem circuits that control orofacial movements.

application_id: 11136419
activity_code: DP1
title: Defining the viral PTMome: Towards the development of novel antiviral approaches
org_name: CLEVELAND CLINIC LERNER COM-CWRU
phr: NARRATIVE
This proposal will identify and characterize the posttranslational modifications (PTMs) of the
proteome of emerging viruses (the `viral PTMome') as well as the modifying enzymes that control
viral PTMs and thereby virus replication and pathogenesis. We will use an integrative approach
that combines proteomics and gene-targeting screens with molecular, biochemical, and virus
infection studies to address fundamental unanswered questions in virology, which will spur
entirely new lines of treatment for a range of infectious diseases that represent public health
threats.

title: Peripheral nervous system aging
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: Narrative
We are interested in the peripheral nervous system, which connects the brain to all organs, and
how it could set the tempo of aging. Understanding the role of peripheral organ innervation during
aging is important because it could lead to entirely new strategies to counter aging and diseases.
Solutions to this question could also be broadly applied to other biological phenomena where time
and organ coordination are critical.

application_id: 11134804
activity_code: DP2
title: Hijacking the T cell machinery for logic-gated CAR T cell control
org_name: DANA-FARBER CANCER INST
phr: Project Narrative
Solid tumors are responsible for the majority of cancer deaths in the United States. Researchers have been
unable to widely employ CAR T cells, an effective form of cancer immunotherapy, to solid tumors due to a lack
of tumor specific antigen targets and the risk of toxicity when target antigens are shared with normal, vital
tissues. This project will optimize innovative and completely novel receptors capable of logic-gated control of
CAR T cells, greatly enhancing their safety and tumor specificity, opening the door to effective therapy of solid
tumors.

title: A pro-metastatic secretory program activated by epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition

phr: Project Narrative  There are few effective therapeutic options for metastatic lung adenocarcinomas (LUADs) that lack actionable  mutations  in  receptor  tyrosine  kinases,  and  there  is  an  urgent  need  to  develop  a  better  understanding  of  the  biological  basis  of  LUAD  growth  and  metastasis.  On  the  basis  of  the  preliminary  results  presented  here,  I  postulate  that  LUAD  growth  and  metastasis  is  driven  by  a  secretory  program  activated  by  epithelial- to-  mesenchymal transition (EMT). If proven true, these findings would show that EMT promotes metastasis through  a cell non- autonomous mechanism, which would advance our understanding of the molecular underpinnings of  LUAD  progression  and  would  provide  a  rationale  to  identify  actionable  targe...

application_id: 11014624
activity_code: DP2
title: Tracking single-cell gene expression heterogeneity and its consequences in bacterial biofilms
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative:
Bacterial biofilms are the predominant lifestyle of bacteria in nature and they can also cause
chronic infections that are difficulty to treat. This study aims to track the gene expression profile
in each biofilm cell and to dissect the intricate interplay between single-cell gene expression,
individual cell behavior, and local cell organization that underlies the developmental program of
bacterial biofilms. Such an understanding is crucial for combatting biofilm-related problems and
harnessing beneficial biofilms.

application_id: 11082175
activity_code: DP2
title: Methods to Rapidly Explore Combinatorial Diversity and their Application to CRISPR-Cas9 Systems
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Here we propose to develop a method for rapidly permuting through combinatorial protein space
at rates two orders of magnitude greater than what is currently available. Using our approach, we
will generate a set of advanced CRISPR-Cas9 transcriptional regulators, and apply these tools to
perform a series of in vivo screens within an animal model of neurodegeneration. The outlined
work will be impactful in several ways as we will establish a new paradigm by which designer
proteins are developed, create a series of highly-potent CRISPR tools with which users can probe
gene function, and provide a general framework for performing precise genetic screens within
complex tissues.

application_id: 11111632
activity_code: DP2
title: High-throughput identification of antibody features for sequence-based epitope prediction
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ILLINOIS AT URBANA-CHAMPAIGN
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Human antibodies are highly diverse and can recognize a wide range of epitopes to confer protection against
different pathogens. The epitope specificity of an antibody is determined by its structure, which is in turn
determined by its primary amino acid sequence. This proposal aims to develop a novel experimental platform
to characterize antibody-antigen interactions in a high-throughput manner and identify molecular features that
can help predict the epitope of an antibody based on its sequence.

application_id: 11014654
activity_code: DP2
title: Harnessing the chromatin conformational code for epigenetic regulation
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: Project narrative
The globular core of a nucleosome, the fundamental regulatory unit of chromatin, is considered to be rigid and
inert. In contrast to this textbook view, we recently discovered that the nucleosome core is malleable and that
this structural flexibility regulates genome architecture and function. The proposed high-risk but high-reward
research program will characterize the structural and functional consequences of the nucleosome conformational
code by developing novel groundbreaking tools to interrogate and manipulate chromatin, impacting both
fundamental and translational science.

title: Synthetic metabolism to armor and enhance a new class of cell therapies
org_name: GEORGIA INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY
phr: Project Narrative
Cancer cells alter their metabolism to be able to sustain enhanced growth rates and to inhibit the immune
system’s ability to respond effectively against them. This project will establish unique therapeutic approaches to
allow immune cells to resist, respond to, and even remediate altered metabolic environments to improve their
ability to fight back against cancers. Our efforts will be directly translatable to the clinic by developing ‘metabolic
armor’ for current CAR-T therapies.

application_id: 11011380
activity_code: DP2
title: Transdiagnostic Intervention to Reduce Internalized Health-Related Stigma
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF FLORIDA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Social stigma due to health conditions increases disease burden and adversely impacts health. The
internalization of health-related stigma (i.e., self-stigma) is associated with impaired mental health and quality
of life, yet little is known about how to reduce internalized stigma. The current project will test the effects of a
novel, transdiagnostic, group counseling intervention, and of general peer support, to determine the optimal
method for helping patients cope with health-related stigma, reducing its internalization, and, ultimately,
enhancing patient quality of life.

application_id: 10883671
activity_code: DP1
title: Mitochondrial metabolite compartmentalization in health and disease
org_name: BETH ISRAEL DEACONESS MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Dysregulated metabolite delivery to the mitochondria lies at the heart of metabolic disorders; however, the vast
majority of mitochondrial transporters remain uncharacterized. By employing our newly developed system
using brown fat, this proposal aims to generate a complete functional map of mitochondrial metabolite
transporters in mammals, i.e., identification of specific substrates and biological/pathological roles of orphan
mitochondrial transporters. Successful completion of the project will establish a conceptual framework to
understand the molecular regulation of mitochondrial metabolite utilization, and also provide a new roadmap
for reversing disease phenotypes that stem from dysregulation in such processes.

application_id: 11468165
activity_code: DP5
title: Leveraging Adaptive Evolution and High-Throughput Techniques to Dissect the Link Between Biochemical Function and Fitness
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA BERKELEY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Enzymes are central to biology and a physical understanding of enzyme function in vitro and in vivo has far-
reaching implications in medicine and industry. Here we will leverage adaptive evolution and high-throughput
technologies to build the first map between enzyme sequence, enzyme function and organism fitness. This
sequence-function-fitness map will allow us to address many important questions in biochemistry and evolution
and will usher in a new era of integrative biochemical evolution studies.

The COVID-19 pandemic has demonstrated the immense risk posed by the emergence of novel viral strains.

application_id: 10937001
activity_code: DP1
title: Elucidating chronic liver disease pathways using somatic genetics
org_name: UT SOUTHWESTERN MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE There are no high throughput genetic methods to uncover mechanisms that drive chronic liver disease's progression to cirrhosis, limiting our ability to develop new therapies. Recently, we showed that some somatic mutations within diseased livers can exert adaptive effects that might benefit tissue health. Using new technologies developed in my lab, we propose to exploit somatic mosaicism as a genetic strategy to 1) identify adaptive genetic pathways and 2) understand how mutant clones expand within chronically damaged tissues, and potentially protect from disease.

title: Iowa CBDRP Comp A: BD-STEPS Core
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF IOWA

application_id: 11169827
activity_code: DP2
title: Host-Dependent Mechanisms that Guide the Longitudinal Dynamic of Sites of SIV Integration
org_name: BETH ISRAEL DEACONESS MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Project Narrative
The integration of SIV into the host genome poses a significant barrier to achieving a successful
HIV cure. This project seeks to elucidate the host-dependent mechanisms that govern
preferences in HIV/SIV integration and the pivotal role SIV integration plays in disrupting host
cellular functions; the latter ultimately leads to immune senescence, premature aging of the
immune system, and, in some cases, tumorigenesis. By addressing these crucial aspects, my
project aims to offer mechanistic insights that can lead to potential therapeutic targets for
preventing integration or reversing host-immune dysfunctions triggered by SIV integration.

application_id: 10745713
activity_code: K08
title: Epigenetic Regulation of Regulatory B Cell Cytokine Expression and Allograft Rejection
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: NARRATIVE We have discovered that the expression of cytokines by B cells prior to kidney transplantation can identify patients who are at increased risk for rejection and loss of their transplants. B cell cytokines remain stable and the immunological risk associated with this stability appears pre-determined in some patients, while in others the cytokine expression changes after transplantation along with the patient’s risk status. The proposed study will identify the genetic regulatory mechanisms that maintain stability vs. plasticity in the B cells, allowing us to accurately determine a patient’s risk for rejection prior to transplantation, and individualize immunosuppression, while providing novel insights into the mechanisms that regulate immunological reactivity.

application_id: 10907021
activity_code: K99
title: Mechanobiology of fracture healing during skeletal disuse
org_name: VIRGINIA COMMONWEALTH UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Extended periods of musculoskeletal disuse and unloading, such as found during paralysis, bedrest, and or spaceflight, increase the risk of bone and muscle weakness and fragility fracture. Treatment options for patients with fractures during musculoskeletal disuse are limited because very little is known about how mechanical loads (or lack thereof) are sensed by different bone cell populations to regulate healing. This project seeks to use a controllable muscle and bone loading platform to identify what mechanical interventions and molecular mechanisms are necessary to improve fracture healing and muscle strength during extended disuse.

title: Protein engineering for the development of novel antimicrobial agents
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Our ability to effectively treat infections is threatened by increasing resistance among many microbes to the antibiotics that we use today. One approach to the development of new antibiotics is to engineer antimicrobial peptides (AMPs), miniproteins naturally produced by the human body to defend against microbes, which may treat infections both by killing microbes and by promoting an appropriate immune response. This proposal uses protein engineering and related technologies to systematically characterize and engineer human AMPs for the development of new types of antibiotics.

title: Dissecting the molecular regulation of T cell localization and function within the Mycobacterium tuberculosis granuloma
org_name: SEATTLE CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Tuberculosis causes 1.4 million deaths annually despite the availability of antibiotics and vaccination,
underscoring the urgent need to improve on existing therapies. These studies seek to characterize how
Mycobacterium tuberculosis affects the trafficking and function of T cells, which are crucial for immunity. This
knowledge could be used for the development of new drugs and vaccines.

activity_code: K08
title: Geographic origins and dispersal of drug-resistant M. tuberculosis in South Africa: Advancing strategies for early detection
org_name: BOSTON MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Drug-resistant tuberculosis (TB) remains an important threat to global public health. Early identification, at the
critical juncture between outbreak and epidemic, can prevent disease and deaths due to drug-resistant TB
infections. In this project, we use genetic data from TB infections and data on human mobility to advance new
strategies for early detection and containment of emerging strains of drug-resistant TB.

application_id: 11091641
activity_code: K08
title: Autoimmunity-Associated B Cells in Lupus Nephritis
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ROCHESTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
This study determines how cytokines like interferon influence the development of autoimmunity-associated B
cells and autoreactive plasma cells in systemic lupus erythematosus. Kidney tissue is examined to determine
if autoimmunity-associated B cell precursors and autoimmunity-promoting factors are found in lupus nephritis.
Understanding how these abnormal B cells develop into cells that make self-reactive antibodies may lead to
new treatment strategies for lupus and lupus nephritis.

application_id: 11118921
activity_code: DP1
title: IDENTIFYING EPIGENOMICS AND CONNECTOMICS OF PROTRACTED OPIOID WITHDRAWAL AND RELAPSE USING CELLULAR BARCODING
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: NARRATIVE
Opioid addiction is a major public health crisis in the United States, and treatment of opioid addiction is
hindered by high rates of relapse after abstinence. We here study the changes to medial orbitofrontal cortex in
mice and rats in response to oxycodone withdrawal and relapse by integrating connectivity, gene expression,
and genome accessibility measurements at the level of single cells and identify conserved core changes by
comparing across species and behaviors. Our work contributes to the basic understanding of the connectomic
and epigenomic changes in opioid addiction and aims to identify conserved changes for treatment.

application_id: 10883577
activity_code: K08
title: Immunometabolic signatures of BCG-induced neonatal trained immunity
org_name: BETH ISRAEL DEACONESS MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Project Narrative Infectious diseases are leading causes of death in newborns who have distinct immunity and metabolic needs. Vaccines are the most effective biomedical intervention to prevent infection and may confer off-target health benefits, but the mechanisms by which they do so are incompletely characterized in early life. The proposed research will explore the effects of Bacille Calmette-Guérin (BCG) vaccine, given yearly to ~150 million newborns across the globe, on neonatal immunity and cellular metabolism to identify molecular signatures of broad vaccine protection against infection that can be harnessed to optimize current and future vaccines tailored to the very young.

application_id: 11257364
activity_code: K08
title: Defining Molecular Epitopes of Protective Antibodies to Flaviviruses
org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: Project Narrative
This application proposes to isolate neutralizing monoclonal antibodies from the plasma of Zika virus recovered
individuals that have been generated from long-lived bone marrow plasma cells, and for the first time, compare
the lineages and viral targets of B cell generated antibodies from both circulating and marrow compartments. In
addition to this novel work with Zika virus, this proposal will identify broadly neutralizing dengue virus antibodies
from the plasma of individuals infected with two different dengue viruses which will inform vaccine design and
have the potential to be developed as pan-dengue therapeutics. This award is thus primed to identify a potential
paradigm shift in our understanding of the mechanisms by which human B cells develop, mature and respond ...

application_id: 10848480
activity_code: K08
title: Characterization of seasonal CoV immunity and operationalization of a novel controlled human infection model for the betacoronavirus OC43
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MARYLAND BALTIMORE
phr: Narrative For both SARS-CoV-2 and influenza the difficulty of inducing long-lasting immunity that can protect against evolving viruses continues to complicate vaccine design. My proposed research will provide a functional assay to evaluate seasonal CoV immunity, characterize of the diversity of influenza mutations after a controlled human infection, and develop a protocol for a seasonal CoV controlled human infection model. These tools are necessary to advance the development of a controlled human infection model with the seasonal CoV OC43, which will provide an efficient mechanism to evaluate broadly protective CoV countermeasures.

application_id: 10397010
activity_code: T32
title: Training in Complex Systems and Data Science Approaches Applied to the Neurobiology of Drug Use
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF VERMONT & ST AGRIC COLLEGE
phr: The overarching aim of this training program is to produce substance abuse researchers well versed in the
theory and application of state-of-the-art analytic tools for complex systems in the context of cognitive
neuroscience broadly defined. The research focus is on characterizing the neural substrates of addiction, with
natural extensions to the study of other psychopathologies, and all work undertaken will maintain an eye
toward clinical application. Research results, methods and analysis pipelines developed by trainees and their
mentors will be made publically available, and may inform the determination of risk factors, treatment and
intervention strategy, and policy decisions made by public health professionals.!

title: Novel gene discovery in disorders of the liver and biliary tree
org_name: CHILDREN'S HOSP OF PHILADELPHIA
phr: Project Narrative Because our understanding of hepatobiliary disease is incomplete, a significant number of children with biliary tree dysgenesis and liver disease have nondiagnostic genetic testing. The goal of this research proposal is to improve the diagnosis and care of children and adults with liver and biliary disease by better characterizing the genetic landscape of these conditions. To accomplish this goal, we will assemble and characterize a large cohort of patients with biliary and liver disease and apply a research pipeline for gene discovery and validation, with additional focus on MED12 as a novel biliary dysgenesis gene.

title: Decoding Functional Cell-Cell Communication to Uncover Cellular State Modulation, Tissue Homeostasis, and Disease Genetics
org_name: WHITEHEAD INSTITUTE FOR BIOMEDICAL RES
phr: 8. PROJECT NARRATIVE
The proposed research is relevant to public health as it aims to develop advanced computational methods to

title: Mass Multivariate Derivation and Validation of AUD Biotypes using Developmental Imaging and Genomic Approaches
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The heterogeneity of alcohol use disorder (AUD) may contribute to the limited effectiveness of treatments. Here, I explore AUD biotypes (subtypes determined by biology) derived from MRI data and characterize the behavioral, genetic, and molecular correlates of these biotypes across the lifespan, as well as their correlates with alcohol involvement trajectories. application_id: 11164622
activity_code: K08
title: Changes in Enteric Microbiota and Inflammation with HIV PrEP
org_name: SEATTLE CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
This proposal will investigate changes to the viral and bacterial communities of the gut in people who take
medicine to prevent HIV infection (PrEP). I will analyze stool and blood samples from volunteers to measure
what viruses and bacteria are there, how they change after 3 months of using PrEP, and how those changes
affect inflammation in the intestines or the entire body. A better understanding of these changes will allow us to
improve the safety and tolerability of PrEP in addition to potential probiotic, prebiotic, or anti-inflamatory
interventions to increase intestinal health.

application_id: 10887226
activity_code: K08
title: Targetable pathways driving sub-optimal early-life CD8 T cell responses
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Viral infections are a leading cause of early-life morbidity and mortality, and CD8 T cells are a crucial component of adaptive immunity responsible for combating viruses and conferring immunological memory. This project aims to understand the role of intrinsically high PI3K/mTOR signaling in early-life CD8 T cells and how this pathway contributes to metabolic and functional peculiarities of early-life T-cell responses in health and severe viral illness. This research will lead to further insights into how early-life CD8 T cells differ functionally from young adults and what pathways could serve as targets for therapies to improve early-life immune responses to infection and vaccination.

application_id: 11307589
activity_code: K08
title: Harnessing machine learning to develop new antibiotics for Neisseria gonorrhoeae
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 The discovery of new antibiotics for Neisseria gonorrhoeae is relevant to public health due to the
increasing prevalence of strains that have developed resistance to ceftriaxone, which is the last remaining
highly effective antibiotic treatment and the standard first-line treatment globally. This project will apply a new
approach to antibiotic discovery in N. gonorrhoeae by using machine learning to virtually screen hundreds of
millions of chemicals for antibacterial activity. This approach promises to address the pressing need for new
antibiotics and stave off the threat of untreatable gonorrhea.

application_id: 11054286
activity_code: K08
title: Evaluating mRNA-lipid nanoparticle platforms and adjuvants to overcome maternal antibody interference
org_name: CHILDREN'S HOSP OF PHILADELPHIA
phr: Project Narrative Despite significant advances in vaccine technology, we still lack vaccines that effectively induce protective antibody responses in newborns and young infants. Vaccine efforts in newborns using conventional platforms are often hindered by interference from maternal antibodies. The proposed research will investigate the potential for mRNA-lipid nanoparticles and lipid nanoparticle adjuvants to overcome maternal antibody interference with the vision that identifying new approaches to vaccine and adjuvant development may impact rational vaccine design for newborns and young infants.
