title: Mechanisms Contributing to T Cell Immune Dysregulation in STAT3 Gain-of-Function
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Primary immune regulatory disorders (PIRD) are a rapidly expanding subset of inborn errors of immunity characterized by disorganized and aberrant immune responses, and often present with variable manifestations such as autoimmunity and infectious complications. This project will investigate the development of antigen- specific responses and cellular factors contributing to T cell dysregulation in STAT3 gain-of-function syndrome, a known monogenic PIRD. application_id: 11054847
activity_code: K08
title: Dynamic FOXP3 Expression in the Development of Regulatory T cells from Induced Pluripotent Stem Cells
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA LOS ANGELES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Regulatory T cells (Tregs) suppress immune activation and inflammation and represent an attractive substrate for adoptive cell therapy in autoimmune diseases. Current bottlenecks exist in utilizing Tregs as therapy due to their rarity and unstable suppressive function. This proposal will study the role of endogenous and exogenous expression of FOXP3 – the master transcription factor of Tregs – in the development of thymic-like Tregs from an inexhaustible source: induced pluripotent stem cells.

application_id: 10894037
activity_code: DP2
title: Using three-dimensional protein networks to uncover immuno-modulatory molecular phenotypes in infectious disease
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: Project Narrative I propose to develop and use a novel framework to integrate host genetic data with three-dimensional structurally-resolved host-pathogen protein networks to uncover immuno-modulatory molecular phenotypes in infectious disease. The incorporation of structural information provides a significant refinement over the traditional principle of “guilt-by-association”, and allows us to hone in on specific molecular phenotypes that modulate infectious disease risk. While the focus of the proposal is to use this approach to infectious diseases, the framework itself is generalizable and can be used across other immunological disease contexts.

application_id: 10875622
activity_code: K08
title: The Effect of Natural Killer Cell-Based Therapy on the HIV Reservoir
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA LOS ANGELES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE / RELEVANCE TO PUBLIC HEALTH
Antiretroviral drugs can inhibit HIV infection, but cannot eliminate all the infected cells in the body that hide
from the immune system. In order to cure HIV, we need to develop methods that accurately quantify the
number of infected cells that remain hidden and explore new treatments that effectively target these HIV-
infected cells. This project has scientific and public health relevance by deepening our understanding of how
HIV remains hidden and ultimately uses this knowledge to develop curative strategies.

application_id: 10755641
activity_code: F31
title: Activity Dependent Changes in the Contextual Memory Engram
org_name: ALBERT EINSTEIN COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The primary goal of this proposal is to identify the activity-dependent mechanisms mediating memory formation in the CA3 region of the hippocampus. Learning and memory is driven by activity-induced changes in neurons and circuits, and the CA3 region of the hippocampus is particularly important in linking sensory experiences to the context in which they occur. Understanding how CA3 neurons change during the formation of a contextual memory will provide us with valuable foundational knowledge of proper cognitive function, and will allow us to further investigate cases of cognitive disorders.

application_id: 10875614
activity_code: K01
title: Tuberculosis in teens: a geospatial approach to predict community transmission
org_name: BOSTON UNIVERSITY MEDICAL CAMPUS
phr: Modified Public Health Relevance Section
Adolescents may be drivers of local tuberculosis transmission; they are a unique population due to their dynamic cognitive, psychological, and physical changes, and they can spread tuberculosis through their expanding social networks. A more robust understanding of tuberculosis epidemics will be gained through the proposed research by using geographic information and tuberculosis strain types to identify individual-, household- and population-level drivers of tuberculosis spread in adolescents. Our study will elucidate important person, place, and time interactions of tuberculosis in adolescents to inform how to best deploy tailored interventions to reduce tuberculosis spread in communities.

application_id: 11127433
activity_code: K08
title: Altered CD4+ T cell responses and resultant asthma following neonatal human metapneumovirus infection
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: NARRATIVE
Respiratory viral infections in neonates and infants are associated with increased acute morbidity and an
increased long-term risk of developing asthma. This proposal aims to understand the unique intrinsic and
extrinsic mediators of the altered CD4+ T cell response following human metapneumovirus infection in neonatal
mice. Further, this proposal will evaluate the long-term effects of neonatal human metapneumovirus infection
on formation of tissue resident memory cells and the role of these cells in asthma pathogenesis.

title: Mechanisms that govern dopaminergic amacrine cell diversity
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: Dopamine signaling is crucial for proper development and function of the retina and reduced signaling is associated with many visual disorders. The dopaminergic amacrine cells are the primary source of dopamine. Understanding molecular mechanisms that specify the formation of these cells is critical to develop therapeutics that can restore dopamine levels in diseased states.

application_id: 11072957
activity_code: F31
title: Sex Differences in T cell metabolism
org_name: HARVARD MEDICAL SCHOOL
phr: Project Narrative
It has long been observed that disease severity and outcomes are different in males and females, yet we don’t
know why. The goal of my proposed project is to answer this question by looking at differences in T cell
metabolism between males and females, and how they relate to viral infection outcomes. Understanding the
biology of sex differences in T cells, metabolism, and infection will offer context to sex differences in immunity
as well as new sex-specific therapies for infection.

application_id: 10896130
activity_code: K08
title: Using natural killer cells to prevent breast cancer metastases
org_name: UT SOUTHWESTERN MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Project Narrative I discovered that invasive breast cancer cells can reprogram natural killer (NK) cells into being metastasis- promoters. I propose to determine the mechanisms driving metastases-reprogrammed NK cells and to determine its significance in human breast cancer. NK cell directed therapies could provide alternative immunotherapies for metastatic breast cancer patients and I will complete key preclinical experiments needed to translate these insights to patient benefit.

title: TRIM 14 is a master regulator of STAT3 activity during the macrophage innate immune responses to Mycobacterium tuberculosis
org_name: TEXAS A&M UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCE CTR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Mycobacterium tuberculosis (Mtb), the causative agent of tuberculosis, successfully evades host defenses to make it one of most infectious pathogens in the world. activity_code: F32
title: Interrogating Dynamic Regulation of Pre-Synaptic Release Probability In Vivo
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative:  Release probability (P(r)) of neurotransmitter containing vesicles is a critical component of information transfer between neurons and is dynamically regulated by a variety of molecular and circuit mechanisms. Here I combine an array of novel imaging, electrophysiological, behavioral, and pharmacological methods to interrogate the regulation of P(r) in vivo at multiple different scales. This will lead to a novel understanding of how individual pre-synaptic sites are regulated and bridge a wealth of literature in reduced preparations to the awake, behaving brain.

application_id: 11291825
activity_code: K08
title: Understanding the mechanism of tropism specific immune restriction in HIV infection
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Efforts to develop a cure for HIV have been hindered by HIV’s rapid mutational rate and ability to evade
immune pressure. During the early clinical stages of HIV infection, a specific subset of HIV, X4-tropic viruses,
are naturally restricted by the innate immune, while R5-tropic viruses replicate freely. In the proposed study,
we will investigate the molecular mechanism that mediate this differential viral restriction using banked patient
samples, in vivo mouse models, and ex vivo primary human cell analyses, with the goal of generating
foundational data for the future development of novel therapeutics that empower our immune system to clear
R5-tropic viral infections.

title: Epidermal Growth Factor Receptor Signaling in Fibrotic Skin Disease
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: NARRATIVE Fibrosis is a common final outcome of inflammatory diseases that results in excess collagen and other extracellular matrix proteins in tissue. This proposal uses mouse models to elucidate how chronic epidermal growth factor receptor activation drives fibrosis in the skin and its relationship with interferon signaling. By understanding how this receptor tyrosine kinase drives the production of matrix proteins, we will be able to develop new therapies that target these processes to help improve patients' lives.

application_id: 11163522
activity_code: DP1
title: Building Bridges to Allow Cross-species Translational genetics for the Study of Addiction
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Human and model organism research has generated oceans of genetic and genomic data that can provide insights into the
neurobiological mechanisms of addiction. However, we lack the tools to integrate these data across species, which is
hindering our ability to make actionable breakthroughs. This project will develop a toolkit for making cross-species
integration a routine task; such framework should enable a follow-up of genetic association results with multi-omics
investigations of molecular mechanisms, to facilitate their clinical translation and improve diagnosis, monitoring and
treatment of addiction.

application_id: 11164832
activity_code: K08
title: Mechanisms of sex discrepancy in autoimmune disease: Regulation of the female-biased VGLL3 immune pathway
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE (PUBLIC HEALTH RELEVANCE)
Women are dying from autoimmune disease at an alarming rate, and the National Institutes of Health identifies
autoimmune disease as the top cause of morbidity among women in the United States. Current therapies for
autoimmune diseases are dangerous and often ineffective, but development of new therapies is hampered by
poor understanding of the mechanisms causing autoimmunity, including why women are so disproportionately
affected. This project will define how the skin drives systemic autoimmune disease, identifying female-biased
pathways and factors critical to autoimmunity in order to pave the way for new therapies to address this
significant threat to public health.

application_id: 11158743
activity_code: K08
title: The Role of Hormonal Dysregulation in Systemic Sclerosis
org_name: MEDICAL UNIVERSITY OF SOUTH CAROLINA
phr: Project Narrative
Systemic sclerosis is a rare autoimmune disease that leads to widespread fibrosis and death. Although rare,
having a better understanding of systemic sclerosis may be helpful in more prevalent diseases where fibrosis
also occurs and accounts for up to 45% of deaths. Because women are more often diagnosed with systemic
sclerosis and have more estrogen, the goal of this project is to discover if estrogen encourages skin fibrosis in
systemic sclerosis, so that medications that block estrogen may be used to treat fibrosis.

phr: Project Narrative
Co-use of alcohol and cannabis (using both substances, either simultaneously or concurrently) represents a
significant public health concern among young adults (YAs), as co-use is common and can exacerbate
negative use-related outcomes, and existing interventions produce small effects. Romantic relationships are

among YAs. The present study seeks to significantly expand our understanding of alcohol and cannabis co-

application_id: 10878955
activity_code: K08
title: Identification of Pathogenic T cells in Axial Spondyloarthritis
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Spondyloarthritis is a complex disease with a multitude of manifestations, spanning the joint, skin, gut, and eye. Given the inherent immune restrictions of the eye, we theorize that sampling the human eye during periods of ocular inflammation will enrich for pathogenic immune cells in spondyloarthritis, specifically T cells. We propose to use single-cell RNA sequencing to identify putative pathogenic T cells in the human eye and to assess whether dysregulation of these T cells track with disease activity in other anatomic sites.

application_id: 11130956
activity_code: K08
title: Role of VISTA in discoid lupus erythematosus
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Discoid lupus erythematosus is a chronic autoimmune skin disease characterized by a complex immune
infiltration and current treatments are often ineffective. We will perform translational immunologic research
including spatial proteomics and spatial transcriptomics to delineate the key cells and inflammatory pathways
that drive disease development in both humans and mice, with particular emphasis on immune inhibitory
receptors. This wok will advance our understanding of discoid lupus and help identify additional targeted
strategies for treating this disease.

title: Immunophenotypes in the Idiopathic Inflammatory Myopathies
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The idiopathic inflammatory myopathies are rare orphan disease that are hallmarked by the presence of various autoantibodies each with their own unique clinical phenotype. In this proposal, we utilize human peripheral blood mononuclear cells (PBMCs) from patients with IIM and healthy controls to understand the immune signatures and transcriptomics that drive IIM pathogenesis. We will also take the prior discovery that Jo1+ patients overexpress the redox sensor TXNIP and ascertain how TXNIP overexpression alters B cell metabolism.

application_id: 11112496
activity_code: DP2
title: Mining the phage playbook to create a potent, generic phage therapy
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The growing spread of antibiotic resistance has led to an urgent need for new treatments to bacterial
infections. While the treatment of bacterial infections with their viruses, bacteriophages, has enormous
potential, it is held back by the high levels of specificity of phages for bacterial strains. Here, I will develop a
phage therapy treatment that is able to overcome existing bacterial defense mechanisms, one of the key
factors driving specificity and thus holding back this therapeutic option.

application_id: 11078296
activity_code: K08
title: Targeting cutaneous nociceptors to reduce Type-17 inflammation in hidradenitis suppurativa
org_name: UNIV OF MASSACHUSETTS MED SCH WORCESTER
phr: The immune system dysfunction underlying Hidradenitis Suppurativa (HS) is poorly understood
and the devastating effects this disease has on quality of life disproportionately affect women
and minorities, highlighting a critical health care gap. This study will comprehensively
characterize activated immune cells surrounding hair follicles within HS lesions, which represent
the pathologic anatomic unit, with spatial precision and test the novel hypothesis that
neurogenic inflammation contributes to dysregulated microbe sensing in HS by inducing strong
Interleukin (IL)-1, TNF- and IL-17-mediated immune responses. We expect that the proposed
studies will identify neurotransmitter inhibitors, many of which are FDA-approved for other
indications, as promising treatments for HS.

application_id: 11074129
activity_code: K08
title: Pathogenic T cells in discoid lupus erythematosus
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Discoid lupus erythematosus is a chronic disfiguring autoimmune skin disease characterized by an immune
cell infiltrate predominantly composed of T lymphocytes, which appear to promote tissue inflammation and
damage, but how they do so is not well understood. I will perform translational immunologic research on
human skin and blood samples with parallel studies in a mouse model to delineate the roles of key survival,
inflammatory, cytotoxic, and other effector pathways in potentially pathogenic T lymphocytes, and to probe how
they are regulated. These efforts will advance our understanding of discoid lupus erythematosus and help
identify targeted strategies for treating this disease.

application_id: 10993153
activity_code: K08
title: Architecture and interactions of Granzyme K+ CD8 T cells in inflamed synovium in rheumatoid arthritis
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: Project Narrative This project will investigate the effects of granzyme K-expressing CD8 T cells on fibroblasts in synovial tissue in patients with rheumatoid arthritis. We have detected CD8 T cells with a similar profile in several other diseased tissues, including kidney in lupus nephritis, inflamed bowel in Crohn’s disease and ulcerative colitis, and lung (broncheoalveolar lavage fluid) in severe SARS-CoV-2 infection. Thus, our findings within the context of rheumatoid arthritis may improve our ability to understand, diagnose, and treat many diseases.

application_id: 10766844
activity_code: K08
title: Single-cell dissection of CD4 T cell changes in patients with immune-related adverse events following PD-1 inhibition
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: Project Narrative PD-1 immune checkpoint inhibition (ICI) in patients with cancer is associated with secondary immune-related adverse events (irAEs) that limit its use, especially for those patients with the higher-grade toxicities. PD-1 signaling is important for maintaining immune homeostasis but the exact mechanism through which PD-1 inhibition leads to autoimmunity is not understood. This proposal will test the hypothesis that de novo loss of PD- 1 signaling enhances the antigen sensitivity of CD4 T cells and leads to the activation of autoreactive CD4 T cells that mediate irAE development.

application_id: 11124607
activity_code: K08
title: Harnessing single cell RNA sequencing and integrative bioinformatics to identify precision therapeutics for dermatomyositis
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: Project Narrative
The majority of children and adults with dermatomyositis (DM) still experience long-term functional impairment
with modern treatments. Drug repositioning is an untapped opportunity to identify existing treatments that work
in a precise manner for complex autoimmune diseases, like DM. Using a combination of single-cell RNA
sequencing, computational drug repurposing, and ex-vivo cell culture assays, we will identify existing
compounds that perturb DM-associated molecular networks in DM-associated cell types with the long-term
goal of improving health outcomes through the identification of precision medicine treatments.

application_id: 11144752
activity_code: K08
title: The Role of the Gut Microbiome in Post-Sepsis Skeletal Muscle Atrophy
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF IOWA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Sepsis is a common and often deadly condition of organ dysfunction due to infection that is responsible for 1.7 million hospitalizations and an estimated 260,000 deaths annually in the United States; survivors of sepsis are often left debilitated due to muscle atrophy, which hinders independence and reduces quality of life. Unfortunately, other than physical rehabilitation, there are no treatments currently available for post-sepsis muscle atrophy because we do not understand how sepsis causes muscle atrophy. This project will study how the gut microbiome, the bacteria that live in the human intestinal tract, contributes to muscle atrophy during sepsis, with a goal of identifying new targets for treatment.

application_id: 11053614
activity_code: K08
title: Contributions of gastrointestinal Staphylococcus aureus and other gut microbiota to pediatric atopic dermatitis
org_name: NEW YORK UNIVERSITY SCHOOL OF MEDICINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Atopic dermatitis is a very common disease with substantial morbidity, most prevalent among infants and children
who are also colonized at high rates in the gut by Staphylococcus aureus. While S. aureus is known to contribute
to atopic dermatitis pathogenesis, studies have primarily focused on S. aureus in the nares and on the skin, with
little attention to S. aureus and other microbiota in the gastrointestinal tract. The proposed project aims to
elucidate the contributions of gastrointestinal S. aureus and other gut microbiota to pediatric atopic dermatitis,
thereby furthering our understanding of S. aureus colonization dynamics beyond the nares and skin, and
potentially revealing novel therapeutic targets for the treatment of atopic dermatitis.

application_id: 10864637
activity_code: K08
title: Contributions of gastrointestinal Staphylococcus aureus and other gut microbiota to pediatric atopic dermatitis
org_name: NEW YORK UNIVERSITY SCHOOL OF MEDICINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Atopic dermatitis is a very common disease with substantial morbidity, most prevalent among infants and children who are also colonized at high rates in the gut by Staphylococcus aureus. While S. aureus is known to contribute to atopic dermatitis pathogenesis, studies have primarily focused on S. aureus in the nares and on the skin, with little attention to S. aureus and other microbiota in the gastrointestinal tract. The proposed project aims to elucidate the contributions of gastrointestinal S. aureus and other gut microbiota to pediatric atopic dermatitis, thereby furthering our understanding of S. aureus colonization dynamics beyond the nares and skin, and potentially revealing novel therapeutic targets for the treatment of atopic dermatitis.

application_id: 10950024
activity_code: K08
title: Role of the gut barrier in inflammatory arthritis
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Antibiotic alteration of the gut microbiota can worsen the severity of inflammatory arthritis but how this occurs is not well understood. This proposal aims to extend our prior work to define how antibiotic-induced gut dysbiosis affects local responses in the gut epithelium and distal inflammation in the joint. This Mentored Clinical Scientist Research Career Development Award (K08) application proposes a research and career development plan over the next 5 years to allow the PI to study this important research question and develop the skills and training to become a successful, independent physician-scientist investigating the gut microbiome interface and the gut- joint axis.

application_id: 11031549
activity_code: K08
title: DISSECTING THE SPATIAL HETEROGENEITY OF SYNOVIAL INFLAMMATION IN RHEUMATOID ARTHRITIS
org_name: BRIGHAM AND WOMEN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Although the field of rheumatology is beginning to subtype rheumatoid arthritis patients using relative abundances of certain cell types in the synovium, our understanding of what these cell types do and how they work together is limited. In this proposal, we will define how immunity is spatially organized in the rheumatoid arthritis synovium, both across rheumatoid arthritis subtypes and in comparison to other autoimmune diseases, using cutting-edge techniques from statistics and artificial intelligence. This will enable mechanistic understanding of known disease subtypes, discovery of improved disease subtypes, and extrapolation of mechanistic and therapeutic hypotheses from other related diseases.

application_id: 11032743
activity_code: K08
title: Targeting MBNL1-mediated alternative splicing in MLL-fusion leukemia
org_name: CINCINNATI CHILDRENS HOSP MED CTR
phr: Project Narrative:
Leukemias with rearrangements of the MLL (KMT2A) gene, classically seen in infants and therapy-related
leukemia, remain difficult to treat and cure even with modern therapies. These leukemias highly express an
RNA binding protein, MBNL1, which regulates alternative RNA splicing and is essential for leukemia cell
survival. The proposed research will explore the mechanisms underlying this dependence, and further develop
a new and much-needed targeted therapy against this protein for this high-risk group of leukemias.

application_id: 10894910
activity_code: K23
title: Leveraging Molecular Technologies to Improve Diagnosis and Management of Pediatric Acute Respiratory Illness in Resource-Constrained Settings
org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The management of pediatric acute respiratory illness (ARI) in resource-constrained settings in sub-Saharan Africa is almost entirely based on clinical symptoms, the non-specific nature of which contributes to both over- and under-treatment with antibiotics. Measuring host immune response gene expression can differentiate between viral and bacterial ARI, but the validation of classifiers based on this methodology has included very little data from sub-Saharan Africa where the burden of ARI is high and co-morbidities that may affect the immune response like malaria and malnutrition are more common. This study seeks to develop and internally validate novel clinical algorithms for pediatric ARI that leverage existing host immune response classifiers for ARI after optimizatio...

activity_code: F31
title: Low Dose Computed Tomography (LDCT) Eligibility and Outcome differences between Sexual and Gender Minorities and their Sexual and Gender Majority Counterparts
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: Project Narrative Low dose computed tomography (LDCT) is a procedure recommended as a screening test for adults who have a high risk for lung cancer that has been shown to reduce lung cancer mortality. Sexual and gender minority (SGM) populations may be at higher risk for lung cancer mortality due to a higher risk of cigarette smoking. Understanding rates of LDCT screening eligibility for SGM individuals, SGM-related LDCT screening disparities, and modifiable factors for such disparities can inform efforts to reduce morbidity and mortality caused by cigarette smoking, such as lung cancer and other lung-related conditions.

activity_code: K99
title: Revealing new short tandem repeat variation in the human population across sequencing technologies: towards rare disease diagnosis and discovery
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF UTAH
phr: Project Narrative Short tandem repeats (STRs) are repetitive DNA sequences that cause dozens of genetic diseases in humans and are difficult to sequence and interpret, leaving individuals with STR-associated disease undiagnosed. This proposal will establish a genetic diagnosis for hundreds of patients, while searching for new STR disease loci. To accomplish this, we will develop new computational approaches and call STRs across the genome in thousands of individuals to establish a population-scale STR catalog, with an emphasis on diverse and under- served populations.

title: Transfusion Biology and Cellular Therapies
org_name: BOSTON CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE While an important focus of Transfusion Medicine has been to maintain immune compatibility of blood components as well as the safe and appropriate use of blood products, the expansive growth in knowledge of blood cell biology has correspondingly increased options for investigation. Transfusion Medicine scientists and specialists now direct the generation of novel blood component products and the translation of cell-based therapies. Our postdoctoral training program is tailored to meet the recognized need for scientific training of individuals to further advance knowledge and clinical use of hematopoietic cellular therapies to treat diseases.

application_id: 10871844
activity_code: K08
title: Exploring the combinatorial efficacy between chemotherapy and T cell checkpoint inhibition and the role of cellular senescence
org_name: SLOAN-KETTERING INST CAN RESEARCH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
In patients with non-small cell lung cancer, combination treatment with chemotherapy and T cell checkpoint
inhibition is more efficacious than chemotherapy alone, so it has recently become the standard of care. Despite
increasing enthusiasm for this combination treatment, the reasons for its combinatorial efficacy remain unclear.
We seek to understand these reasons by investigating treatment-induced mediators of inflammation which, if
identified, would allow for rationally designed combinations and inform further drug discovery efforts.

application_id: 10875391
activity_code: K08
title: Immunologic Biomarkers of Rejection in Clinical Liver Transplantation
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF SOUTHERN CALIFORNIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Hepatocellular carcinoma is on pace to become the leading global indication for liver transplantation, and
rejection continues to be an important cause of graft loss and failure post-transplant. The diagnosis of rejection
is not correlative to changes in liver blood tests and thus requires an invasive biopsy, and very little is known
about which immune cells mediate this disease process. This application seeks to overcome this barrier by
developing new mass cytometry-based methods to measure liver transplant rejection severity and progression,
and these methods may become valuable tools for future clinical trials of immunosuppression minimization or
withdrawal.

application_id: 11056723
activity_code: K08
title: Mechanistic Determination of KRAS Lung Cancer Regression upon CRAF Suppression
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Advanced non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) is the leading cause of cancer mortality worldwide with limited
treatment options. This proposal utilizes biochemical and immunological techniques to investigate the
mechanism of a novel pathway that induces tumor regression in NSCLC. The findings from this study will have
a direct impact on the development of new therapeutic strategies for patients with this deadly disease.

application_id: 10828406
activity_code: K08
title: Local blockade of IL10R on innate immune cells to mediate both innate and adaptive anti-tumor immunity
org_name: SLOAN-KETTERING INST CAN RESEARCH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
In situ vaccination is a type of cancer immunotherapy that consists of administering a treatment directly into a
tumor in order to regress that tumor and initiate a systemic immune response against tumor antigens. Our
preliminary data suggest that by incorporating a blocking antibody against the IL10 receptor into in situ
vaccines we enhance innate immune cells' ability to (i) directly kill tumor cells and (ii) prime tumor-specific T-
cells that act systemically. In experiments that will lead to clinical trials aimed at extending the lives of patients
with solid cancers, we will investigate the mechanism and the generalizability of this effect.

application_id: 11095983
activity_code: K08
title: Characterizing the XRN1 exoribonuclease as a therapeutic target in non-small cell lung cancer
org_name: DANA-FARBER CANCER INST
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) is a leading cause of cancer-related mortality in the United States and
worldwide, underscoring the critical need to identify new therapeutic targets for this disease. This project seeks
to define the fundamental mechanisms by which the XRN1 exoribonuclease, an enzyme that functions to
degrade cellular RNA, regulates the survival of NSCLC cells and the anti-tumor immune response to NSCLC
tumors. The proposed research will determine whether targeting XRN1 may represent a unique and broadly
applicable treatment strategy for patients with NSCLC.

application_id: 11128349
activity_code: K08
title: Elucidating the Mitochondrial and Nuclear functions of ATP Synthase Subunit ATP5A1 that Maintain Genome Integrity in Response to Oxidative Stress
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ALABAMA AT BIRMINGHAM
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Maintenance of genome integrity is a fundamental function of all cellular life that depends upon robust antioxidant
systems to defend against reactive oxygen species. I discovered a novel nuclear function for ATP synthase
subunit alpha (ATP5A1) to respond to oxidative stress that involves suppression of RNA/DNA hybrids and
protection from DNA damage. This project will further elucidate the role of ATP5A1 in coordinating oxidative
metabolism with genome maintenance and lead to new research and therapeutic opportunities at the interface
of oxidative stress, genome maintenance, and cancer.

org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: Alzheimer’s disease and related dementias (ADRD) is a public health priority and is well-established as a health concern. Yet, little is understood about the epidemiology and risk of ADRD among adults from under resourced communities, including people who use male or female hormones. This proposal will determine the extent of differences in ADRD outcomes and ADRD risk among adults from under-resourced communities,
characterize psychosocial stressors (e.g., stress-induced conditions, negative public attitudes, social rejection and isolation, economic instability) expected to influence cognition, and explore associations between cognitive function, hormone replacement therapy, psychosocial stressors, and ADRD risk and protective factors among

application_id: 11010416
activity_code: F30
title: Multiplex functional assay of variant effect in the retinal transcription factor CRX
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Genetic variants in the gene CRX can cause inherited retinal diseases that lead to vision loss and blindness, but many CRX genetic variants are uncharacterized. This proposal aims to apply Deep Mutational Scanning, a new technique for studying many genetic variants at the same time, to comprehensively identify genetic variants in CRX that cause disease. This work will produce a “look-up table” listing all possible variants in CRX by their functional activity and propensity to cause disease, and will be directly applicable to the diagnosis of patients with diseases caused by mutations of CRX.

application_id: 11143842
activity_code: K01
title: Collaboration on HIV and AgingResearch through the Study of Mitochondria
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: NARRATIVE/RELEVANCE
The lifespan of people with HIV has been considerably extended, but the burden of age-related comorbidities
remains high among them. This proposal will evaluate the role of mitochondrial function and DNA methylation
in biological aging (represented by frailty phenotypes, epigenetic clock acceleration, and immunosenescence)
among people with HIV, as well evaluate the potential of exercise to reverse mitochondrial function damage
and epigenetic clock acceleration caused by HIV. Completion of this proposal will enhance the understanding
of the role of mitochondrial function in age acceleration among people with HIV as well as highlight potential
interventions to mitigate the damage, which ultimately could lead to improvements in treatment and long-term
care strategies for ...

application_id: 11083674
activity_code: K08
title: Mechanisms of resistance to MEK Inhibition in RAS-pathway activated chronic myelomonocytic leukemia
org_name: UTAH STATE HIGHER EDUCATION SYSTEM--UNIVERSITY OF UTAH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Molecularly targeted treatments have revolutionized cancer treatment but the development of drug resistance to
cancer-directed therapy is a major barrier to improved survival outcomes. This research proposal combines a
rationally designed clinical trial with a laboratory-based approach to investigate the efficacy of a molecularly
targeted agent in chronic myelomonocytic leukemia and identify potential sources of drug resistance to this
agent. These findings will inform novel treatment strategies for chronic myelomonocytic leukemia by
circumventing the emergence of drug resistance, leading to better patient outcomes in this poor-risk disease.

application_id: 10923880
activity_code: K08
title: The Impact of Genetic Ancestry on Racial Disparities in Hepatocellular Carcinoma Risk and Mortality
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MIAMI SCHOOL OF MEDICINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC), also known as primary liver cancer, disproportionately affects minorities and the burden of this deadly disease has increased significantly. The proposed study will determine if genetic differences in African, European or Native American ancestry are responsible for the significant racial differences in liver cancer risk and survival, either directly or indirectly by interacting with other risk factors or social factors. Understanding which genetic features increase liver cancer risk or worsen survival could potentially improve our ability to (1) identify those people who are at highest risk and (2) develop screening and treatment strategies that are based on a person’s individualized risk.

application_id: 11241994
activity_code: K23
title: Clinical and Non-Invasive Biomarkers of Myotonic Dystrophy
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Myotonic dystrophy (DM) is the most frequent muscular dystrophy in adults with a prevalence of 1 in 8,000
people, it affects several organs such as skeletal muscle, heart, and brain, it is inherited in an autosomal
dominant fashion, and no cure exists to date. Promising therapies are in current development to target the
expanded RNA-mediated toxicity associated with this disease; however, reliable biomarkers to monitor disease
activity and response to upcoming treatments are lacking. For this purpose, this research proposal aims at
identifying non-invasive, painless, and quantitative biomarkers of DM muscle disease by using, in parallel but
independently, muscle ultrasound and MRI, and electrical impedance myography at two-year interval in these
patients.

application_id: 11003357
activity_code: F32
title: Maternal organelle contribution to offspring germline health
org_name: WHITEHEAD INSTITUTE FOR BIOMEDICAL RES
phr: Project Narrative Organelles are essential cellular compartments with a diverse set of functions. How parental gametes partition their organelles to their progeny and the significance parental organelles play in progeny development remain poorly understood. I take a multi-disciplinary approach using the model organism Drosophila melanogaster to identify maternally deposited organelle proteins that persist in their offspring and track how these long-lived proteins influence germline health of the next generation.

application_id: 11101294

title: Non-invasive characterization of human soft tissue sarcoma response to radiation therapy
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 Although half of patients with soft tissue sarcoma develop distant metastases after radiation therapy and
surgery, there are currently no biomarkers to identify patients who would benefit from adjuvant systemic therapy
and no known genetic alterations that mediate soft tissue sarcoma response to radiation therapy. We propose
to use circulating tumor DNA analysis to identify patients at high risk of relapse who could benefit from additional
therapy and discover genetic alterations that mediate radiation response. This work will lay the foundation for
future clinical trials incorporating circulating tumor DNA analysis and tumor genomics to personalize treatment
approaches and improve outcomes in patients with soft tissue sarcomas and other malignancies treated with
radiati...

application_id: 11137774
activity_code: K08
title: Enhancing the efficacy of Radiation Therapy for brainstem glioma by targeting ATM
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Brainstem gliomas are lethal brain tumors that represent the most common cause of brain
tumor-related death in children. Radiation therapy can provide temporary relief from
neurological symptoms, but despite radiation therapy the tumors eventually progress and lead
to death. In this project we will investigate the mechanisms by which brainstem gliomas resist
radiation therapy, which will guide the design of new treatment strategies that enhance the
efficacy of radiation therapy in order to improve survival for patients with brainstem gliomas.

application_id: 11118679
activity_code: T32
title: Regenerative Engineering of Musculoskeletal Tissues- a Convergence Doctoral Training Program
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CONNECTICUT SCH OF MED/DNT
phr: PUBLIC HEALTH RELEVANCE
Disorders of the musculoskeletal system with advancing age or due to injury and trauma are among the most
debilitating to the human body and costly to the healthcare system with disability. Novel treatments will require
convergence of molecular, cellular, and organismic research through interdisciplinary integration of biomedical
science and engineering. This T32 Program is based on the unique concept of training Ph.D. candidates in the
realm of scientific convergence applied to the field of regenerative engineering to enable these discoveries.

application_id: 11175417
activity_code: K08
title: Identifying responders to chemotherapy in invasive lobular carcinoma of the breast: development of a multivariable clinical prediction tool
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: Project Narrative
By conventional measures, women with estrogen receptor positive invasive lobular breast cancer (ILC) have
poor responses to standard treatment approaches. Given the poor outcomes seen in patients with ILC, there
is a great need to improve patient selection for standard chemotherapy and identify novel treatment strategies.
In this project, I will develop a new clinical prediction tool to identify chemotherapy responders, and conduct a
pilot trial testing a novel agent for estrogen receptor positive tumors.

application_id: 10895415
activity_code: K01
title: Dissecting the role of Six1 and its co-factors during calvarial bone and suture development
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF DELAWARE
phr: NARRATIVE Variants in SIX1, a transcription factor implicated in many cellular processes during craniofacial development and in branchio-oto-renal syndrome, have been recently linked to craniosynostosis (premature closure of the sutures between the skull bones); this is one of the most prevalent birth defects affecting ~1:2500 live births. The studies proposed in this NIDCR career development award application will address the major knowledge gap regarding the role of Six1 in calvarial bone development, and provide new knowledge regarding the pathogenesis of this prevalent birth defect, thus facilitating diagnosis and care of craniosynostosis patients.

application_id: 11124157
activity_code: K23
title: The Landscape of Serious Infections following Kidney Transplantation in People Living with HIV
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Kidney transplantation (KT) is a rapidly-expanding, lifesaving intervention for people living with HIV (PLWH)
and end-stage renal disease, yet the landscape of serious post-transplant infections and associated risk
factors including immunosuppressive regimens remain undefined. This proposal aims to utilize national KT
cohort data to characterize the burden, timing, and spectrum of infections in HIV+ KT recipients, with
comparison to HIV- KT controls, in order to evaluate modifiable risk factors and guide potential targeted
monitoring and prophylaxis. In addition, we seek to understand whether there are differential infectious risks
associated with HIV+ organ donation within the emerging population of HIV-to-HIV KT recently legalized under
the HIV Organ Policy Equity (HOPE...

application_id: 11113485
activity_code: K99
title: The role of motor cortex in control of flight in the mammalian brain
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA BERKELEY
phr: Our understanding of how the motor cortex controls natural complex behaviors is crucial for treating movement disorders which affect millions across the world, yet it remains limited, partly due to the absence of a tractable model system that naturally exhibits complex, dexterous yet highly reproducible movement. We propose the first study of the motor cortical computations underlying flight in bats, which is a complex yet naturally reproducible behavior that requires high coordination and precision achieved through the control hand like sophisticated wings. The insight gained from this new experimental paradigm for dexterous movement has the potential to advance fundamental understanding and thus treatment prospects for movement disorders in the future.

application_id: 10907522
activity_code: K23
title: Erythrocyte contribution to coagulopathy and cerebral oxygenation after intracerebral hemorrhage
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE: Hemostasis and blood clot formation is thought to be driven primarily by coagulation factors, platelets, and fibrinogen and intracerebral hemorrhage (ICH) treatment paradigms focus on optimizing these components to minimize ongoing bleeding. However, outside of their known role of oxygen delivery, there is evidence that erythrocytes, otherwise known as red blood cells (RBCs), also play a role in hemostasis and blood clot formation. This project will explore the independent contribution of RBCs on both hemostasis and cerebral oxygenation after ICH in efforts to identify novel treatment targets to improve ICH outcome.

application_id: 11135590
activity_code: K08
title: Targeting the DNA damage response with PARP and ATR inhibition to potentiate cytotoxicity and improve efficacy of immune checkpoint blockade in IDH mutant gliomas
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Immune checkpoint inhibitors have, thus far, been ineffective in the treatment of gliomas, largely due to the
immunologically “cold” microenvironment and a low number of neoantigens. Our group recently discovered that
IDH1/2 mutations, which are common in gliomas, induce homologous recombination defects and confer
sensitivity to DNA damage response inhibitors. The goal of this project is to uncover strategies to exploit
IDH1/2-induced DNA repair defects with DNA damage response inhibitors to induce synthetic lethality and
generate an immunologically favorable tumor microenvironment thereby improving the response to immune
checkpoint inhibition.

application_id: 11045093
activity_code: K08
title: The Pediatric Advanced Cancer Experience (PACE): Measuring Quality of End-of-Life Care for Children with Cancer
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Although most children with cancer are cured long-term, 10-15% of children will ultimately develop advanced,
incurable illness, rendering cancer the leading cause of non-accidental death in children. Currently, no
measures exist to assess the quality of care children with cancer receive near the end of life. The proposed
research will help develop family-centered measures of high quality end-of-life care and test novel methods of
evaluating quality.

application_id: 10911335
activity_code: K08
title: The role of IL6-induced LAG3 as a resistance mechanism to PD1 blockade in NSCLC patients
org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE: Non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) leads to more cancer death annually than any other cancer, and despite the landmark approval of anti-PD-1 therapy for NSCLC, the majority of patients still have progressive disease. The IL-6/STAT3 pathway has been identified in association with decreased response to anti-PD-1 therapy in NSCLC patients, but the mechanism by which IL-6 leads to anti-PD-1 resistance is poorly understood. This proposed study will advance our basic understanding of IL-6-mediated anti-PD-1 resistance, and inform future combination therapy development in NSCLC for patients that have not responded to anti-PD-1 therapy.

application_id: 10867293
activity_code: K08
title: The Pediatric Advanced Cancer Experience (PACE): Measuring Quality of End-of-Life Care for Children with Cancer
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Although most children with cancer are cured long-term, 10-15% of children will ultimately develop advanced, incurable illness, rendering cancer the leading cause of non-accidental death in children. Currently, no measures exist to assess the quality of care children with cancer receive near the end of life. The proposed research will help develop family-centered measures of high quality end-of-life care and test novel methods of evaluating quality.

activity_code: K08
title: The role of IL6-induced LAG3 as a resistance mechanism to PD1 blockade in NSCLC patients
org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE:
Non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) leads to more cancer death annually than any other cancer, and despite
the landmark approval of anti-PD-1 therapy for NSCLC, the majority of patients still have progressive disease.
The IL-6/STAT3 pathway has been identified in association with decreased response to anti-PD-1 therapy in
NSCLC patients, but the mechanism by which IL-6 leads to anti-PD-1 resistance is poorly understood. This
proposed study will advance our basic understanding of IL-6-mediated anti-PD-1 resistance, and inform future
combination therapy development in NSCLC for patients that have not responded to anti-PD-1 therapy.

application_id: 11051239
activity_code: K08
title: Targeting the Stroma for Pancreatic Cancer Treatment
org_name: CEDARS-SINAI MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Pancreatic cancer remains a deadly and treatment resistant malignancy in large part due to significant stromal
desmoplasia and an immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment (TME). This proposal seeks to elucidate if
targeting a master regulator of the TME, focal adhesion kinase, can alter the stroma and immunosuppressive
TME in order to sensitize pancreas cancer to immunotherapy. By employing a neoadjuvant translational research
strategy combined with expert mentorship, hands-on training and intensive didactics, these challenges of
pancreatic cancer can be addressed with the ultimate goal of reversing the grim clinical outcomes of patients
with this deadly disease.

application_id: 11109707
activity_code: K08
title: Elucidating the molecular and cellular mechanisms of immune evasion in pancreatic cancer
org_name: DANA-FARBER CANCER INST
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) remains a highly lethal malignancy and immune-based therapies have been largely unsuccessful to date. This proposal utilizes advances in single-cell transcriptomics and highly multiplexed imaging, paired with recently developed preclinical models of neoantigen-expressing pancreatic cancer and co-clinical profiling of patient samples, to elucidate the molecular and cellular mechanisms of immune evasion in pancreatic cancer. I anticipate that this work will nominate additional targets for immune-based therapy in pancreatic cancer and other cancers.

title: Investigating the Effect of Antibody Mediated Feedback on B Cell Clonal Selection
org_name: WEILL MEDICAL COLL OF CORNELL UNIV
phr: Project Narrative The mechanisms by which B cell responses are modulated by serum antibody remain poorly defined. This proposal seeks to (1) gain mechanistic insight into the regulatory role immunoglobulin plays in humoral responses and (2) evaluate the implications of antibody mediated feedback on B cell clonal selection. Completion of this work will provide insight into a mode of regulation of adaptive immunity that is important for improved vaccination design.

title: Engineered microsystems to assess endothelial contribution to myeloproliferative neoplasm thrombosis
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MINNESOTA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Thrombosis is the leading cause of mortality among patients with JAKV617F+ myeloproliferative neoplasms (MPNs). Although JAKV617F+ endothelial cells are activated in MPN, treatments do not target the vasculature. Using endothelialized microfluidics models to assess endothelial function, we will determine whether reducing JAKV617F+-mediated signaling using JAK-STAT and Gas6-Axl-MERTK inhibitors decreases thrombosis in MPN and thereby establish a mechanistic rationale for novel antithrombotic therapies in MPN.

title: Molecular Genetic Mechanisms of Infantile Epilepsies and the Impact of Genetic Diagnosis
org_name: BOSTON CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Infantile epilepsies are common, affecting 1 in 1000 infants, and are associated with significant morbidity,
mortality, healthcare costs, and caregiver burden. Although most infantile epilepsies are believed to have
genetic causes, most infants with epilepsy remain genetically “unsolved” and the full genetic landscape of
infantile epilepsies is unknown, which limits our ability to develop precision therapies and ultimately improve
outcomes for this vulnerable population. This proposal aims to discover new genetic causes of infantile
epilepsies and evaluate the impact of these discoveries on infants with epilepsy and their families, contributing
to knowledge that will inform our scientific understanding of normal and abnormal brain development and guide
clinical care and i...

title: Optimizing implementation of evidence-based mental health interventions to promote reach and retention among migrants in transit in humanitarian emergencies
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Displaced populations experience an elevated burden of mental disorder alongside very limited access to mental health services. As forced displacement continues to rise to unprecedented levels (over 84 million people in 2021), innovative strategies to improve reach and retention in mental health services for hard-to- reach displaced populations, such as migrants in transit, are needed to reduce the global burden of mental disorders. By leveraging participatory methods in implementation science, human-centered design, and systems science, this research aims to discover novel approaches to improving access to care for migrants in transit, which will facilitate innovations that may be translated to improving mental health service delivery in other settings and hard-to-reach ...

application_id: 10862876
activity_code: K08
title: Molecular Imaging of Fibrosis for Improved Treatment Planning of Pancreatic Ductal Adenocarcinoma
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) is an invasive and rapidly progressive malignancy that remains highly lethal despite recent therapeutic advances. There is an urgent and unmet clinical need for more reliable and accurate imaging tools to optimize delineation of the tumor and non-invasive evaluation of response to neoadjuvant chemoradiotherapy. The purpose of this study is to develop an image-guided paradigm using a novel collagen specific PET imaging probe to address these needs with the ultimate goal of improved management and clinical care of PDAC patients.

title: Regulation and targeting of tumor cell states and plasticity in pancreatic cancer
org_name: DANA-FARBER CANCER INST
phr: Project Narrative
Transcriptional cell states are correlated with clinical outcomes and therapeutic sensitivity in pancreatic cancer,
but the mechanisms regulating cell state specification and tumor cell plasticity are not well understood. This
research proposal examines the mechanisms by which TGF-β specifies the pancreatic cancer basal cell state,
investigates how TGF-β-mediated cellular plasticity drives chemo-resistance in organoid models and serially
collected metastatic biopsies analyzed with single-cell RNA-sequencing, and applies pharmacologic and genetic
screening techniques in isogenic state-variant pancreatic cancer organoid models to identify cell state-specific
therapeutic vulnerabilities. These studies will advance our understanding of how tumor cell states and cellular
plast...

application_id: 10901017
activity_code: DP1
title: DEVELOPING GPCR MODULATORS OF SOMATOSTATIN INTERNEURONS FOR THE TREATMENT OF OPIOID USE DISORDER
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: Approved medications and available treatments for opioid use disorder (OUD) do not adequately address persistent symptoms that can lead to relapse; thus, we desperately need to identify new molecular targets for medication development. In this proposal, we will identify targets expressed by a specific subtype of neuron within prefrontal cortex that has been implicated and regulating motivated and affective behaviors. We will use a combination of transcriptomics, electrophysiology, pharmacology, and genetic engineering to define how candidate targets regulate brain function and opioid use behaviors.

application_id: 11143775
activity_code: K08
title: Defining a therapeutic platform for DIPG with mRNA CAR T cells and microglia inhibition
org_name: CHILDREN'S HOSP OF PHILADELPHIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Diffuse intrinsic pontine glioma is a devastating pediatric brain tumor that despite decades of clinical trials
remains fatal with a median survival of less than a year. This proposal focuses on the development of safe and
effective immune-based therapies for these tumors, using chimeric antigen receptor T cell therapy. Through
these studies we hope to improve our understanding of the tumor growth within the immune microenvironment
and translate effective immunotherapies to patients.

application_id: 10915507
activity_code: K08
title: Cancer Cell-Extrinsic and Intrinsic Regulators of Tumorigenesis
org_name: ICAHN SCHOOL OF MEDICINE AT MOUNT SINAI
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  Cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma (cSCC) is the second most common cancer overall in the United States, of which a substantial number of cases are unresectable and lack treatment options. Therapeutic strategies are limited in part due to poor understanding of how intratumoral heterogeneity (ITH) arises and triggers invasive behavior in cSCC, but recent work suggests that factors from the tumor microenvironment (TME) and cancer cell-intrinsic factors both contribute to ITH. This work aims to identify specific crosstalk pathways between the tumor and TME that lead to invasion and tumor progression to aid in developing therapeutic strategies to disable these processes.

application_id: 11128749
activity_code: K08
title: Implementation and Evaluation of a Screening Tool for Sexual Dysfunction for Pediatric Cancer Survivors
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Long term survivors of childhood cancer, of which there are over 400,000 in the United States, have increased
chronic and mental health conditions and reduced health-related quality of life and socioeconomic attainment
than their peers. One such long term effect, sexual dysfunction, is identified by adolescent and young adult-
aged childhood cancer survivors (AYA-CCS) as a bothersome yet unmet need affecting their mental health
and quality of life. Completion of this study will lead to improved screening and diagnosis of sexual dysfunction
among AYA-CCS, which is the first step in improving quality of care for this underrecognized and undertreated
condition.

application_id: 11079434
activity_code: F31
title: Investigating the molecular mechanisms underlying aldosterone-mediated sodium appetite
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF IOWA
phr: Project Narrative
Hypertension and cardiovascular disorders are major contributors to premature death worldwide and their
prevalence and severity are exacerbated by excess salt intake. The steroid hormone aldosterone induces
sodium intake, but we have a poor understanding of how this occurs. The proposed study will investigate the
molecular and genetic changes that occur in specialized aldosterone-sensing neurons after aldosterone
treatment which may allow for the future development of therapies to reduce excess salt intake.

activity_code: T32
title: Eukaryotic Gene Regulation (EGR) Training Program
org_name: PENNSYLVANIA STATE UNIVERSITY, THE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Nearly all aspects of biology and human disease are rooted in gene regulation. Our knowledge and abilities to understand, control, and rectify gene expression and mis-expression is fundamental to basic biological knowledge and medicine. We propose a continuation of our successful predoctoral training program to prepare a cadre of young scientists who excel in cross-disciplinary skills and address fundamental questions related to gene regulation processes and their impact on human health.

title: Skin Cancer Risks and Risk Prediction in Patients with Actinic Keratosis
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF TX MD ANDERSON CAN CTR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Actinic keratoses are common pre-cancerous skin lesions that can transform into cutaneous squamous cell
carcinoma and whose presence may also be associated with increased risk for other types of skin cancer
(melanoma and basal cell carcinoma). In this project, we will determine the absolute risks of skin cancer over
time in patients with actinic keratosis, which are unknown; assess what skin cancer related follow-up care is
currently being provided to patients with actinic keratosis, which is uncharacterized; and develop a skin cancer
risk prediction model for patients with actinic keratosis, which has not previously been done. Completion of this
project will directly inform recommendations for skin cancer surveillance and early detection in patients with
actinic keratosi...

application_id: 11253265
activity_code: K23
title: Short-course ampicillin in preterm neonates to promote antimicrobial stewardship
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE/PUBLIC HEALTH RELEVANCE
Antibiotic exposures in preterm infants are common, but excess exposures can predispose to patient harm.
Rigorous studies that aim to minimize unnecessary antibiotic exposures in this population are needed. This
proposal seeks to minimize unnecessary exposure to ampicillin, the most commonly used antibiotic in preterm
infants, and through the input of patients and families as well as neonatal experts, create a tool that can be
used to study the outcomes of infants in future clinical trials that aim to reduce antibiotic exposures.

title: Understanding Alzheimer disease heterogeneity in Hispanic populations.
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE: Recent epidemiological studies show a rapid increase in Alzheimer's Disease (AD) in Hispanics compared to non-Hispanics whites. However, there is limited understanding about AD determinants in Hispanics and the effect of genes by environment interactions. The proposed application aims to determine the influence of genetics and social disparities of health on age at onset, cognitive profiles, and biomarker trajectories in Hispanic populations.

application_id: 11258518
activity_code: K23
title: Development of a Mobile Health Intervention for Blood Pressure Management in Pregnancy
org_name: UNIV OF MASSACHUSETTS MED SCH WORCESTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The U.S. has the highest rate of maternal mortality among high-income countries and hypertension is a leading
cause, contributing to 40% of maternal deaths. To improve the quality and cultural relevance of hypertension
care, this proposal will examine the effects of the digital health approach Moms@Home on home blood pressure
monitoring in a diverse population of pregnant women with hypertension. By facilitating more timely diagnosis
and potentially better blood pressure control with digital health and home blood pressure monitoring, this
proposal holds promise to reduce the risk of preventable and devastating clinical events for women with high-
risk pregnancies and their children.

application_id: 11171520
activity_code: K08
title: Harnessing treatment-induced tumor evolution and collateral sensitivities using a human rectal cancer co-clinical platform
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: Advanced rectal tumors are treated with combined chemotherapy and radiation therapy (chemoradiation) prior
to surgery, but there is wide variation in the magnitude of tumor response. Optimizing non-surgical
chemoradiation-based treatments could spare some patients the need to undergo a morbid surgery, but will
require a detailed understanding of how chemoradiation causes evolution of tumor subpopulations as well as
the development of unique approaches to therapeutically target these evolving subpopulations. This project will
use cutting-edge techniques to dissect tumor responses to chemoradiation as well as an innovative approach
to identify new treatment strategies for locally advanced rectal cancer.

application_id: 10986101
activity_code: K23
title: Optimizing Anti-mycobacterial Therapy Using A Pharmacology-based Approach
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: Project narrative The proposed research will apply principles of clinical pharmacology in order to better define the therapeutic window and optimal use of three drugs used in the treatment of mycobacterial infections. This project aims to establish the bactericidal kinetics of standard of care for Mycobacterium avium complex lung disease, define the pharmacokinetics (PK)-toxicodynamic (TD) relationships for a novel nitroimidazole antibiotic, and describe the magnitude of drug-drug interaction between first-line tuberculosis treatment and a promising agent for adjunctive host-directed therapy. The knowledge gained by this proposal through these diverse yet complementary aims will strengthen the science of tuberculosis and nontuberculous mycobacteria therapeutics.

application_id: 11141021
activity_code: K08
title: Impact of clonal hematopoiesis mutations on toxicity and outcomes following oncologic therapy
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF TX MD ANDERSON CAN CTR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Clonal hematopoiesis (CH) mutations are acquired mutations in the blood present in the majority of older
individuals that may contribute to worse oncologic outcomes in patients with solid malignancy. In this project
we will use individual genetic and clinical data to determine whether CH mutations and cancer therapies
interact to result in worse cancer treatment toxicity and outcomes and to investigate whether there are clinical
markers to determine which individuals may be most at risk for these adverse outcomes. Completion of this
project will provide key insight regarding the importance of CH mutations in patients with solid malignancy and
guide future investigations regarding whether knowledge of CH mutations in patients with solid malignancy can
improve cancer care.

application_id: 11103324
activity_code: K08
title: Metabolite driven mechanisms by which gut microbes impact checkpoint inhibitor success in non-small cell lung cancer patients
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Despite significant improvement in treatment using immune checkpoint inhibitors, non-small cell lung cancer
remains the leading cause of cancer death, representing 22% of all cancer deaths. The gut microbiome has
been identified as an independent, novel modulator of systemic anti-tumor responses to immunotherapy, but
data remain limited by inconsistencies, lack of longitudinal analysis, and lack of functional characterization. The
current proposal seeks to identify key metabolites and microbes or microbial communities that mechanistically
impact systemic and intra-tumoral immune responses that either enhance or inhibit responses to immune
checkpoint inhibitor therapy.

application_id: 11142617
activity_code: K08
title: Pilot trial testing mobile health psychosocial intervention for Adolescentsand Young Adults with cancer
org_name: SEATTLE CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Cancer among Adolescents and Young Adults (AYAs) is associated with poor psychosocial outcomes and
quality of life, and patients experience disproportionately high levels of anxiety, depression, and posttraumatic
stress in relation to their younger pediatric and older adult counterparts. Findings from the proposed project will
help address the unmet psychosocial needs of AYA cancer patients using a novel mobile health psychosocial
care delivery model with the advantages of appeal, reach, and cost-effectiveness. Ultimately, this work may help
improve patient health-related quality of life and emotional well-being, and reduce the burden of cancer, by
expanding access to evidence-based care.

activity_code: K23
title: Sleep-Wake Disturbance as a Predictor of Delirium and Subsequent Alzheimer's Disease and Related Dementias after Surgical Aortic Valve Replacement
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ROCHESTER
phr: Project Narrative
Delirium is terrifying, costs an estimated $164 billion annually in the US alone and is associated with a greater
risk of death, institutionalization, and Alzheimer’s disease and related dementias (ADRD); ADRD, in turn,
affects more than 5 million Americans and exacts an incalculable human toll. Sleep-wake disturbance (SWD) is
an observable, measurable, and potentially modifiable aspect of both delirium and ADRD. It may offer novel
opportunities to prevent and treat delirium as well as to mitigate the development of subsequent ADRD. This
project aims to examine home-based measures of SWD as physiological markers for delirium risk in aortic
valve surgery patients while serving as a vehicle for career development for the PI to become an independent
physician-scientist in de...

title: Examining the neural substrates of reward in the lateral septum
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The lateral septum (LS) is anatomically situated to control a wide range of motivated behaviors, many of which are dysregulated in psychiatric disorders. application_id: 11184214
activity_code: DP5
title: Cellular phenotypic heterogeneity and resistance to radiotherapy in pancreatic adenocarcinoma
org_name: OHIO STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Cellular phenotypic heterogeneity within tumors, such as subpopulations of cells with increased capacity for
self-renewal referred to as cancer stem cells, exists within many cancers is believed to drive resistance to
therapies such and radiation and chemotherapy. Our preliminary results in pancreatic cancer revealed a
supportive niche of Wnt-secreting cancer cells that drives a Lgr5+ stem-cell-like state in another cancer cell
subpopulation, thereby promoting and maintaining stemness within pancreatic tumors. This proposal will test
the hypothesis that Wnt-driven cellular phenotypic heterogeneity and stemness promote radioresistance in
pancreatic tumors.

application_id: 11138642
activity_code: K08
title: Defining pro-metastatic and endothelial-regulatory roles for LIN28B in hepatocellular carcinoma
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CHICAGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
LIN28B is a pleiotropic RNA-binding oncofetal protein that correlates with poor prognosis in hepatocellular 
carcinoma (HCC). This proposal seeks to understand how LIN28B contributes directly to the metastatic 
phenotype of HCC cells and how it contributes to the angiogenic and endothelial modulatory phenotype of HCC 
cells using a combination of cell, animal, and human specimen studies. I anticipate that these results will provide 
rationale and targets for novel drug and biomarker development for HCC.

application_id: 11143663
activity_code: K08
title: Deciphering non-canonical translation in high risk medulloblastoma
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
This project aims to identify new driver genes and potential therapeutic targets in the Group 3 subtype of
medulloblastoma, which is an aggressive pediatric brain cancer with poor outcomes. This career development
grant will provide the applicant with research training to investigate the non-canonical protein, ASNSD1 uORF,
as a novel Group 3 medulloblastoma driver gene. The information gained by this project will enable future study
of ASNSD1 uORF as a potential therapeutic target in Group 3 medulloblastoma and demonstrate the importance
of non-canonical proteins as disease driver genes.

title: cGAS-STING and therapeutic immune responses in neuroblastoma
org_name: CHILDREN'S HOSP OF PHILADELPHIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Despite intensive therapy, patients with high-risk neuroblastoma frequently suffer from relapses that prove
fatal. To prevent and treat relapse, we need a better understanding of how to target the populations of cancer
cells that survive our current therapies. This proposal aims to target drug-resistant cancer cells by exploiting
differences in how they sense inflammatory stimuli in order to enhance their susceptibility to immunotherapy.

title: Artificial Intelligence-Assisted Clinical Decision Support for Preventing Hypoglycemia in Hospitalized Patients
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
For the 1 in 5 patients hospitalized with diabetes, hypoglycemia, the most common adverse side effect of
insulin, can be life-threatening. This research expands upon previous work to use artificial intelligence to
predict and prevent hypoglycemia. The goal is to develop a clinical decision support tool for use in the
electronic health record that increases the safety of care for patients with diabetes.

application_id: 10751053
activity_code: K23
title: Optimizing Anti-mycobacterial Therapy Using A Pharmacology-based Approach
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: Project narrative The proposed research will apply principles of clinical pharmacology in order to better define the therapeutic window and optimal use of three drugs used in the treatment of mycobacterial infections. This project aims to establish the bactericidal kinetics of standard of care for Mycobacterium avium complex lung disease, define the pharmacokinetics (PK)-toxicodynamic (TD) relationships for a novel nitroimidazole antibiotic, and describe the magnitude of drug-drug interaction between first-line tuberculosis treatment and a promising agent for adjunctive host-directed therapy. The knowledge gained by this proposal through these diverse yet complementary aims will strengthen the science of tuberculosis and nontuberculous mycobacteria therapeutics.

application_id: 11144294
activity_code: K08
title: Defining Pre-treatment Correlates of Patient GD2 CAR T Cell Exhaustion and Memory Using Multi-Dimensional Immune Profiling
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
While chimeric antigen receptor T cell (CAR-T) therapies have revolutionized treatment options in a variety of
pediatric cancers, CAR-Ts have provided little benefit to metastatic or relapsed solid tumor patients, in part
because of inadequate CAR-T expansion. To understand the potential causes of poor CAR-T expansion, this
research proposal will evaluate the effect of costimulatory domain and pre-apheresis chemotherapy on solid
tumor patient CAR-T expansion and efficacy. Results will identify predictors or drivers of CAR-T efficacy and
provide a platform to improve future CAR-T treatment options for children with solid tumors.

title: Semi-automated bladder cancer screening using machine learning: clinical validation and implementation.
org_name: DARTMOUTH-HITCHCOCK CLINIC
phr: Project Narrative: Bladder cancer screening by cytopathologists is difficult and prone to high interobserver variability, and to combat this I developed the AutoParis machine learning algorithm as a quantitative diagnostic aid to help the cytopathologist render the correct diagnosis according to the gold standard Paris System for Urine Cytology. But before AutoParis is ready for clinical deployment it must: have its training set expanded, be retrained in a more flexible framework (Pytorch), have a graphical user interface, and be proven non-inferior / superior to standard cytological bladder cancer screening in a head-to-head comparison. This career development award will enable me to gain the necessary computer programming skills so I can advance the AutoParis system to the clinical deplo...

activity_code: K08
title: Using natural killer cells to prevent breast cancer metastases
org_name: UT SOUTHWESTERN MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Project Narrative
I discovered that invasive breast cancer cells can reprogram natural killer (NK) cells into being metastasis-
promoters. I propose to determine the mechanisms driving metastases-reprogrammed NK cells and to
determine its significance in human breast cancer. NK cell directed therapies could provide alternative
immunotherapies for metastatic breast cancer patients and I will complete key preclinical experiments needed
to translate these insights to patient benefit.

application_id: 10904630
activity_code: K08
title: The role of stem-like CD8 T-cells in the abscopal response
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Combined radiation and checkpoint treatment has shown clinical potential for advanced malignancies including melanoma, however, responses outside the radiation field (abscopal effect) still occur in only a minority of patients. This proposal may provide important insight into a novel mechanism for anti-cancer T cell stimulation which is less toxic and has higher local and distant response rates than current approaches. In addition, it may lead to a re-evaluation of the need for and benefit of surgical removal of the tumor-draining lymph nodes.

title: Northwestern University Infectious Diseases Education and Science (NuIDeas)
org_name: NORTHWESTERN UNIVERSITY AT CHICAGO
phr: Project Narrative
The continued emergence of new pathogens such as SARS-CoV-2, the unacceptable morbidity and mortality
due to conventional infections, and the epidemics of antibiotic-resistant bacteria highlight the need for well-
trained researchers in infectious diseases. This postgraduate training program in infectious diseases will
provide training for investigators capable of harnessing the discoveries in the basic sciences of microbiology
and immunology to develop novel interventions aimed at ameliorating the burden of infectious diseases.

title: Addressing Colorectal Cancer-Related Distress: Developing and Evaluating a Couple-Based Mindfulness Intervention
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Coping with metastatic colorectal cancer (mCRC) is a highly distressing experience for patients as well as their partners. Clinical interventions that offer brief, accessible, and effective coping tools to both members of the couple may significantly improve the quality of life of both patient and partner, thus addressing an important gap in current clinical care. The proposed research offers to fill this gap by developing and piloting a brief, couple- based mindfulness intervention aimed to reduce distress and improve the quality of life of patients with mCRC as well as their partners.
