title: Targeting the immunologic vulnerabilities of small cell lung carcinoma
org_name: BRIGHAM AND WOMEN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Small cell lung cancer (SCLC) remains relatively refractory to multiple therapeutic modalities, including immune checkpoint blockade, despite its high tumor mutation burden. This proposal seeks to exploit newfound immunologic vulnerabilities of SCLC conferred by epigenetic control of MHC Class I antigen presentation. Rational targeting of distinct immunologic subtypes of SCLC could lead to a deeper understanding of epigenetic modulation of immunogenicity, and, ultimately, novel immunotherapies for this treatment-refractory tumor.

title: Elucidating the role of tumor cell-intrinsic hypoxia inducible factor (HIF)-1α and HIF-2α pathway activation in tumor immune evasion
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CHICAGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Cancer remains the second leading cause of mortality in the United States. title: The FYI on MRI: A Multilevel Decision Support Intervention for Screening Breast MRI
org_name: GEORGETOWN UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Women at high risk for breast cancer can decide to supplement breast cancer screening with MRI, but lack of

application_id: 11087566
activity_code: K08
title: Therapeutic targeting of RTK protein granules in cancer
org_name: SLOAN-KETTERING INST CAN RESEARCH
phr: Project Narrative
Gene rearrangements involving receptor tyrosine kinases (RTKs) represent a frequent and diverse set of
oncogenic driver alterations across human cancer, and resistance to tyrosine kinase inhibitor (TKI) therapies
are inevitable for most patients with advanced cancers and the major cause of mortality. We recently
discovered that notable RTK fusion oncoproteins form their own pathogenic subcellular compartment,
membraneless cytoplasmic RTK protein granules, to coordinate oncogenic RTK and RAS signaling. This
proposal will test therapeutic approaches that exploit the unique signaling properties of this cancer-specific
biomolecular condensate.

title: Elucidating cancer-intrinsic mechanisms of perineural invasion in pancreatic cancer
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Emerging evidence indicates that the active recruitment of nerve fibers into tumors and subsequent cancer cell
invasion (perineural invasion) plays an important role in cancer development, metastasis, therapeutic resistance,
recurrence, pain, and death across many malignancies including pancreatic cancer, but the diverse molecular
mechanisms underlying this tumor-nerve dialogue remain largely unknown. To address this gap in knowledge,
we will comprehensively identify cell-type specific genes linked to perineural invasion in human pancreatic cancer
and then investigate their functional roles in a novel genetically-engineered mouse model. The proposed studies
will provide a high-resolution roadmap of critical gene expression programs and intercellular interactions in the
pa...

application_id: 11324525
activity_code: K23
title: Developing a modified brief alcohol-focused intervention tailored for patients with alcohol use disorder in opioid agonist treatment
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF NOTRE DAME
phr: 8. Project Narrative
Alcohol use increases the risk for treatment dropout, relapse, and overdose in patients receiving opioid agonist
treatment for opioid use. This project will develop and evaluate a modified alcohol-focused intervention tailored
to address alcohol use in patients in opioid agonist treatment who are struggling with alcohol use disorder. This
brief and scalable tailored intervention will have the potential to decrease alcohol use and improve opioid
agonist treatment outcomes with minimal resources, adding in an innovative way to efforts to address the
ongoing opioid overdose crisis in the United States.

application_id: 10806201
activity_code: K99
title: NET stabilization: mechanistic and therapeutic studies in thromboinflammatory disoders
org_name: CHILDREN'S HOSP OF PHILADELPHIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Neutrophils, a class of white blood cells that combat bacteria, can become overactive when fighting overwhelming infection in a condition called sepsis, releasing anti-bacteria compounds that contribute to life- threatening organ damage. I propose a new therapy that neutralizes these dangerous compounds without interfering with neutrophils’ ability to control infections. My goal is to further understand how this strategy works and to bring it to patient care to improve survival in sepsis and other conditions complicated by neutrophil-related inflammation, such as sickle cell disease.

phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The management of pediatric acute respiratory illness (ARI) in resource-constrained settings in sub-Saharan
Africa is almost entirely based on clinical symptoms, the non-specific nature of which contributes to both over-

little data from sub-Saharan Africa where the burden of ARI is high and co-morbidities that may affect the
immune response like malaria and malnutrition are more common. This study seeks to develop and internally
validate novel clinical algorithms for pediatric ARI that leverage existing host immune response classifiers for

application_id: 10857301
activity_code: K23
title: Infant Protection from Influenza through Maternal Influenza Immunization
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: NARRATIVE Infants are the highest risk pediatric group for severe morbidity and mortality related to influenza and require unique interventions to protect them. Maternal influenza immunization reduces infections in these children, but research is needed to better understand how maternal vaccine protects infants and how it may be optimized to reduce infant infections. By comprehensively studying the role of maternal influenza immunization in the protection of infants against influenza, this project will directly inform efforts to improve delivery of influenza vaccine to pregnant women and influenza-related outcomes for infants.

application_id: 10873065
activity_code: K23
title: Infant Peanut Allergy Prevention: Understanding and Supporting Caregivers to Achieve Adherence
org_name: LURIE CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL OF CHICAGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The Prevention of Peanut Allergy (PPA) Guidelines offer a rare opportunity to prevent a chronic and life- threatening condition. As caregivers are the ultimate drivers of the PPA guideline implementation, furthering our understanding of caregiver needs and addressing them with novel interventions that support caregivers is needed. Completion of these studies will augment and strengthen our current food allergy prevention approach.

activity_code: K23
title: Cytomegalovirus-specific Immune Reconstitution after Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation in the Era of Modern Antiviral Prophylaxis
org_name: BECKMAN RESEARCH INSTITUTE/CITY OF HOPE
phr: Project Narrative
Cytomegalovirus (CMV) infection causes an increased risk of complications and death after hematopoietic cell
transplantation. Recent studies suggest that new medications for the prevention of graft-versus-host disease
(GVHD) after transplant may promote the development of immunity against CMV; however, it is unclear whether
these new GVHD preventative drugs can promote CMV immunity in the era of modern CMV prevention. This
proposal will seek to answer this question, using cutting edge technology and analytical methods, in order to
inform management of CMV in the context of new and future cancer treatments.

that pet ownership and close contact with pets are protective against colonization with C. difficile and

colonization resistance against C. difficile are shared between patients and their pets and that pet contact can
mitigate antimicrobial-associated gut dysbiosis and the risk of C. difficile infection. This study will further define
epidemiologic and pathophysiologic characteristics of C. difficile infection and gut microbiome dysbiosis that...

application_id: 10908542
activity_code: K23
title: SLE-AWARE: SLE-- A Window into APOL1 Regulation andExpression
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MARYLAND BALTIMORE
phr: NARRATIVE
Up to 60% of African Americans, who are at heightened risk for kidney and cardiovascular disease (CVD), carry
at least one Apolipoprotein L1 (APOL1) coding change variant allele. These variants associate with adverse
renal and CVD outcomes—particularly in chronic inflammatory disease such as SLE. By leveraging SLE
monocyte transcriptional profiles validated by primary cell culture models, this study will test the overall
hypothesis that immune activation and resultant increased APOL1 expression synergizes with APOL1 genotype
to precipitate human disease.

application_id: 10898755
activity_code: K23
title: Precision characterization of antimicrobial resistance gene dynamics in bloodstream infection risk after hematopoietic stem cell transplantation
org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE In patients undergoing hematopoietic stem cell transplantation, systemic infections with antibiotic resistant organisms often result from resident gut bacteria and are associated with an extremely high mortality rate. Despite the significant burden of multidrug-resistant infections, knowledge of how antibiotic resistance is acquired or disseminated in gut bacteria and contributes to the development of systemic infections is limited. This study will use novel next- generation sequencing of the gut flora to understand the antimicrobial resistance gene dynamics that may increase the risk of bloodstream infections in patients with cancer.

application_id: 10865000
activity_code: K23
title: Immune profiling to stratify Clostridioides difficile infection outcomes
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF VIRGINIA
phr: C. difficile is the leading cause of healthcare-associated infection, yet no single test or validated model is capable of reliably telling which patients are at risk of dying from the infection. New research suggests that a patient's immune system, and possibly other factors such as Binary Toxin, may be the key to understanding how they will respond to C. difficile. This work will combine known risk factors with new markers from the blood and stool into an integrated scoring system that predicts which patients will develop severe or life-threatening C. difficile infection.

application_id: 11231672
activity_code: K23
title: Community-onset urinary tract infections caused by extended-spectrum beta-lactamase-producing Escherichia coli in women of diverse backgrounds
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Community-onset antimicrobial resistant (AMR) urinary tract infections (UTI) are a growing public health threat,
particularly for women. The objective of this proposal is to identify individual- and neighborhood-level risk
factors, predominant genotypes, and complications for UTI caused by extended-spectrum beta-lactamase-
producing Escherichia coli (E. coli) (ESBL-E. coli), as well as temporal and spatial clusters of ESBL-E. coli.
This approach requires the application of a combination of social, spatial, and molecular epidemiology methods
which can be applied to other AMR infections.

application_id: 11164485
activity_code: K08
title: Pathogenic role of human papillomavirus (HPV) DNA Integration and clonal expansion in cervical tumorigenesis
org_name: ALBERT EINSTEIN COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
I propose that HPV DNA integration into the human genome promotes is a crucial early event in cervical
dysplasia and cancer that promotes the emergence of expanded clones of altered cells. The proposed
project will directly analyze early steps in proliferation and clonal evolution of keratinocytes harboring
HPV DNA integrations in both patient samples and cell cultures. It will formally test the hypothesis that
HPV DNA insertion acts by stabilizing expression of viral oncogenes and by activating human cancer
genes that flank sites of viral DNA integration in the human genome.

application_id: 11074668
activity_code: K08
title: Contribution of myeloid cells to the anti-tumor adaptive immune response after radiation in a pediatric brain tumor
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: Project narrative
Brain tumor is the leading cause of death by cancer in the pediatric population; thus there is a great need for
novel therapies. Radiation is an important part of therapy for many high-grade tumors including medulloblastoma,
the most common malignant pediatric brain tumor, but it is rarely curative and comes with significant side effects.
This project aims to identify reversible causes of immune suppression in medulloblastomas treated with radiation
so that we can leverage the benefits of immunotherapy to achieve long-term remission for patients with
treatment-resistant tumors.

application_id: 10995045
activity_code: K08
title: Elucidating Critical Dependencies Underlying Therapeutic Evasion in Philadelphia Chromosome-like Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Relapsed acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL) is a leading cause of cancer death in children/adolescents, and Philadelphia chromosome-like ALL is a common, high-risk subtype characterized by miswired signaling, high relapse rates, and extremely poor survival. Our research uses systems biology approaches to identify how changes in multiple pathways at the single-cell level may lead to leukemia resistance and relapse. Through these studies, we hope to improve our understanding of the critical biologic mechanisms driving this high-risk leukemia and to develop more effective combination precision medicine therapies to cure patients.

application_id: 11097158
activity_code: K23
title: Arbovirus Prediction and Mitigation in the Indo-Pacific
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative:
Revitalizing Informal Settlements and their Environments (RISE) is a cluster randomized control trial of a water
infrastructure intervention which is currently underway in 24 urban slum communities in Indonesia and Fiji, two
countries at high risk for arbovirus transmission and climate change. In my K32 project, I will leverage the RISE
platform to evaluate risk factors for dengue and chikungunya infections, test the impact of this intervention on
transmission, and create a mathematical model to investigate transmission patterns under a range of climate
and intervention scenarios. These studies will lay the groundwork for developing future arbovirus prediction
and adaptation strategies and launch my career in emerging infectious disease epidemiology.

application_id: 11112298
activity_code: K08
title: Developing a Values Elicitation Tool to Improve Treatment Decision-Making in Acute Myeloid Leukemia
org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Significant growth in treatment options for patients with acute myeloid leukemia (AML) has increased the
importance of understanding patients’ values and preferences to determine appropriate therapy. This project
aims to evaluate the feasibility of implementing a novel patient decision support tool to improve treatment
decision-making for older adults with AML. This tool uses best-worst scaling, an innovative, yet robust method
to elicit patient values, which may be widely applicable to other treatment settings across oncology.

application_id: 10907531
activity_code: F32
title: Developing novel neural network tools for accurate and interpretable dynamical modeling of neural circuits
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: Recent advances in dynamical systems theory suggests that the best way to understand the mechanism of neural computation may not lie at the level of individual neurons, but instead through the dynamics of neural populations. To leverage this new perspective, we need tools that can model the dynamics of neural circuits, but the best models currently available have significant limitations that make their dynamical estimates inaccurate, and therefore poorly suited to give interpretable insight into neural computation. I propose a novel neural network architecture that could solve this problem, allowing computational models to learn accurate dynamics from neural data arising from single or multiple interacting neural circuits and helping us infer how neural computations are built by population...

title: Targeting myeloid cells to increase efficacy of immunotherapy against brain tumors.
org_name: CASE WESTERN RESERVE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Glioblastoma is a universally lethal cancer that is currently refractory to immunotherapies that have proven to be breakthrough treatments for other cancers. title: Understanding the Influence of Hormone Therapy on Breast Cancer Risk and Screening through Mixed Methods
org_name: MEDICAL COLLEGE OF WISCONSIN
phr: This application addresses a critical gap in the literature by providing the necessary foundational data to extend our knowledge of breast cancer (BC) risk among individuals who take hormone therapy (HT) and begin to inform and possibly expand BC screening guidelines for these individuals. This project will also extend the applicant’s knowledge, skills, and experience to support a successful career as a surgeon–scientist. Next steps include an R01 application to develop a multi-institutional longitudinal BC screening program for individuals on HT to definitively establish BC risk, provide evidence-based BC screening guidelines, and optimize BC screening access and delivery for people who have taken or currently take HT.

application_id: 11123450
activity_code: K08
title: Improving the activity of CAR T cells for acute myeloid leukemia
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Acute myeloid leukemia (AML) has thus far proven resistant to recent advances in cancer immunotherapy,
such as bispecific antibodies and chimeric antigen receptor (CAR) T cells. This proposal will investigate how
targeting normal myeloid cells can have detrimental effects on CAR T cell activity. These results will help
determine why CAR T cells have not been effective against AML, and identify methods to overcome these
limitations and improve the success of this therapy.

application_id: 10912792
activity_code: K08
title: Differential Wnt Dependencies in Colon Epithelium.
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative Colonic neoplasia, from adenoma to carcinoma exhibits “niche independence” where normal regulatory mechanisms involving Wnt signaling for cell proliferation are unchecked, resulting in tumors. Strategies to inhibit such signaling have not worked therapeutically, however, increasing Wnt signaling even further in tumors has a non-intuitive result where they end up ultimately inhibited. In contrast, normal colonic epithelium has improved growth with increased Wnt signaling. This proposal seeks to leverage this phenomenon to inhibit colon cancer but improve normal colon cell function, which may unlock a new paradigm of colon cancer treatment.

application_id: 11132954
activity_code: K08
title: Investigating SLC46A3 as a negative regulator of nanoparticle drug delivery
org_name: SEATTLE CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Nanoparticle-based therapies have many potential advantages for treating cancer, but there are currently no
clinical biomarkers to predict which patients are most likely to benefit from nanotherapies. This proposal seeks
to validate the solute carrier SLC46A3 as a regulator of phospholipid metabolism in cancer cells and the first
predictive biomarker for liposomal nanoparticle delivery.

application_id: 10880631
activity_code: K08
title: Characterizing the immune infiltrate in muscle-invasive urothelial carcinoma
org_name: OHIO STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Urothelial carcinoma is understudied despite being the 6th most common cancer in the US. Outcomes for localized, muscle-invasive urothelial carcinoma are unsatisfactory with the current standard of care of neoadjuvant chemotherapy followed by radical cystectomy. This project will characterize the immune infiltrate before and after neoadjuvant therapy and will develop predictive biomarkers to ultimately design novel, more effective, personalized regimens to improve survival.

application_id: 10806442
activity_code: K08
title: Elucidating the role of IRF4 in reprogramming the tumor microenvironment in follicular lymphoma
org_name: MAYO CLINIC ROCHESTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE A substantial subset of follicular lymphoma (FL) patients has an early relapse and a poor clinical outcome; however, little is known of the molecular mechanisms responsible for these aggressive features. We have demonstrated that the tumor microenvironment (TME) plays a key role in treatment failure and found that FLs with increased expression of IRF4 have dysregulated immune signaling, a suppressive TME, and a poor prognosis. In this project, we aim to elucidate the mechanisms by which IRF4 controls tumor immunity and define the concordant immunophenotypic changes, ultimately paving the way for novel precision therapies for this unfavorable patient population.

application_id: 10877730
activity_code: K08
title: Improving Diagnosis in Gastrointestinal Cancer: Integrating Prediction Models into Routine Clinical Care
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Iron deficiency anemia (IDA) is a marker of various clinical conditions, including serious diagnoses such as gastrointestinal (GI) tract cancers, but it is not always identified by clinicians. Clinical prediction models could help clinicians to improve the diagnosis of GI tract cancer sooner using electronic health record (EHR) data, but clinical prediction models are not routinely used. This proposal will refine a prediction model for GI tract cancers, identify barriers to using the model, and design a strategy to help encourage clinicians to use the model.

title: Assessment of a Radiomics-Based Computer-Aided Diagnosis Tool for Cancer Risk Stratification of Pulmonary Nodules
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Pulmonary nodules (PNs) are detected via computed tomography (CT) in approximately 2 million adults in the U.S. each year, but the current diagnostic process for PN evaluation and management results in delayed cancer diagnoses and unnecessary invasive biopsy procedures among individuals with benign diagnoses. Existing clinical malignancy risk prediction models provide only modest diagnostic accuracy, but radiomics- based computer-aided diagnosis (CAD) tools, which synthesize quantitative features from CT imaging data to predict cancer risk, may help clinicians estimate PN malignancy risk more accurately. This proposal will evaluate the clinical utility, clinical effectiveness, and cost-effectiveness of a CAD-based risk stratification strategy for PN evaluation and managem...

application_id: 10757410
activity_code: R25
title: The Mid-Atlantic Neuroscience Diversity Scholars (MINDS) Program
org_name: TEMPLE UNIV OF THE COMMONWEALTH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE While the US adult population is approximately 61.3% non-Hispanic white, 17.8% Hispanic or Latino, 13.3% African American, 5.7% Asian, and 1.5% American Indian, Alaskan Native, or Pacific Islander, the picture in academia is much different, with over 85% of full-time faculty identifying as non-Hispanic white or Asian. This loss of diversity is limiting our potential as a nation to build knowledge and improve human health. Our Mid- Atlantic Neuroscience Diversity Scholars (MiNDS) program is a five-year plan to bolster the number of underrepresented minority (URM) students within the neuroscience academic pipeline and build a foundation for URM students to succeed in graduate school and beyond.

application_id: 11099673
activity_code: K08
title: Stress-induced immunosuppression via downregulation of interferon in triple negative breast cancer
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Breast cancer is the second leading cause of cancer death among women in the United States, with triple
negative breast cancer (TNBC) being the most aggressive and deadly form, and the only breast cancer subtype
where immunotherapy is approved. Psychosocial stress, experienced by a large majority of breast cancer
patients, remains an understudied and underappreciated contributor to the development of aggressive disease
and resistance to treatments, including immunotherapy. The proposed work will provide insights into a novel
mechanism of psychosocial stress-induced immunosuppression and uncover promising approaches that could
be translated for therapeutic benefit for patients with TNBC.

application_id: 10887066
activity_code: K08
title: Epigenetic regulators define a dynamic chromatin landscape in NUP98-rearranged leukemia
org_name: DANA-FARBER CANCER INST
phr: Project Narrative Acute myeloid leukemia (AML) driven by oncogenic fusion proteins involving the Nucleoporin 98 (NUP98) gene make up the highest percentage of pediatric patients with relapsed and incurable disease. I recently reported that inhibiting the Menin-MLL1 chromatin complex in NUP98-fusion AML can halt self-renewal and induce myeloid differentiation through transcriptional reprogramming. The goals of this proposal are to define the mechanism by which transcription of pro-leukemogenic genes is maintained by NUP98-fusion proteins and how these loci are epigenetically silenced upon degradation or displacement of NUP98-fusion proteins from chromatin.

activity_code: K08
title: Epigenetic regulators define a dynamic chromatin landscape in NUP98-rearranged leukemia
org_name: DANA-FARBER CANCER INST
phr: Project Narrative
Acute myeloid leukemia (AML) driven by oncogenic fusion proteins involving the Nucleoporin 98 (NUP98) gene
make up the highest percentage of pediatric patients with relapsed and incurable disease. I recently reported
that inhibiting the Menin-MLL1 chromatin complex in NUP98-fusion AML can halt self-renewal and induce
myeloid differentiation through transcriptional reprogramming. The goals of this proposal are to define the
mechanism by which transcription of pro-leukemogenic genes is maintained by NUP98-fusion proteins and how
these loci are epigenetically silenced upon degradation or displacement of NUP98-fusion proteins from
chromatin.

application_id: 11158647
activity_code: K08
title: Microbial Dysbiosis in Pancreatic Cancer Initiation and Progression
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: Project Narrative
The tumor-associated microbiome regulates pancreatic cancer progression but the exact
mechanisms via which bacteria regulate tumorigenesis is still unknown. The proposed work will
(1) determine the influence of tumor-associated bacteria on pancreatic cancer cells, (2) determine
their ability to activate and differentiate pancreatic stromal cells, and (3) characterize the
mechanisms of pathogenicity unique to pancreatic cancer-associated bacteria. Knowledge
gained from our proposed experiments will provide insight into therapeutic strategies for gut
microbial modulation in treating pancreatic cancer.

application_id: 10985814
activity_code: K08
title: Tumor Microenvironment Determinants of TKI Response in Oncogene-Driven Lung Cancer
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: Patients with ALK+ lung cancer experience variable levels of tumor control when treated with tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKI), and our data suggest neutrophils within the tumor microenvironment can reduce patient response to TKI. Using immunocompetent mouse models and patient specimens, this proposal will determine how neutrophils dampen TKI response. These insights will support future clinical trial approaches with novel immunotherapies not yet considered in patients with ALK+ NSCLC.

application_id: 11053550
activity_code: K08
title: Defining the role of GNAS in gastrointestinal metastasis
org_name: SLOAN-KETTERING INST CAN RESEARCH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Patients with gastrointestinal (GI) cancers that metastasize to the peritoneal cavity suffer high morbidity and
mortality and have few effective treatment options. Mutations in the GNAS gene are associated with higher rates
of peritoneal metastasis, as well as decreased responses to chemotherapy in many GI cancers. The study will
investigate the impact of GNAS mutations on tumor molecular signaling cascades and tumor progression,
improve fundamental knowledge of tumor peritoneal metastasis, and evaluate GNAS inhibition as a new
promising treatment strategy for the large number of patients with metastatic GI cancers.

application_id: 11003781
activity_code: R25
title: Research Immersion in Brain, Spinal cord (RIBS), & Peripheral nerves for URM High School Students
org_name: CASE WESTERN RESERVE UNIVERSITY
phr: PI: Neena Singh PROJECT NARRATIVE The Research Immersion in Brain, Spinal cord (RIBS), and peripheral nerve disorders Program is designed to educate and immerse high school students from under-represented minority (URM) schools in a 8-week hands-on research experience in active laboratories on CWRU campus. The goal is to increase awareness of common neurological disorders, and build the confidence of young students to pursue careers in Neurological Research and Health care. Long-term student-mentor relationships will be an added benefit.

application_id: 10759441
activity_code: K23
title: Impact of intestinal helminth infection on HPV co-infection and the cervico-vaginal environment.
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: Project Narrative Cervical cancer continues to cause disability and death in millions of women living in tropical regions of the world who do not have access to cervical cancer screening and human papilloma virus (HPV) vaccination. People living in tropical countries are commonly infected with intestinal worms (helminths), which can live largely undetected in their hosts by muting the immune response. Understanding the impact of helminth infection on the cervico-vaginal microenvironment may lead to the development of improved cervical cancer prevention strategies in tropical countries.

activity_code: K08
title: Biomarker-Driven Strategies to Overcome Immunotherapy Resistance in STK11-Deficient NSCLC
org_name: OHIO STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: NARRATIVE
Loss-of-function alterations in STK11 have been shown to confer poor prognosis and resistance to immune
checkpoint blockade (ICB) in NSCLC through incompletely understood mechanisms of tumor intrinsic resistance
and immunosuppressive features of the tumor microenvironment (TME). Here, we propose investigation of tumor
differentiation phenotypes as a key determinant of immune resistance, and parallel evaluation of TRAF2/cIAP1
as a critical and targetable resistance mechanism that appears to be influenced by differentiation state. Our work
will combine in vitro mechanistic studies, in vivo and patient level studies of TME and ICB response, and
development of differentiation-based biomarker that can accurately detect these phenotypes in clinical samples.
Our results will establish T...

application_id: 10871886
activity_code: K08
title: Decoding the epigenetic landscape that delineates T cell homeostatic proliferation from uncontrolled growth”
org_name: ST. JUDE CHILDREN'S RESEARCH HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The mentored research proposed here will investigate the role of clonal hematopoiesis (CH)-associated genes in driving T cell proliferation. Specifically, we will look at disruption of three main regulators of CH (DNMT3A, TET2, ASXL1) to determine the specific epigenetic programs that delineate T cell homeostasis from a hyperproliferative state. These results will inform on the underlying programs that sustain T cell proliferative capacity and guide future efforts for the design of CAR T cells.

application_id: 10893577
activity_code: K08
title: Microbial Dysbiosis in Pancreatic Cancer Initiation and Progression
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: Project Narrative The tumor-associated microbiome regulates pancreatic cancer progression but the exact mechanisms via which bacteria regulate tumorigenesis is still unknown. The proposed work will (1) determine the influence of tumor-associated bacteria on pancreatic cancer cells, (2) determine their ability to activate and differentiate pancreatic stromal cells, and (3) characterize the mechanisms of pathogenicity unique to pancreatic cancer-associated bacteria. Knowledge gained from our proposed experiments will provide insight into therapeutic strategies for gut microbial modulation in treating pancreatic cancer.

application_id: 11146623
activity_code: K08
title: Tumor Microenvironment Determinants of TKI Response in Oncogene-Driven Lung Cancer
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: Patients with ALK+ lung cancer experience variable levels of tumor control when treated with
tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKI), and our data suggest neutrophils within the tumor
microenvironment can reduce patient response to TKI. Using immunocompetent mouse models
and patient specimens, this proposal will determine how neutrophils dampen TKI response.
These insights will support future clinical trial approaches with novel immunotherapies not yet
considered in patients with ALK+ NSCLC.

application_id: 11173693
activity_code: K08
title: Tumor-Immune Interactions and Cisplatin Resistance in Bladder Cancer
org_name: BRIGHAM AND WOMEN'S HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative
The proposed research is relevant to public health because it focuses on developing new
combination strategies to treating bladder cancer which a common and lethal disease. This project will
dissect the mechanisms by which tumor-associated macrophages drive resistance to current systemic
therapies and will investigate the mechanisms of tumor-macrophage crosstalk that prevent
immunogenic cancer cell death with the ultimate goal of developing more effective macrophage-
directed strategies. Thus, the proposed research is relevant to the part of the NIH’s mission to
lengthening life.

application_id: 11146522
activity_code: K08
title: Elucidating mechanisms of checkpoint inhibitor-induced diabetes
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative:
Therapeutic agents targeting checkpoint inhibitors have transformed cancer treatment by enhancing anti-tumor
immune responses but can also cause a breakdown in self-tolerance resulting in autoimmune complications
affecting most organs. We showed that changes in immune cells and their inflammatory mediators modify
pancreatic β cells and cause diabetes following checkpoint inhibitor immunotherapy. The proposed studies,
using checkpoint inhibitor-induced diabetes as a model, will enhance our understanding of immune-target cell
interactions and the impact of tumor on development of immune related adverse events, which could
potentially lead to identification of biomarkers and therapeutic targets of these events that do not impact tumor
responses.

application_id: 10907688
activity_code: K23
title: Sleep Management And Recovery after Traumatic Brain Injury in Kids (SMART-Kids): Evidence for targeting sleep to improve outcomes
org_name: OREGON HEALTH & SCIENCE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Sleep wake disturbances occur in over 50% of the 50,000 children surviving traumatic brain injury (TBI) hospitalization each year and lead to poor quality of life, though few interventions exist to treat this important morbidity. Children with TBI also suffer cognitive impairments that could be compounded by poor sleep, particularly with respect to executive functions like memory and attention that are vital for academic achievement and optimal development. This proposal will determine the relationship between sleep wake disturbances and important executive functions after TBI, as well as test an early sleep management intervention as a target to improve outcomes in these vulnerable children.

application_id: 11134692
activity_code: K08
title: Leveraging Health Systems to Increase Implementation of Evidence-based Surgical Cancer Care
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ALABAMA AT BIRMINGHAM
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Most patients with common cancers in the United States receive surgical care within health systems, but
quality is inconsistent across system hub and spoke sites. This project will evaluate the effect of treatment in a
health system hub or spoke on receipt of evidence-based surgical cancer care and will identify health system
characteristics and strategies that are associated with system performance and consistency across sites. The
product, an intervention map for system-level implementation of oncologic evidence, can be used by health
systems to provide consistent, high-quality care across sites, increasing equity in cancer care delivery.

application_id: 11017711
activity_code: K23
title: Deciphering the roles of eosinophils and T lymphocytes in EGID
org_name: NORTHWESTERN UNIVERSITY AT CHICAGO
phr: NARRATIVE Eosinophilic gastrointestinal diseases (EGIDs) of the upper GI tract are predominantly food-triggered chronic eosinophilic disorders with significant impact on quality of life and predictive, non-invasive diagnostic testing and treatments are sorely lacking. This proposal aims to expand and deepen our knowledge of two types of immune cells, namely eosinophils and specialized T cells, in EGID pathogenesis in a cohort of well- characterized EGID patients. The knowledge gained will ultimately impact patient care by improving diagnostics, providing biomarkers for monitoring disease activity and identifying potential targets for novel therapeutics.

title: Identification of antimicrobial specialized metabolites from the human oral microbiome to target multidrug-resistant pathogens
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WISCONSIN-MADISON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Multidrug-resistant (MDR) pathogens result in over 2.8 million potentially life-threatening infections in the United States annually. This proposal aims to establish how the microbes naturally associated with our bodies (i.e. our microbiome) protect against difficult-to-treat MDR pathogens. From this work, we will gain a clearer understanding of the factors that render the oral microbiome vulnerable to invading pathogens and will identify promising lead molecules for antibiotic drug development.

phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE

distress, pain) faced by hematologic cancer patients. A pilot randomized controlled trial will assess the feasibility,

application_id: 10886916
activity_code: K08
title: Molecular Dynamics of Glioma Formation and DNA Damage Response by Mutant IDH1
org_name: DANA-FARBER CANCER INST
phr: Project Narrative: Gliomas are the most common primary brain tumors in adults, a subset of which harbor mutations in the metabolic gene isocitrate dehydrogenase 1 (IDH1). My project uses a novel genetically engineered mouse model of mutant IDH1-driven astrocytoma and other models to address how mutant IDH1 inhibitors affect glioma tumor biology, with the goal of leveraging this knowledge to develop new therapies. Collectively, these studies will address clinically relevant questions with direct translational applicability to patients with IDH1-mutant gliomas.

application_id: 10892862
activity_code: K08
title: Facilitation of Information Exchange for Shared Decision Making for Lung Cancer Screening
org_name: UNIV OF MASSACHUSETTS MED SCH WORCESTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Shared decision making prior to lung cancer screening is recommended since lung cancer screening has both benefits and risks. However, shared decision making rarely happens in primary care settings due to difficulty in identifying lung cancer screening eligible patients, lack of patient awareness, lack of provider knowledge, and limited visit time. This pilot study will compare a multi-strategy technology-assisted implementation program to usual care on lung cancer screening uptake, and will provide a foundation for a future large-scale pragmatic trial to improve the uptake of and longitudinal adherence to annual lung cancer screening by supporting shared decision making.

title: Integrating Electronic Patient-Reported Outcomes into Discharge Planning
org_name: SLOAN-KETTERING INST CAN RESEARCH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The proposed research aims to assess and refine an electronic patient-reported outcomes (PROs) tool, “Goals to Discharge” (G2D), for surgical oncology patients focused on discharge planning to augment currently established discharge pathways. application_id: 11066553
activity_code: K08
title: Chi gung: a community-based strategy in Chinese immigrant women to improve colorectal and breast cancer screening.
org_name: ICAHN SCHOOL OF MEDICINE AT MOUNT SINAI
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
While screening has been shown to reduce mortality from colorectal (CRC) and breast cancer (BC), screening
uptake remains low among Chinese immigrant women, a group in whom CRC and BC are leading causes of
preventable death. The goal of this study is to develop a socio-culturally sensitive, community-engaged
intervention to improve CRC and BC screening in Chinese immigrant women and to test its preliminary impact
through a pilot randomized controlled trial. These findings will provide proof-of-concept for an integrated
approach to comprehensive cancer screening participation and will improve access, delivery, and outcomes for
underserved and underrepresented communities.

activity_code: K08
title: Leveraging a community-academic partnership to pilot a family-centered communication intervention for young adult childhood cancer survivors
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: The long-term health risks of childhood cancer therapy have been known for decades, yet adherence to lifelong survivorship care is poor, which leads to adverse health outcomes. Communication among young adult childhood cancer survivors, parents/caregivers, and clinicians is essential for survivors to learn to manage their care, but it is complex and even more challenging for some families. This project will address these gaps by partnering with a community organization to develop, pilot test, and refine a tailored family-centered communication intervention for young adult childhood cancer survivors and their parents/caregivers, working toward the goal of improving survivorship care.

title: Targeted Radionuclide Therapy to Enhance the Efficacy of CAR T Cells Against Neuroblastoma
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WISCONSIN-MADISON
phr: Project Narrative
 Despite revolutionizing the treatment of hematological malignancies, chimeric antigen receptor (CAR) T cell
therapy has had limited efficacy against solid tumors including pediatric neuroblastoma, which is associated with
a dismal prognosis upon recurrence. To overcome the molecular and cellular mechanisms that limit the efficacy
of CAR T cells against neuroblastoma, we will use targeted radionuclide therapies that selectively deliver low
dose radiation to all tumors throughout a patient’s body to stimulate infiltration and activation of CAR T cells in
these sites. We expect that this project will provide the preclinical data needed to justify clinical studies that will
test the safety and efficacy of combining for the first-time targeted radionuclide therapy and CAR T c...

application_id: 11135428
activity_code: K08
title: Improving Health-Related Quality of Life Communication Between AYA Oncology Patients and Clinicians: A Patient-Centered Intervention
org_name: CONNECTICUT CHILDREN'S MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Adolescent and young adults (AYAs) with cancer want to discuss relevant health-related quality of life (HRQOL) issues and concerns with their oncology clinicians during treatment and in survivorship, yet these conversations rarely take place. This inadequate communication puts AYAs at immediate risk for making uninformed personal health decisions that can lead to profound long-term consequences for future quality of life. In the proposed study, we will use stakeholder input to develop, refine, and assess a novel AYA-centered communication intervention to improve HRQOL conversations between AYAs and their oncology clinicians, with the ultimate goal of increasing AYA access to critical health care and education that they need across the cancer continuum.

title: CE23-003 - Caregiver Relational Responsiveness (RR): An Understudied Fidelity Construct as a Mechanism to Increase Protective Factors Against Maltreatment.
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: Project Narrative Despite the known association between the quality of participant relational engagement with service providers and clinical outcomes, limited studies have examined caregiver Relational Responsiveness (RR) as a mechanism to achieve maltreatment prevention program outcomes. Using a realist-informed mixed method approach, this study will examine RR’s role in mediating the effects of a community-based maltreatment prevention program, the Family Success Network (FSN) on protective factors against maltreatment and the contexts within which RR’s mediation effects are activated or inhibited. This study will advance the Healthy People 2030 priority area of injury and violence prevention by building the base for the science of caregiver engagement in maltreatment prevention programs...

application_id: 10983632
activity_code: K23
title: Understanding the Development of Mucosal Immunity to Poliovirus: Adjuvants and Modulation of the Enteric Microbiota
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF VERMONT & ST AGRIC COLLEGE
phr: Project Narrative Critical to polio eradication efforts is development of new vaccines that stimulate effective mucosal immunity in the gut to control viral shedding and eliminate environmental reservoirs of poliovirus (PV). This project will provide critical information to better understand the immunologic and microbiologic factors impacting development of effective mucosal immunity to PV. It will also assess the effect of a novel mucosal adjuvant (dmLT) on systemic and mucosal immune responses to Inactivated Poliovirus Vaccines (IPV). The goal of these investigations is to guide future studies directed at harnessing immune-microbiota interactions to enhance mucosal immune responses to polio vaccines and other enteric pathogens.

title: Immune biomarkers to predict outcomes in solid organ transplant patients with bloodstream infections

Bacterial bloodstream infections (BSI) are among the most common and serious infections in solid organ
transplant (SOT) recipients. Optimal immunosuppression management in SOT recipients with BSI is unknown

title: Sentinel populations and transmission hotspots of isoniazid-resistant tuberculosis
org_name: WEILL MEDICAL COLL OF CORNELL UNIV
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Isoniazid-resistant TB (INHr-TB) is the most common form of drug-resistant TB worldwide, and is a precursor
to multidrug-resistant TB. INH resistance is rarely diagnosed leading to major gaps in our understanding of this
disease. This project will provide critical insight into the epidemiology of INHr-TB including sentinel populations
for the emergence of INHr-TB and congregate settings outside the household where INHr-TB is transmitted.

activity_code: K08
title: Lymphatic-adipocyte interactions in obesity and lymphedema
org_name: BETH ISRAEL DEACONESS MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Project Narrative The relationship between adipose tissue and its resident lymphatic vasculature is complex and bidirectional. While defective lymphatic function, as seen in lymphedema, promotes fat deposition, obesity, conversely, compromises lymphatic flow. By exploring lymphatic endothelial cell communication with other adipose tissue cell types, this proposal aims to improve our understanding of how the lymphatic system contributes to adipocyte biology and overall metabolic health.

application_id: 11141863
activity_code: K08
title: Improving response prediction to neoadjuvant therapy in pancreatic cancer
org_name: DANA-FARBER CANCER INST
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Localized pancreatic cancers (PC) have a high risk of recurrence and are treated with neoadjuvant
chemotherapy which improves cure rates though identifying early resistance during NAC remains a clinical
challenge. This project utilizes advances in single cell transcriptomics, highly multiplexed imaging and ctDNA
technologies, paired with large clinically annotated PC biospecimen repositories to develop methods for
assessing response and identifying resistance to neoadjuvant therapies used in localized PC. I anticipate that
this work will nominate novel biomarkers for clinical use.

activity_code: K08
title: A Nurse-Led, Coping and Supportive Care Intervention to Improve Psychosocial Outcomes of Patients with Triple-Negative Breast Cancer During the Transition from Curative Therapy to Surveillance
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Patients with triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC) undergo rigorous treatment regimens yet frequently experience high cancer recurrence rates and cancer death, leading to uncertainty, distress, and poor quality of life. The proposed research project aims to develop, refine, and pilot test a nurse-led, coping, and supportive care intervention for patients with TNBC during the critical transition from curative therapy to surveillance, designed to alleviate distress, improve quality of life, and increase stress-coping skills. By focusing on this previously understudied high-risk group of patients with breast cancer, study findings will inform future research utilizing risk-stratified survivorship care models with potential applications to other high-risk cancer survivors.

title: Personalizing Antimicrobial Use in Ventilator-Associated Pneumonia
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The current approach to treatment of ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP)—a common, costly, and highly morbid infection—is inflexible and impersonal. The present approach increases side-effect risk, promotes drug- resistant infections, and under-treats many patients. This project aims to surmount barriers to personalizing VAP antibiotic treatment durations by identifying lung biomarkers that reliably identify cure of infection and creating novel VAP trial outcomes that inform bedside care.

application_id: 11247912
activity_code: F31
title: Contributions of Astrocyte Kir4.1/5.1 Channels to Disordered Breathing in Rett Syndrome
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CONNECTICUT STORRS
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Rett syndrome (RTT) is a neurodevelopmental disorder with a high mortality rate caused in part by respiratory
dysfunction. RTT is caused by mutations in the gene encoding methyl-CpG-binding protein 2 (MECP2) and
evidence suggests MeCP2 regulates expression of astrocyte Kir4.1 channels; however, it is not known how
loss of MeCP2 or Kir4.1 channels disrupts breathing. This project seeks to identify astrocytes and Kir4.1
channels as the cellular and molecular basis for breathing problems in a mouse model of RTT.

activity_code: K08
title: Improving response prediction to neoadjuvant therapy in pancreatic cancer
org_name: DANA-FARBER CANCER INST
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Localized pancreatic cancers (PC) have a high risk of recurrence and are treated with neoadjuvant chemotherapy which improves cure rates though identifying early resistance during NAC remains a clinical challenge. This project utilizes advances in single cell transcriptomics, highly multiplexed imaging and ctDNA technologies, paired with large clinically annotated PC biospecimen repositories to develop methods for assessing response and identifying resistance to neoadjuvant therapies used in localized PC. I anticipate that this work will nominate novel biomarkers for clinical use.

title: IAFF HazMat Training at DOE Nuclear Sites
org_name: INTERNATIONAL ASSOCIATION FIRE FIGHTERS
phr: Project Narrative The IAFF has partnered with the NIEHS Worker Training Program since 1995, delivering 649 classes and training 13,943 workers, including firefighters, at DOE nuclear weapons complexes to prevent work-related harm and enhance safety. application_id: 11158692
activity_code: K23
title: Redefining C.difficile patient outcomes through network medicine
org_name: BETH ISRAEL DEACONESS MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The Centers for Disease Control and Infection consider Clostridioides difficile infection an urgent public health
threat; this career development award addresses our limited ability to predict its severe complications, response
to treatment, or recurrence of disease, and to distinguish it from indolent carriage. We hypothesize that by
integrating fecal immune, metabolic and microbial data, with modern bioinformatics tools, we can improve our
ability to predict these situations and understand the biological mechanisms behind them. Biological markers in
stool will be valuable tools to choose or design more timely and effective treatments for patients.

application_id: 11167503
activity_code: K08
title: Automatic integrated biomarkers to improve prediction of lung cancer outcomes
org_name: SLOAN-KETTERING INST CAN RESEARCH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The digitization of health records and widespread availability of tumor DNA sequencing results offer a chance to
better risk-stratify patients with implications for therapy selection, monitoring, and side effect mitigation. Here,
we will test whether multimodal, artificial intelligence pipelines improve outcome modeling over standard
predictors for patients with non-small cell lung cancer at three points: 1) Patients with localized disease at risk
for relapse following definitive therapy, 2) Patients with oligometastatic disease considering consolidative
radiotherapy, 3) Patients at risk for central nervous system disease who may benefit from MRI surveillance.

application_id: 10907787
activity_code: K01
title: Impact of Environmental Exposures on Lung Immunity over Age
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Elderly people are more susceptible to respiratory tract infections. Pulmonary macrophages are among the first cell types to respond to all the environmental exposures (e.g., air pollution) and functional discrepancies in this innate immune cell subset can increase the severity of diseases such as influenza due to aggravated inflammation; however, age-associated changes in pulmonary macrophages that are under constant influence of the environment exposures is not well studied. This proposal is designed to investigate the hypothesis that atmospheric particulate matter is contained within the macrophages and due to these particulates, aging pulmonary macrophages have altered transcriptional profile with compromised respiratory immune responses.

application_id: 11175412
activity_code: K08
title: Development of a novel survey to screen for chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy in young children
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy (CIPN) is a debilitating condition that occurs in up to 87% of
children treated with chemotherapy and often persists in long-term childhood cancer survivors. CIPN is
particularly challenging to identify in young children because there are no validated instruments to measure
CIPN in children less than five years old, even though 40% of children at risk for CIPN are in this age group,
and early intervention can improve their symptoms. The overarching goal of this proposal is to develop a
patient-centered clinician-guided instrument to screen for CIPN in young children and collect preliminary data
to inform future implementation.

title: Behavioral and neural properties of spatial direction comprehension in aging and amestic mild cognitive impairment
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF FLORIDA
phr: Project Narrative Spatial navigation, a fundamental human behavior which facilitates a safe and independent life, is among the first behaviors to decline in healthy aging and Alzheimer’s disease and related dementias, greatly contributing to poor health outcomes and a loss of independence in these populations. Supporting spatial navigation requires an understanding of how the brain constructs spatial representations from communication tools that convey spatial directions in abstracted forms (i.e., signs, maps, verbal directions, and global positioning systems). This proposal examines behavioral and neural properties of spatial direction comprehension, and how those properties are altered in older adults and individuals with amnestic mild cognitive impairment, providing a novel characteriza...

application_id: 11141608
activity_code: K08
title: A community health worker intervention to improve lung cancer screening uptake in community health centers serving Black and Hispanic communities
org_name: BAYSTATE MEDICAL CENTER, INC.
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE:
Lung cancer screening (LCS) can reduce lung cancer-related mortality by 20%, but only 5-10% of eligible
individuals have received an initial LCS, with Black and Hispanic individuals less likely to be screened. The
goal of this study is to partner with community stakeholders to jointly develop and pilot test a multi-component
community health worker intervention targeting key barriers to improve LCS and tobacco treatment utilization.
The proposed activities will lay the groundwork for a subsequent R01 grant, conducting a fully powered
randomized clinical trial to establish CHWs as an evidence-based practice that will facilitate access to
screening and reduce lung cancer mortality both overall and among underserved populations.

title: Summer Institute in Statistics and Modeling in Infectious Diseases
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: Public Health Relevance Statement
Statistical and mathematical methods for the study of infectious diseases and for
discovery and evaluation of interventions have become increasingly important. The
Summer Institute in Statistics and Modeling in Infectious Diseases (SISMID) provides
necessary and innovative training in statistical and mathematical modeling to students,
postdoctoral fellows, and professionals. This research education program enables them
to formulate research questions, analyze data, design studies, and model dynamics and
evolution of infectious diseases including emerging infectious diseases relevant to
important public health and biomedical research questions.

application_id: 10757726
activity_code: F30
title: Resistance Mechanisms in ROS1-Fusion-Driven Malignancies
org_name: OREGON HEALTH & SCIENCE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Gene fusions of the receptor tyrosine kinase, ROS1, have been shown to drive neoplastic transformation of cells in a range of malignancies and targeted therapy with tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKI) that have activity against ROS1 kinase domain, has led to significant reduction of tumor burden in patients. Unfortunately, clinical resistance to TKI therapy frequently occurs, which limits its long-term durability as an anti-tumor treatment strategy. This project will explore a novel mechanism by which resistance to ROS1-TKI emerges from an alternate signaling pathway and predict new resistant point mutations in ROS1 that confer resistance to the next generation of ROS1-TKI therapy.

title: Validation and implementation of ctDNA as a clinically informative biomarkerin AYAs with sarcomas.
org_name: DANA-FARBER CANCER INST
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Adolescents and young adults with sarcomas suffer a significant burden of morbidity and mortality with minimal

application_id: 10806316
activity_code: K08
title: Defining Tumor Microenvironmental Interactions that drive THY1-Mediated Treatment Resistance in Glioblastoma
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: Glioblastoma is the most common primary brain tumor in adults and is uniformly fatal. We have identified a rare population of tumors cells that may be responsible for treatment resistance resulting from signaling/interactions within its local tumor microenvironment. We now seek to study these cells and their interactions in hopes that inhibiting their signaling mechanisms will result in treatment sensitivity and lead to the identification of novel therapies for patients.

title: next-generation sequencing approaches to identify genotype-phenotype relationships during miRNA tuning of neural crest osteogenesis

Defective neural crest differentiation contributes to the occurrence of craniofacial birth defects that place
substantial psychological and physical burden on the affected individuals due to disfigurement and dysfunction.
We propose here to elucidate the role of microRNA361 as an epigenetic regulator of craniofacial skeletogenesis

application_id: 10812399
activity_code: K08
title: Dual Use of ENDS and Combustible Cigarettes: Shared, Distinct, and Cross-Conditioned Processes
org_name: BROWN UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Dual use of electronic nicotine delivery systems (ENDS) and combustible cigarettes is the most common
pattern of tobacco use among young adults. However, little is known about the basic behavioral processes that
underlie dual use and the degree to which these processes are shared between products. The current project
aims to examine behavioral underpinnings of ENDS use and tobacco dual use and craving among young
adults in laboratory and naturalistic settings - focusing on cue reactivity, attentional bias, and negative affect -
so that such knowledge can inform future research and intervention efforts to reduce the public health burden
associated with tobacco use in the U.S.

application_id: 11113801
activity_code: K08
title: The Role of Propionate Metabolism in Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer
org_name: WEILL MEDICAL COLL OF CORNELL UNIV
phr: Project Narrative Recent studies have shown that genetic changes explain only 50% of lung cancer disease progression, strongly suggesting there are unidentified, non-genetic mechanisms that are driving drug resistance and metastasis. In preliminary experiments, we have identified that propionate metabolism, a pathway previously not known to play a role in cancer, is dysregulated in lung cancer, and its dysregulation can drive drug resistance and metastatic programs. This proposal aims to improve our understanding of propionate metabolism in lung cancer by defining the mechanisms and consequences of its dysregulation, potentially identifying a new pathway that can be targeted therapeutically.

org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA LOS ANGELES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE There are limited data on SARS-CoV-2 hybrid immunity, defined as vaccine-induced immunity before or after natural infection, in pregnant women. application_id: 11190817
activity_code: K23
title: Modeling the Impact of Interventions to Reduce Typhoidal Salmonella Transmission in the Indo-Pacific.
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The typhoidal Salmonellas (TS), primarily Salmonella Typhi and Paratyphi A, remain a major
cause of global morbidity and mortality. Revitalizing Informal Settlements and their Environments
(RISE) is a cluster-randomized controlled trial of community-level upgrades of water, sanitation,
and hygiene (WASH) infrastructure currently underway in 24 informal urban settlements in
Indonesia and Fiji, two countries at high risk for TS. To inform improved future interventions under
different scenarios, the data from this controlled trial, including serosurveys and human and
environmental microbiome data, will be used to establish compartmental modeling approaches to
predict and assess the impact of WASH interventions on TS transmission.

application_id: 11240753
activity_code: F32
title: Structural investigations into the regulatory states of human Ribonucleotide Reductase
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Ribonucleotide reductase (RNR) is an essential metalloenzyme found in all living organisms. RNR proteins
catalyze the reduction of ribonucleotides into deoxyribonucleotides which are required for DNA synthesis and
repair. RNR inhibitors are used clinically for the treatment of cancer, however, our understanding of the
regulatory pathways of this enzyme is still incomplete. The proposed aims will use structural biology
techniques to investigate the mechanism of human RNR activity and inactivation by determining structures of
the protein in multiple regulatory states.

title: Characterizing mechanisms by which HIV-infected macrophages neutralize NK cell effector functions to facilitate their persistence
org_name: UNIV OF MASSACHUSETTS MED SCH WORCESTER
phr: Project Narrative HIV is a chronic disease that fails to be controlled by the immune system, which may stem, in part, from the virus’ ability to infect hard-to-kill immune cells, such as macrophages. In this proposal, I will investigate mechanisms by which HIV-infected macrophages limit killing by natural killer (NK) cells to promote their survival. org_name: MIRIAM HOSPITAL

activity_code: K23
title: Optimizing Implementation of Digital X-ray with Computer-Aided Detection for Community-Based Tuberculosis Screening
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Computer-aided detection (CAD), which uses artificial intelligence to analyze chest X-rays, is now WHO-
recommended for tuberculosis (TB) screening and holds the potential to improve TB detection in high-burden,
low-resource communities. This proposed research seeks to evaluate the adaptive application of CAD for
community-based TB screening in sub-Saharan Africa, utilizing individualized and context-specific methods, as
an alternative to the current standard of a "one-size-fits-all" approach. By assessing diagnostic accuracy,
implementation barriers, and cost-effectiveness of each screening strategy, the insights gained from this study
could be used to facilitate earlier detection of TB and reduce the high morbidity and mortality caused by
tuberculosis.

activity_code: K00
title: Translation of small open reading frames in 3'UTR enhances translation of canonical open reading frames
org_name: ROCKEFELLER UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Novel mechanisms for post-transcriptional gene expression regulation continue to emerge. I recently identified the role of ribosome to enhance translation of canonical open reading frames by translating small ORFs (downstream ORFs or dORFs) in the 3’UTR. Since dORF is a novel, widespread and strong regulatory element among vertebrates, this proposal aims to study the mechanism of dORF regulation and the biological impact of dORF dysregulation in cancer. This will help us better understand post-transcriptional regulation in cancer and further, may provide dORF as new diagnostic regions of the mRNA and potential targets for drug design.

application_id: 11241073
activity_code: F32
title: Identification of the core organizational principles within nuclear mRNAs
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA AT DAVIS
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Small molecule therapeutics have traditionally focused on binding protein targets for inhibition, an unsuitable
approach when mutations in disease already cause a loss of function. Nuclear export of messenger RNA is an
indispensable step to making proteins in the cell and mutations in many diseases inhibit the export process. The
work proposed work here provides a potential novel framework for replacing protein function lost in disease by
designing small molecule therapeutics that target mRNA structure to restore messenger RNA export and gene
expression.

title: Assessing the impact of water handling practices and seasonality on the effectiveness of a drinking water intervention in Beira, Mozambique
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  Enteric infections, specifically the resulting diarrheal disease, are a leading cause of morbidity and mortality for children under 5 years old in low-income settings. phr: Narrative Resilience, characterized as the capacity to recover quickly from highly stressful or traumatic experiences, may lessen the gravity of negative consequences of opioid use disorder (OUD). This study seeks to achieve a better understanding of resilience through the development of a human laboratory model that integrates measurement of psychobiological components of resilience (e.g., emotional flexibility – the ability to flexibly regulate emotions in a context-appropriate manner) with behavioral measures of stress reactivity (e.g., heroin demand on the heroin purchase task – a proxy for heroin self-administration). Developing a laboratory model of resilience will permit investigating whether resilience is stable and if it can be improved—a prerequisite to measure the effects of any...

application_id: 11052644
activity_code: K08
title: Role of Fragile X Mental Retardation Protein on Gene Expression in Auditory Brainstem Development
org_name: UT SOUTHWESTERN MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Project Narrative
The absence of Fragile X Mental Retardation Protein (FMRP) results in the most common single
gene cause of hereditary autism spectrum disorder, which is also associated with
hypersensitivity to sounds. We will investigate how FMRP regulates gene expression in the
developing auditory system that may modulate neuroplasticity. Results may reveal therapeutic
targets to treat hypersensitivity to or abnormal processing of sounds.

application_id: 10757662
activity_code: K08
title: Engineering protease biomarkers to guide surgical therapy for vestibular schwannoma and Neurofibromatosis Type 2
org_name: OHIO STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  Vestibular schwannoma (VS) is a brain tumor that severely impairs one’s ability to hear and communicate. Surgical removal of VS through a craniotomy leads to significant morbidities including hearing loss, paralysis of the face and injury to critical parts of the brain. Using a combination of the state-of-the art tools, including cell and animal models of VS, genomic sequencing and nanotechnology, this proposed study identifies new biomarkers that could help surgeons identify which VS are aggressive, and develops nanoparticles that home to tumor cells to label areas of the tumor to make surgery more precise and safer for patients.

application_id: 10909294
activity_code: K38
title: Improving the Identification of Opioid-Associated Out-of-Hospital Cardiac Arrest
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA AT DAVIS
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Rates of opioid-associated out-of-hospital cardiac arrest (OA-OHCA) in the United States have been rising exponentially over the past decade and the American Heart Association has identified the rigorous evaluation of naloxone’s efficacy in OA-OHCA as an important knowledge gap. There are currently no evidence-based methods for the real-time identification of OA-OHCA, making clinical trials of targeted naloxone in this population difficult. Developing externally valid and generalizable criteria to identify suspected OA-OHCA is the first step in designing an ethical and successful clinical trial of targeted-naloxone treatment in OA-OHCA, relevant to NIH’s mission to protect and improve health.

activity_code: K00
title: A Multi-omics Approach to Examine Symptoms and Medication Adherence in Women with Breast Cancer
org_name: CASE WESTERN RESERVE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative: A substantial proportion of women are not adherent to aromatase inhibitor (AI) therapy, primarily due to their experience of highly variable treatment- and/or disease- related symptoms. Symptoms and adherence, especially their impact on each other, have not been well-characterized temporally, nor have potential biologic mechanisms related to AI absorption, distribution, metabolism, and elimination (ADME). The purpose of the proposed F99/K00 training and research will utilize a biobehavioral, multi-omics approach to gain a deeper understanding of the temporal variability and interplay of symptoms, adherence, and omics to guide future interventions and their timing in women with breast cancer.

application_id: 11046510
activity_code: K08
title: Cellular reprogramming of cochlear glial cells to regenerate auditory neurons
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF SOUTHERN CALIFORNIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Damage to the spiral ganglion nerve leads to permanent deafness, and a cochlear implant is the most effective
treatment for severe-profound deafness. However, cochlear implants are ineffective with neuronal
degeneration, and regenerative medicine, such as direct neuronal reprogramming to replenish the damaged
spiral ganglion neurons, holds enormous potential to cure deafness and improve cochlear implant outcomes.
This grant explores approaches to advance cellular reprogramming of cochlear glial cells into induced spiral
ganglion neurons using a new mouse model with selective ablation of spiral ganglion neurons, optimizing gene
delivery into cochlear glial cells, and enhancing reprogramming efficiency by combining micro RNAs with
neuronal transcription factors.

title: Improving middle grade STEM interest and increased learning using GN and DOC
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MONTANA
phr: Project Narrative This SEPA project will develop graphic novels (GN) and documentary (DOC) style videos to encourage STEM education in 7th and 9th grade students. title: Modulation of MMPs gene expression and activity by the microbiome in caries
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF IOWA
phr: Project Narrative A group of collagen-degrading enzymes present in dentin is suggested to be responsible for tissue breakdown that advances caries lesions, but the real contribution of these enzymes to disease progression and how their expression and activity is modulated are unclear. This study will fill major gaps in the understanding of endogenous processes in caries progression and provide insights into interactions between the microbiome present at different stages of the disease with host responses that express and/or activate dentinal enzymes. activity_code: K08
title: Modulation of MMPs gene expression and activity by the microbiome in caries
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF IOWA
phr: Project Narrative Dentin matrix metalloproteinases (MMPs) are considered key proteases for tissue breakdown that advance caries lesions, but the actual contribution of MMPs to disease progression and how their expression and activity are modulated are unclear. The K08 parent award will fill major gaps in understanding endogenous processes in caries progression and provide insights into interactions between the microbiome present at different stages of the disease with host responses that express and/or activate dentinal enzymes. This supplemental award aims to ensure the continuity of the proposed training and research goals, mainly related to dentin-pulp genomics and proteomics, OMICS approaches and techniques, bioinformatics, and scientific writing.

activity_code: K00
title: Targeting the Epigenome to Improve Responses to Immunotherapy
org_name: BRIGHAM AND WOMEN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Breast cancer is the most commonly diagnosed cancer among women in the United States, meaning that identifying effective therapies and improving upon current standard-of-care treatment options is critically important. The research conducted during the F99 phase will explore how the function of dendritic cells is altered during TIM-3 blockade, while the K00 phase will focus on understanding how the location of immune cells within the tumor affects the induction of anti-tumor immunity. Both of these studies will increase our understanding of the mechanisms behind the anti-tumor immune response, and have the potential to inform the design of future immunotherapeutic interventions.

title: Evaluating METTL1-mediated m7G tRNA methylation as a novel dependency in alveolar rhadomyosarcoma
org_name: DANA-FARBER CANCER INST
phr: Alveolar rhabdomyosarcoma (ARMS) is the most common childhood sarcoma and is driven by the PAX3/7-FOXO1 fusion protein. Therapeutically targeting the PAX3/7-FOXO1 fusion protein has proven challenging and there remains a critical need to identify new novel dependencies and targets that may prove as more effective therapeutic avenues. The K00 phase of this proposal will profile the METTL1-dependent changes in m7G tRNA methylation and establish METTL1 and/or its targets as potential therapeutic targets for the treatment of ARMS.

org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: NARRATIVE Patients with disproportional jaws have underbites or open bites and suffer from speech disorders twenty times more than the general public, whereby these patients seek jaw surgery and braces to treat issues heavily impacting their quality of life, including abnormal chewing, facial esthetics, and speech. application_id: 10757636
activity_code: F30
title: Investigating the Roles of Phospholipase A2s and Diacylglycerol Signaling in Ferroptosis
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Ferroptosis is an iron-dependent, oxidative form of regulated cell death that is morphologically and biochemically distinct from other cell death pathways, and has been implicated in numerous cancer therapies and natural tumor suppression mechanisms. This proposal will utilize small molecule and lipid probes, genetic modulation techniques, lipidomic profiling and activation state assays to investigate the roles of lipid remodeling and lipid-responsive signaling pathways in mediating ferroptotic cell death. Building a more comprehensive model of the molecular networks involved in ferroptosis may uncover new avenues of research to exploit this cell death pathway for therapeutic benefit in cancer.

activity_code: K08
title: Deciphering the Role of Frizzled Receptors in Palatal Development
org_name: VAN ANDEL RESEARCH INSTITUTE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Orofacial clefts are one of the most common structural birth defects with an average incidence of 1 in 700 newborns. Components of the Wnt signaling pathway, including the frizzled receptors, have been shown to be necessary for normal palatal development, and mutations in one of these genes, Frizzled 2, have been associated with congenital disorders affecting craniofacial development. The objective of this proposal is to determine how mutations in Frizzled 2 affects the developing palate to ultimately support the development of novel therapeutic approaches.

application_id: 11105886
activity_code: K00
title: Elucidating the role of RNA splicing in the metastatic seeding and outgrowth of aggressive cancers.
org_name: DANA-FARBER CANCER INST
phr: Project Narrative Cancer metastasis is a deadly, elusive, and heterogeneous disease which lacks effective targeted therapeutics, and understanding the mechanisms underlying cancer metastasis remains to be fully elucidated. RNA splicing lends to cellular complexity and heterogeneity by producing distinct RNA and protein isoforms from a single gene, and the dysregulation of RNA splicing has been implicated in cancer development and progression. This research proposal seeks to identify novel mechanisms in which dysregulated alternative splicing contributes to metastatic seeding and outgrowth in breast and other aggressive cancers, with the ultimate goal of identifying potential biomarkers and therapeutic targets of cancer metastasis.

title: Pancreatic cancer stem cells: PD2-mediated novel mechanistic link and metabolomic alterations
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE This research proposal outlines a training plan for becoming an independent cancer researcher with a focus in cancer stem cells (CSCs) and pancreatic cancer (PC). In F99 predoctoral training, I will investigate the role of Pancreatic Differentiation2 (PD2) in pancreatic CSCs and CSC-mediated PC progression, with the goal of deciphering the mechanism of PD2-dependent CSC maintenance.
