application_id: 10747951
activity_code: F32
title: A Genomic Approach to Discovering Novel Cathepsin Inhibitors from Cyanobacteria
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO
phr: Project Narrative There are currently no FDA-approved medications to treat the neuropathology that follows traumatic brain injury; however, inhibition of cathepsins B and L have been associated with improved neurocognitive and behavioral outcomes after neuronal injury in animal models. Cyanobacteria are known to produce natural product compounds with neurologic bioactivity, including cathepsin and other protease inhibitors. In this proposal, we hypothesize that novel cathepsin inhibitors can be discovered from cyanobacteria through an innovative genomic approach, which includes pharmacophore-based genome mining, delineation of biosynthetic gene clusters, cultivation, and heterologous gene expression.

title: Cellular engineering to improve the efficacy and specificity of targeted immunotherapy
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY
phr: Project Narrative Engineered T cells programmed to recognize and kill tumors have emerged as an extraordinarily powerful tool for combating cancer. Nonetheless, aside from chimeric antigen receptor T cells for the treatment of blood cancers, most engineered cell therapies are often ineffective and potentially highly toxic, particularly those that target solid tumors. This research proposal develops synthetic biology tools to combat three major challenges of engineered T cells: tumor recognition, toxicity and the immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment.

title: Automatic identification of early bone loss patterns from radiographs invisible to human eyes for early periodontal disease diagnosis and prevention
org_name: TEMPLE UNIV OF THE COMMONWEALTH
phr: Project Narrative Periodontitis affects nearly half of the US adults and is associated with poor quality of life and increased healthcare costs. Periodontitis can be prevented if the high-risk patients are identified before the disease initiation and progression. A prediction model that identifies early bone loss patterns using advanced image processing and informatics methods proposed in this study may identify high-risk patients before the disease initiation to provide preventive management.

title: Controlled Antigen Delivery for Prophylactic Cancer Vaccination
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Carcinomas, solid tumors of epithelial origin, represent up to 90% of all cancer cases in the United States and present a persistent healthcare challenge. Immunotherapy and chemotherapy are promising treatment strategies for carcinomas, but they have limited efficacy and are associated with side effects that impact patient quality of life. This F99/K00 award proposal focuses on developing a peptide-based hydrogel for the local delivery of chemotherapies and immune checkpoint inhibitors to improve treatment efficacy while avoiding systemic toxicity and side effects.

activity_code: K08
title: Nanotransfection-based Cell Reprogramming to Drive Bone Revascularization in a Novel Mouse Model of Autogenous Cranioplasty
org_name: OHIO STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Traumatic brain injury (TBI) is a leading cause of death and disability, with an estimated 1.5 million Americans sustaining a TBI each year. Although decompressive craniectomy is a lifesaving procedure in severe TBI, subsequent replacement of the cryopreserved autogenous bone to reconstruct the critical size skull defect is accompanied by a high risk of resorption and failure. This proposal aims to utilize a novel mouse model to further delineate the mechanisms of cryopreserved calvarial bone healing and failure, and to implement an innovative, nanotransfection- based vasculogenic cell reprogramming approach to revascularize cryopreserved bone during cranioplasty.

activity_code: K00
title: Profiling and leveraging bystander T cells within the tumor microenvironment
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MINNESOTA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Although T cells specific for tumor antigens have been a primary target of immunotherapies, tumor-nonspecific “bystander” T cells are often found in human solid tumors. Despite being a major component of the tumor infiltrate, little is known concerning the role of bystander T cells within solid tumors. This project seeks to define mechanisms that underlie bystander entry and function within solid tumors and leverage bystander T cells to enhance anti-tumor immune responses.

application_id: 10784348
activity_code: K08
title: DECIPHERING MECHANISMS OF IL-37 MEDIATED CONTROL OF PERIODONTAL INFLAMMATION
org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The onset of periodontitis involves an influx of immune cells and a cascade of inflammatory biomolecules in response to a dysbiotic microbiota. Interleukin-37, an anti-inflammatory cytokine, is dysregulated in severe forms of periodontitis; however, its role in the disease’s pathogenesis remains poorly defined. The proposed research will begin to unravel the complex processes that interleukin-37 employs during periodontal inflammation with emphasis on regulatory mechanisms required for its bioactivity in the innate immune response by the periodontium.

application_id: 10833004
activity_code: K08
title: Evaluation of variability in care and outcomes for patients with gastrointestinal bleeding
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
There is substantial variability in patient outcomes after hospitalization for gastrointestinal bleeding (GIB)
across hospitals. Patient-related factors, hospital structure and processes of care play major roles in
determining outcomes for many other diseases, but the impact of these factors on outcomes related to GIB has
not been adequately defined. This novel proposal will evaluate the effect of patient-level factors to generate
risk-standardized rates of GIB outcomes across hospitals and to identify modifiable hospital-level factors using
a mixed methods approach to study comprehensively how modifiable factors are associated with better
outcomes.

title: Brain Drivers, Cognition, and Parkinson's Disease: A Psychometric Approach
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF FLORIDA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Parkinson’s disease is one of the fastest growing neurodegenerative diseases and is often accompanied by progressive, eventually debilitating cognitive decline to dementia. The goal of the proposed project is to determine whether a more comprehensive approach for assessing neurobiological dysfunction will better predict current and future cognitive disturbance. Broadly, improving the assessment of cognitive risk could inform both clinical prognosis for individuals with PD and allow for a more targeted selection of participants into experimental trials aiming to slow impending cognitive decline.

title: Role of Interferon lambda signaling in mucosal homeostasis and infantile-onset inflammatory bowel disease
org_name: BOSTON CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Inflammatory bowel diseases (IBD) are now considered global diseases with fastest growing incidence among
young patients diagnosed before 6 years of age. The pathophysiology of IBD remains incompletely understood
involving the importance of the microbiome and more recently, the virome and available therapies remain
ineffective in a large number of patients with IBD. This project will serve to better understand the mechanistic
contribution of interferon lambdas (IFNλs) in maintaining intestinal homeostasis, preventing development of
IBD and their therapeutic potential.

title: Dissecting the cellular interplays of adipose tissue remodeling in the regulation of insulin sensitivity
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Both obesity and consequent diabetes are highly prevalent worldwide, and given their profound impact on morbidity and mortality, limiting these conditions is a major health challenge of our time. This proposal seeks to address critical knowledge gaps in our mechanistic understanding of the molecular and cellular factors driving a process termed fat “fibrosis”, which we showed is associated with insulin resistance in humans, and the extent to which these factors reciprocally govern an alternative process, termed “beiging”, which is associated with metabolic health and insulin sensitivity. activity_code: K08
title: Delineation of pathogenic mechanisms of NOS1AP and TRIM8 mutations in monogenic SRNS/FSGS.
org_name: BOSTON CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL
phr: NARRATIVE
Steroid resistant nephrotic syndrome (SRNS) is a leading cause of chronic kidney disease in the first three
decades of life1. Renal biopsies typically reveal focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS), and these children
are unresponsive to standard steroid therapy and invariably progress to end-stage renal disease. This career
development grant will explore the pathogenic mechanisms by which human mutations in the novel disease
genes NOS1AP and TRIM8 cause SRNS/FSGS.

activity_code: F31
title: An Engineered Hydrogel Platform to Improve Neural Organoid Reproducibility for a Multi-Organoid Disease Model of 22q11.2 Deletion Syndrome
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative l Studies of human neurodevelopment and neurodevelopmental disorders suffer due to a lack of adequate model systems. To address this urgent need, my proposed work seeks to identify an engineered biomaterials system that is both permissive and instructive in guiding reproducible neural organoid maturation to develop a robust human stem cell-based neural organoid culture platform. This interdisciplinary approach will facilitate the fusion of organoids representing different brain regions (to form assembloids) to study temporally critical development processes and progenitor migration in a disease model of 22q11.2 deletion syndrome, revealing important fundamental insights into brain development and disease.

application_id: 11059864
activity_code: K08
title: A Targeted Approach to the Surveillance of Precursor Lesions for Gastric Cancer
org_name: SLOAN-KETTERING INST CAN RESEARCH
phr: Project Narrative
Data are limited regarding risk factors for progression of gastric intestinal metaplasia to gastric cancer in the
Unites States (US) population, and regarding which individuals may benefit from preventative interventions
such as endoscopic screening/surveillance. The proposed research will utilize pathology data from across the
US to characterize the prevalence of extensive and limited gastric intestinal metaplasia, and the respective
risks of progression of these lesions to gastric cancer. Subsequently, we will utilize simulation modeling to
assess how targeted screening and surveillance can impact health outcomes in patients across 64 specific,
diverse risk-factor profiles, both on a patient level and a population level.

title: Scholars in BioMedical Sciences (SBMS) Training Program
org_name: STATE UNIVERSITY NEW YORK STONY BROOK
phr: Narrative The SBMS training program provides didactic training in translational science and supports clinical research opportunities for PhD candidates starting their second or third year of doctoral training. The goal of this program is to expose the students to biomedical research, promote an understanding of the challenges of medical research, bridge the gap between basic biology and medical practice and provide relevance and urgency to basic science research and establish collaborations between basic and clinical researchers.

application_id: 10758209
activity_code: F30
title: Elucidating roles of microglial lipid droplets in neurodegeneration
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Alzheimer disease is the most common cause of dementia with no clearly effective disease modifying therapies. Alterations in glial lipid metabolism have been shown to be central to AD pathogenesis, and the present study is designed to evaluate the effects of modulating microglial lipid buffering capacity on inflammation and disease progression in a mouse model of tauopathy. Our findings will inform the development of novel classes of microglia-targeted therapies that may slow the progression of neurological diseases characterized by aberrant lipid metabolism like AD.

application_id: 11116834
activity_code: F32
title: Impact of bidirectional signaling between megakaryocytes and osteosarcoma cells on metastasis
org_name: ST. JUDE CHILDREN'S RESEARCH HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Metastasis of cancer cells from the bone to the lung is responsible for the majority of osteosarcoma patient deaths, but how this process happens is not completely understood. Platelets are known to facilitate metastasis of other cancers, and studies have shown that cancer progression can affect megakaryocytes (platelet producing cells) but specific interactions between osteosarcoma cells, megakaryocytes, and platelets have not been established. This proposal focuses on examining how interactions between osteosarcoma cells and megakaryocytes in the bone marrow influence how megakaryocytes make platelets, alter the function of those platelets, and promote osteosarcoma metastasis to the lungs.

title: The impact of diet on granulopoiesis, neutrophil function, and sickle cell disease pathology
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Millions globally are at risk of nutritional stress and these external signals serve as a potent regulator of human physiology and pathophysiology. It is largely unknown why sickle cell disease (SCD) presents with variable severity between patients. As much of the pathology in SCD is driven by neutrophils, we aim to evaluate how nutritional stress regulates neutrophils development, function, and contribution to SCD severity, with applications to other neutrophil-driven diseases.

activity_code: K08
title: A Targeted Approach to the Surveillance of Precursor Lesions for Gastric Cancer
org_name: SLOAN-KETTERING INST CAN RESEARCH
phr: Project Narrative Data are limited regarding risk factors for progression of gastric intestinal metaplasia to gastric cancer in the Unites States (US) population, and regarding which individuals may benefit from preventative interventions such as endoscopic screening/surveillance. The proposed research will utilize pathology data from across the US to characterize the prevalence of extensive and limited gastric intestinal metaplasia, and the respective risks of progression of these lesions to gastric cancer. Subsequently, we will utilize simulation modeling to assess how targeted screening and surveillance can impact health outcomes in patients across 64 specific, diverse risk-factor profiles, both on a patient level and a population level.

application_id: 10814212
activity_code: K08
title: Regulation of portal hypertension through neutrophil-platelet interactions in liver sinusoids
org_name: MAYO CLINIC ROCHESTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Portal hypertension is a common final pathway of chronic liver disease and is responsible for significant
morbidity and mortality among patients with liver disease. The goal of this research is to elucidate the role of
neutrophil-platelet interactions in driving microvascular thrombosis which we previously identified as critical in
the pathogenesis of portal hypertension. This proposal is expected to identify clinically-relevant mechanisms to
inhibit the neutrophil-platelet interactions which drive portal hypertension.

title: Assessing the efficacy of NIR therapy to modulate shared secondary injury mechanisms in a murine model of ischemic stroke and porcine model of TBI
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: Project Narrative Due to the similarities between pig and human brain anatomy, development, and neuroinflammatory response, pigs are a highly promising model for studying age-specific responses to mechanical trauma. Here we have developed a training plan where I will use a battery of multiparametric behavioral and physiological assessments to characterize post-injury changes in a pig model of pediatric concussion. The findings from these studies could encourage researchers to use the pig to develop novel therapies for use in neurotrauma patients, and ultimately improve diagnostics and prognostication on the front lines of TBI patient care.

application_id: 10757734
activity_code: F31
title: Pathologic Substrates of Neuropsychiatric Symptoms in Aphasic Dementia
org_name: NORTHWESTERN UNIVERSITY AT CHICAGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Dementia diagnoses are projected to rise exponentially in the next several decades, with the vast majority of patients developing neuropsychiatric symptoms that are burdensome and difficult to treat. One of the most challenging aspects of identifying the biological substrates contributing to symptoms in dementia is that one dementia syndrome can be caused by different postmortem pathologies. This study will examine associations between anatomic distributions of pathology and neuropsychiatric symptoms in primary progressive aphasia— a language-based dementia caused by multiple pathologies—with the goal of uncovering the putative substrates of neurodegeneration that may enhance diagnostic accuracy, predictions of future decline, and treatment approaches.

activity_code: K00
title: Training in lifespan behavioral, social, and neuroscience research connecting early-life cognitive decline to late-life ADRD
org_name: HARVARD UNIVERSITY
phr: Findings from the proposed line of research will link accelerated brain and biological aging in midlife to brain aging in older adults with cognitive impairment as well as Alzheimer’s disease and related dementias (ADRD). These findings may help uncover the origins and risk factors for individual differences in brain aging that could help identify those most in need of intervention in midlife as well as help develop novel preventions to target ADRD before disease onset. The long-term goal of this line of research is to lower the global burden of ADRD by advancing our understanding of individual differences in brain aging.

Physical inactivity among US adults is high and increases with age, creating a public health concern because it

activity_code: K08
title: Identifying functional and anatomic mechanisms of glucose mobilization by cholecystokinin b receptor containing neurons in the ventromedial nucleus of the hypothalamus
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: Project Narrative
Stress-induced hyperglycemia is an important survival response that fuels the metabolic activity needed to fight
and escape threats; however severe or chronic activation of this response can lead to diabetes. This project
aims to define the neural circuits and metabolic mechanisms that regulate stress-induced glucose mobilization
and differentiate these from the circuits that mediate stress-induced behaviors. Once these mechanisms have
been identified, we will be able to develop therapeutics that can target metabolic outcomes of stress-induced
hyperglycemia without affecting behavioral responses.

application_id: 11136394
activity_code: K08
title: Mechanisms of Thyroid Autoimmunity in Checkpoint Immunotherapy
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA LOS ANGELES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Immune checkpoint inhibitors are a power new form of cancer treatment, but their benefits are limited by the
development of autoimmune side effects in many patients. Thyroid disease is one of the most common of these
autoimmune toxicities and has both overlapping and distinct features from other forms of thyroid autoimmune
disease (e.g. Hashimoto’s thyroiditis). Our project will investigate the cause of these autoimmune toxicities using
a mouse model and studies in patients with thyroid disease to advance our understanding of causes of thyroid
autoimmunity and so that therapies may be developed.

application_id: 11139520
activity_code: K08
title: Loss of the Exocrine Pancreas Improves Glucose Tolerance and Insulin Secretion
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: Diminished first-phase insulin secretion is the early and detrimental defect in type 2 diabetes.
The preliminary data show an increase in first-phase insulin secretion and improved glucose
tolerance to supranormal levels following exocrine pancreas ablation. This proposal aims to
understand the dramatic increase in the first-phase insulin secretion following the exocrine
tissue ablation, test the effect of exocrine pancreas loss in mice models of hyperglycemia, and
identify the underlying causes of the improved insulin secretion that accompany loss of
the exocrine tissue.

application_id: 11093532
activity_code: K00
title: Plasticity of cortical circuits in health, aging, and Alzheimer's disease
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY
phr: Project Narrative  The cerebral cortex is instrumental to all human and animal cognition, ranging from sensation to decision making to movement. The complexity of cortical circuits is a major obstacle to both understanding cortical function as a whole and to treating maladies that might afflict it, including numerous neurological and psychiatric disorders. The proposed studies will reveal how cortical circuits interact to execute cortical computations, how these interactions are impaired by aging and neurodegeneration, and how we can noninvasively stimulate cortical circuits to galvanize the brain to fight against pathologies caused by aging and Alzheimer’s disease.

title: Dissecting the mechanisms behind biphasic beta cell disease caused by mutations in HNF1alpha
org_name: CHILDREN'S HOSP OF PHILADELPHIA
phr: Project Narrative
The role of the pancreatic b-cell is to secrete insulin in response to glucose. Defects in this process can result
in inadequate insulin secretion causing diabetes or excessive insulin secretion causing hyperinsulinism. The
goal of this project is to understand how the same mutations in the transcription factor HNF1a can cause both
of these opposing diseases through disruption at different points along the insulin secretion cascade.

The goal of this proposal is to determine the cell types and circuits that are active in the nucleus accumbens

application_id: 10909239
activity_code: K08
title: Bridging the gap of late gestation human nephrogenesis using a non-human primate model
org_name: CINCINNATI CHILDRENS HOSP MED CTR
phr: Project Narrative
 Human nephrogenesis is complete by 34-36 weeks gestation, with 60% of nephrons forming during the
third trimester. Given improved survival of periviable gestational ages coupled with the fact that nephrogenesis
continues for no more than 40 days after premature birth, preterm infants are at the low end of nephron
endowment and at greater risk for chronic kidney disease and end stage renal disease in adulthood. Uncovering
the molecular mechanisms governing primate-specific late gestation nephrogenesis will have a positive impact
on the health of preterm infants by identifying potential druggable targets that will help extend this critical period
postnatally.

title: How dynamic interactions of RNA binding proteins control activity-dependent translation in neurons
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIV NEW YORK MORNINGSIDE

application_id: 11168758
activity_code: K08
title: Predicting Postoperative Acute Kidney Injury through Integration of Genetics and Electronic Health Records
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PUBLIC HEALTH RELEVANCE STATEMENT
Project Narrative: While acute kidney injury remains a major driver of morbidity and mortality
in the perioperative period, the ability to accurately identify patients at risk for this complication
remains limited despite decades of clinical research. The role of genetic information in
predicting acute kidney injury remains largely unknown and requires large scale electronic
health records and genomic datasets. We hypothesize that integrating patient data generated
in the perioperative period into the most advanced predictive genomic algorithms, will enable a
novel and personal risk assessment for postoperative acute kidney injury.

activity_code: K99
title: Investigating the principles of physiological and pathological vascular remodeling via 4D imaging of live mouse skin
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The proposed study will investigate fundamental cellular and molecular mechanisms of how the vascular network of the skin - the largest organ in our body - develops, maintains adult homeostasis, and repairs itself to support life-long function and health. I have established a state of the art live imaging approach to visualize, manipulate, and longitudinally track blood vessel cells in the live mouse skin, to interrogate for the first time the cellular dynamics underlying skin vascular remodeling and homeostasis. This research is of paramount importance, given the indispensable function of the vasculature to all tissues in our body and the yet unknown mechanisms that differentiate neonatal versus adult vascular regeneration.

activity_code: K00
title: Ceramide NanoLiposomes as a novel therapeutic for prostate cancer
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: Project Narrative  Sphingolipid metabolites are critical lipid-derived second messengers for oncogenesis and several sphingolipid-based therapeutics have shown great promise as cancer therapeutics. This research focuses on the regulation of sphingolipid metabolism by the androgen receptor and the use of ceramide nanoliposomes (CNL) as a novel therapeutic for prostate cancer patients. Moreover, the synergistic dynamics between epigenetics and lipid biology will be studied in tumors to uncover new therapeutic targets.

application_id: 11039723
activity_code: R36
title: Investigating the fetal origins of myeloid-biased hematopoietic stem cells
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE/RELEVANCE During aging, de novo production of blood cells is skewed towards the myeloid linage at the expense of the lymphoid, due to a relative increase of myeloid-biased hematopoietic stem cells (HSCs). The proposed research is aimed at understanding the initiation of myeloid-biased HSCs as they emerge early in development and increase in frequency throughout the aging process. The findings from this study will uncover the molecular programs that regulate HSC fate, provide insight into mechanisms underlying various pathologies observed in the elderly, including diminished adaptive immunity and the onset of several myeloid-related diseases, and form the first step to developing targeted therapies for rejuvenating the aged hematopoietic system.

application_id: 11257741
activity_code: K08
title: The Unfolded Protein Response in Murine and Pediatric Cholestatic Liver Diseases
org_name: LURIE CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL OF CHICAGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE:
Cholestatic liver diseases, including biliary atresia, are the leading indication for pediatric liver transplantation.
Evidence suggests that the unfolded protein response (UPR) is important in the progression of several adult liver
diseases, yet the UPR is understudied in pediatric cholestatic liver diseases. We aim to define the role of the
liver XBP1 pathway of the UPR postnatally and in models of cholestatic liver diseases in mice and human
patients with biliary atresia in order to develop UPR specific therapeutic interventions for pediatric patients with
cholestatic liver diseases.

title: Purdue PREP for Translational Biomedical Sciences

application_id: 11143300
activity_code: K08
title: Structural regulation of megalin recycling in the proximal tubule
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: Narrative
Megalin captures proteins from the urinary space to produce an essentially protein-free urine, thereby
protecting the kidney tubules from damage caused by proteinuria. Megalin recycles between cell surface and
intracellular compartments to carry out this function, but the molecular basis for this recycling is not well
understood. I will use structural approaches to study the molecular basis of its recycling.

phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The postpartum period represents a unique time in a women’s life when many women gain excess weight and make the transition to becoming overweight or obese. title: Machine learning-based methods for the analysis of microbial glycomes and proteomes in inflammatory bowel disease.
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative Inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) affects over 1.3 million Americans, but despite intensive research, the exact cause of IBD remains unclear. Interactions between the surface proteins and sugars of microbes and human cells have important effects on inflammation and its regulation, but their investigation is difficult due to the complex genetic and chemical environment of the gut. This project aims to design and apply advanced computational methods based on deep learning to enhance the discovery of significant changes in microbial proteins and sugars with a goal of gaining new insights into the development, progression, and treatment of IBD.

application_id: 10747988
activity_code: K00
title: Matrix Accumulation in the Metastatic Niche
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative After metastasis to the lungs, breast cancer cells are exposed to unique forces and matrix proteins that are not present at the primary tumor. My previous findings indicate that cyclic stretching, mimicking breathing dynamics within a 3D model of the early metastatic niche, can drive tumor cells into a state of growth arrest without widespread cell death. Because stromal cells are the key contributor of matrix composition and architecture, my goal is to evaluate the unique fibroblast subtypes conditioned by breast cancer cells at different stages of the metastatic cascade and characterize how the tissue changes they produce alter the metastatic progression of disseminated breast cancer cells.

phr: Black women are more likely to experience earlier onset of menopause, which serves as a marker for reproductive, somatic, and overall health. The proposed project aims to characterize and examine the extent to which environmental hazards are associated with early menopause among Black women. This work seeks to promote a more inclusive framework for menopause research by centering and prioritizing the menopausal experience of Black women.

application_id: 11082245
activity_code: K08
title: Elucidating the role of mechanical forces in diabetic wound healing
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: NARRATIVE
Diabetes is associated with significant impairments in wound healing which can be attributed in part to alterations
in the “cellular ecology” of local cell populations; however, the role of external mechanical forces in modulating
these cell population dynamics within the wound environment remains poorly understood. Our fundamental
hypothesis is that local tissue mechanical forces contribute to the disruption of cellular ecology in diabetic wound
healing and that mitigation of these forces can improve healing. This proposal focuses on identifying the
mechanisms leading to altered subpopulations in diabetic human and mouse wound healing, and using real
world data (RWD) to translate these findings to develop clinical strategies to treat non-healing diabetic wounds.

title: Intestinal mitochondrial dysfunction and the gut-brain-immune axis in models of Parkinson's Disease

application_id: 10884231
activity_code: K08
title: Dissecting the Human Diabetic Bone Marrow Niche
org_name: UNIV OF MASSACHUSETTS MED SCH WORCESTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Approximately 30% of all diabetics develop a diabetic foot ulcer, resulting in a lower extremity amputation every 30 seconds worldwide because of a life-threatening infection caused by diabetic foot ulcers. An important driver in non- healing diabetic wounds is impairment in the innate immune system; however, there is limited understanding of the mechanism by which diabetes impairs the innate immune system. I aim to identify the mechanisms underlying diabetes-related impaired innate immune dysfunction that prevents wound healing to facilitate the discovery of novel therapeutics that may prevent amputations.

activity_code: K08
title: Understanding the physiology and pathophysiology of kidney-derived vasopressin
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Vasopressin is a hormone that regulates water balance, blood pressure and contributes to progression of non-diabetic, diabetic, and polycystic kidney disease (PKD), through mechanisms that are still unclear. Vasopressin production was thought to be limited to the brain, but we have evidence that it is also made in the kidney. This project aims to understand the role that kidney-derived vasopressin plays in health and disease using cells and transgenic mouse models with a targeted deletion of vasopressin in the kidney.

application_id: 11059951
activity_code: K08
title: Dissecting circuits underlying loss of control relevant to binge eating
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Binge eating is a maladaptive behavior widely present in the general population that is strongly associated with
obesity risk. Loss of control over eating, a core diagnostic feature, is associated with difficulties disengaging
from eating, and research examining feeding offset is needed to dissect circuit abnormalities. This project will
characterize neural activity patterns contributing to feeding offset to ultimately develop improved treatments for
aberrant eating contributing to obesity risk.

application_id: 11176923
activity_code: K08
title: Digital Tools for Precision Nephrology
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The proposal aims to establish robust phenotyping pipelines for early detection of Collagen Type IV-Associated
Nephropathy (COL4A-AN) using electronic health record (EHR) data. To overcome diagnostic challenges of
this prevalent monogenic form of chronic kidney disease (CKD), the study focuses on utilizing Natural
Language Processing (NLP) to extract nuanced phenotypic traits from unstructured clinical narratives and build
accurate and portable prediction models. These models lay the foundation for developing effective EHR-based
decision support tools that enable clinicians to identify patients with genetic CKD subtypes, even when
unfamiliar with specific genetic syndromes.

application_id: 11321192
activity_code: K08
title: Elucidating the Role of NAD+ and PARylation in Nephron Endowment
org_name: UT SOUTHWESTERN MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Pregnant mothers frequently have health concerns that reduce the level of a nutrient called
NAD+ in their bodies. This may affect the development of baby’s kidneys so that they are born
with less kidney filters, called nephrons -- a phenomenon that has health risks for the child that
last into adulthood. The goal of this application is to determine how NAD+ affects the
development of the baby kidneys and how it may be possible to enhance kidney development
so that children may have healthier kidneys throughout their lifespans.

application_id: 10783878
activity_code: K08
title: Longitudinal Associations of Posttraumatic Stress Disorder with Incident Type 2 Diabetes and Poor Glycemic Control in a Large National Cohort
org_name: BOSTON UNIVERSITY MEDICAL CAMPUS
phr: Project Narrative The proposed project leverages a unique longitudinal cohort of over 6 million individuals to examine the extent to which posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD), a psychiatric disorder that can develop after trauma exposure, is associated with incident type 2 diabetes and poor glycemic control in individuals who developed type 2 diabetes during the study period. This study also will investigate the extent to which associations between PTSD and type 2 diabetes differ across sex, race, and ethnicity. This work has the potential to advance our knowledge about factors that contribute to type 2 diabetes risk and ultimately may have implications for tailoring diabetes-related prevention and intervention efforts for individuals with PTSD.

application_id: 10905843
activity_code: R36
title: The Involvement of the Cerebellum in Sensory Over-Responsivity in Autism
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA LOS ANGELES
phr: Project Narrative This project aims to examine the relationship between functional cerebellar atypicalities and sensory over- responsivity (SOR) in autism spectrum disorder (ASD). Findings from this study will provide new insight into the involvement of the cerebellum in ASD symptomatology and help to characterize the complex neural mechanisms underlying SOR. Results derived from this research will inform future behavioral and psychopharmacological treatment strategies to target sensory symptoms in ASD youth.

application_id: 11005727
activity_code: K08
title: Diversity generation in the gastrointestinal microbiome
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA LOS ANGELES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Factors that promote microbial colonization in complex and dynamic communities such as the human gastrointestinal microbiome are poorly understood, but knowledge of their functions could lead to therapeutic interventions that reverse dysbiotic states. We predict that rapid gene diversifiers, such as Diversity Generating Retroelements (DGRs), provide bacteria with selective fitness advantages through rapid evolution of ligand binding proteins that facilitate colonization within the gut microbiome. By testing this prediction, we expect to generate a toolkit for engineering adaptative systems in beneficial microbes that allow efficient engraftment in the gut microbiome.

application_id: 11067749
activity_code: K08
title: Transcriptomic Diagnosis and Staging of Sepsis-Associated Acute Kidney Injury
org_name: INDIANA UNIVERSITY INDIANAPOLIS
phr: Sepsis is a lethal condition commonly complicated by kidney failure (AKI) yet, despite decades of investigation we lack reliable predictors and treatment. In the present study we will utilize mouse models of sepsis associated AKI (sAKI) to establish blood-based biomarkers correlated with critical stages in the pathogenesis of renal tissue injury which will be used to diagnose and stage human AKI blood samples. The immediate outcome of this work will be the development of a liquid biopsy to diagnose and stage human sAKI.

title: Genetic Regulation of IgE Glycosylation
org_name: UT SOUTHWESTERN MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Project Narrative Allergic diseases constitute a major health burden and have a definite genetic component; however, the genes regulating allergy are largely unexplored. Recently, we conducted a classic genetic screen of allergy, which yielded several novel phenotypes of IgE production including benadryl, which rendered mice resistant to anaphylaxis. This proposal seeks to characterize how a mannose producing enzyme regulates IgE production, glycosylation, and function in allergic disease models.

title: Leveraging social media to promote body positivity among Latinx adolescents
org_name: NEW YORK UNIVERSITY SCHOOL OF MEDICINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Body positivity, the promotion and acceptance of diverse body sizes and appearances, is one approach to mitigating weight stigma, which is associated with depression, anxiety, disordered eating, reduced physical activity, and weight gain. Latinx adolescents have higher rates obesity than their white counterparts and are at risk of experiencing multiple forms of stigmatization. This study will assess the impact of a social media campaign that promotes body positivity on weight stigma and mental health among Latinx youth, compared to a control campaign.

A thorough elucidation of the transcriptional determinants and molecular heterogeneity of Barrett’s esophagus
will uncover novel avenues to detect and treat these precancerous metaplasias to ultimately prevent the devel-
opment of esophageal adenocarcinoma. Here, I will investigate the roles of two pioneering transcription factors
involved in GI tract patterning, SOX2 and CDX2, in Barrett’s esophagus development and maintenance using
novel genetic mouse models and my unique biobank of human-derived Barrett’s esophagus organoids. These
innovative studies will deﬁne a stepwise transcriptional reprograming towards an intestinal phenotype seen in
Barrett’s esophagus, and establish a multi-omic pipeline to further elucidate the heterogeneity and molecular

title: Organellar Na+/H+ Exchangers and Intracellular pH Regulation in Schizophrenia Brain
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ALABAMA AT BIRMINGHAM

application_id: 10896347
activity_code: F30
title: Age-dependent differences in the immune response to human metapneumovirus
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: III. PROJECT NARRATIVE Human metapneumovirus (HMPV) is a debilitating infection and leading cause of severe respiratory infections in young children, the elderly, and the immunocompromised. The specific mechanism behind the impaired immune response to HMPV in the elderly leading to severe respiratory disease is incompletely understood. In this proposal, I aim to elucidate the age-specific differences in the immune response to HMPV in the lung utilizing the aged murine model to identify candidate molecules/pathways that ultimately can be clinically translatable and targeted in an HMPV vaccine as well as the management and treatment of related respiratory viruses afflicting the elderly.

application_id: 11176049
activity_code: K08
title: Endoplasmic Reticulum Stress Controls Innate Lymphoid Cells in Intestinal Inflammation
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 Innate lymphoid cells reside on the mucosal surface and orchestrate tissue homeostasis; however, their
regulatory mechanisms and clinical significance in intestinal inflammation remain unclear. Here, we intend to
unravel the mechanistic role of IRE1α-XBP1-mediated endoplasmic reticulum stress in innate lymphoid cells and
their implications in inflammatory bowel disease, using a comprehensive approach involving multicellular
organoid culture, mouse models, and human samples. After completion of these studies, I anticipate I will be
well-prepared to establish an independent research program and be highly competitive for R-series grants.

application_id: 10909057
activity_code: F31
title: Applying a multidimensional measure of human mobility to understand drivers of HIV incidence in Rakai, Uganda
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Mobile populations experience higher rates of HIV acquisition and inadequate access to health services in sub- Saharan Africa, yet HIV programs are still ill-equipped to meet the needs of specific mobile groups. Since population movement also facilitates the spatial spread of the virus, HIV programs working towards elimination must target and tailor services to mobile populations at the greatest risk of HIV acquisition and onward transmission. This study will characterize distinct forms of mobility in south-central Uganda, an area with substantial population movement, and relate each to critical gaps in the HIV response.

activity_code: K08
title: Therapeutic Targeting of Transcription-Driven Epigenetic Defects in SF3B1 Mutant Myelodysplastic syndromes (MDS)
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE: Myelodysplastic syndrome (MDS) is a common blood disorder in which the bone marrow fails to produce enough blood cells, resulting in anemia, infection and bleeding. Although we know what genetic changes (mutations) cause MDS, the precise mechanisms that cause MDS are still unclear, and this has prevented development of effective therapies. This proposal seeks to improve our understanding of molecular pathways of MDS, especially those affecting processing of ribonucleic acid (RNA); it is hoped that this work will help develop novel therapies and offer a path to cure for patients with MDS.

application_id: 11175841
activity_code: K08
title: Mechanisms of mechanosensation and inflammation-induced hypersensitivityin colon innervating DRG sensory neurons
org_name: HARVARD MEDICAL SCHOOL
phr: Project Narrative  Gastrointestinal (GI) sensation is necessary under physiologic and pathologic conditions, and during inflammation, such as inflammatory bowel disease (IBD), mediates abdominal pain. The dorsal root ganglia (DRG) sensory neurons provide the sensory innervation to the colon and allow for pain responses. Here, I propose to uncover how subtypes of DRG neurons respond to inflammation and understand the mechanisms through which they mediate mechanosensation and inflammation-induced mechanical hypersensitivity, which will be critical for identifying therapeutic targets to treat IBD- associated abdominal pain.

org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA-IRVINE
phr: Project Narrative Increased infectious disease outbreaks, highlighted by the current COVID-19 pandemic, serves as a constant reminder of the importance of diagnostics for infectious diseases and the large diagnostic gap in our response arsenal. Whispering Gallery Mode (WGM) devices that consist of microbubble resonators are a novel class of highly sensitive optical sensor with simple fluid handling. title: Development of N-acyl fluspirilene mediated proteasome enhancement for the treatment of neurodegenerative diseases
org_name: MICHIGAN STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: Neurodegenerative diseases, including Alzheimer’s disease (AD) and Alzheimer’s disease related dementias (ADRD) are characterized by accumulation of intrinsically disordered proteins (IDPs) and impairment of proteasome-mediated protein degradation. application_id: 10805186
activity_code: K08
title: Rapid Anatomic and Quantitative MR Imaging of the Skull Base and Face
org_name: BRIGHAM AND WOMEN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Our goal is to devise clinically optimal MRI synthetic image contrasts for the skull base and face that can be tailored for field strength, gradient capabilities, spatial resolution, and desired imaging time. We propose to generate novel qualitative MRI contrasts that optimize clinically-relevant contrast-to-noise and simultaneously acquire relaxometry biomarkers that are co-registered to the anatomic images at the voxel level with markedly reduced scan times compared with current standard-of-care skull base and face MR imaging. We also propose to develop a novel pulse sequence that incorporates scanner gradient strength as a parameter to further decrease scan times and to evaluate these pulse sequences across 1.5T, 3T, and 7T field strengths.

application_id: 11158751
activity_code: K08
title: Bladders and biomes: Environmental compounds as modifiers of microbiomes, metabolomes, and urothelium
org_name: OHIO STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Bladder cancer is strongly linked to environmental chemical exposures including polycyclic aromatic
hydrocarbons (PAHs), but the mechanisms underlying these links remain unclear, and not all exposed
individuals develop bladder cancer. PAHs like benzo[a]pyrene (BaP) are found in automobile exhaust and
tobacco smoke, and differential metabolism of these chemicals by host-associated microbes could be a
contributing factor to bladder cancer pathogenesis; therefore, the aim of this proposal is to define gut and urine
microbial metabolism of BaP and determine how this metabolism affects bladder cells. This work impacts
public health by establishing a novel chemical-microbe-host framework for evaluating bladder health within the
broader context of bladder cancer development.

application_id: 10886620
activity_code: K08
title: Investigating mechanosensation in the glaucomatous optic nerve head
org_name: UPSTATE MEDICAL UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Glaucoma, a leading cause of irreversible blindness worldwide, is projected to affect 100 million individuals by the year 2040. It is defined as an age-associated optic neuropathy characterized by retinal ganglion cell death; a barrier to treating glaucoma is that the mechanisms underlying cell susceptibility in this disease are very poorly understood. This proposal investigates mechanisms by which astrocytes, the primary support cells for retinal ganglion cells, sense their mechanical environment in glaucoma; the data obtained from these studies will help to develop novel therapies to treat this difficult disease.

activity_code: K08
title: Mechanisms of Photoreceptor Contribution to Retinal Inflammation in Diabetic Retinopathy
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Project Narrative Diabetic retinopathy blinds approximately 12% of diabetic patients each year and its incidence is expected to increase. While there are currently no therapeutic strategies directed at its early stages, there is accumulating evidence that retinal photoreceptors are involved in the pathogenesis of diabetic retinopathy and are major contributors to diabetes-induced local inflammation in the retina. This project aims to study the responses of photoreceptors to hyperglycemia and interventions that address this pathway with the potential to be effective in the early stages of diabetic retinopathy to prevent the development of vision-threatening pathology.

application_id: 10806675
activity_code: K08
title: Mechanisms of proinflammatory glial activation in retinal degeneration
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: Activation of the innate immune system has been recognized as a significant component of numerous forms of neurodegenerative disease. This project aims to characterize the factors influencing microglia, key mediators of inflammation in the retina, to switch from homeostatic to proinflammatory functions as part of disease progression in age-related macular degeneration. Using novel methods for gene knockout of key pathways in human stem-cell derived microglia-like cells, single-cell RNA sequencing analysis, and highly multiplexed molecular mapping in tissue with digital histologic analysis, this work will identify tissue microenvironment factors and immune checkpoints representing novel therapeutic targets for retinal degeneration.

application_id: 11089437
activity_code: K08
title: Lipid Trafficking and Metabolism in the Retinal Pigment Epithelium
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE:
Degeneration of the retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) underlies age-related macular degeneration (AMD), the
leading cause of irreversible vision loss in the developed world. Avenues to improve RPE function in the dry form
of AMD are desperately needed, as the disease currently has no FDA-approved treatments. Here, we explore
the role of a lipid storage organelle, termed a lipid droplet, in maintaining the RPE’s health and function, with
implications for developing novel therapeutics for dry AMD.

activity_code: K01
title: Culturally Tailored Nutrition Therapy To Improve Dietary Adherence of Type 2 Diabetes Patients in Benin, Africa
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ARIZONA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Most patients with type 2 diabetes (T2D) in Benin have difficulty adjusting their diet to reach their target
glycated hemoglobin level (HbA1c), increasing their risk of complications. Through training and research
activities, a culturally tailored medical nutrition therapy (MNT) will be used to improve dietary adherence and
health outcomes, thus reducing the disease burden of this high-risk population. If successful, this study may be
included in future multi-component self-management interventions and be of immediate benefit to clinics.

application_id: 11259556
activity_code: K08
title: Human Ocular Surface Electrophysiology
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Dry eye disease is a multifactorial disease of the ocular surface characterized by impaired tear film
homeostasis accompanied by ocular symptoms that affects approximately 6.8% of adults in the USA, with a
global prevalence as high as 50%. Despite this significant disease burden, there are currently just four FDA-
approved therapies for dry eye disease, each targeting only the inflammatory pathway and having limited
efficacy. The proposed research will advance novel dry eye disease therapeutics that promote tear fluid
secretion by targeting ion transport proteins on epithelial cells lining the ocular surface.

activity_code: K08
title: Elucidation of the Molecular Mechanisms Driving Genetically-Induced High Myopia
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  The main aims of this study are to: 1) Determine the extent that Smad2/3 activation contributes to myopia progression, structural changes indicative of pathological myopia (PM), and retinal degeneration in mouse models of Marfan syndrome (MFS) and Rbp3-mediated retinitis pigmentosa (RP); 2) Determine a role for TGFβ- dependent mitogen-activated protein kinase (MAPK) signaling in myopia progression, PM changes and retinal degeneration in MFS and Rbp3-deficient mice. The scientific goal of this proposal is to elucidate the mechanisms driving excess eye growth, its structural complications, and concomitant retinal degeneration in two etiologically- distinct causes of monogenic myopia to identify and target common drivers of disease progression.

application_id: 10759461
activity_code: F30
title: Formation of Retinyl-Opsins by Retinyl Formate as Molecular Shades Against Light-Induced Retinal Damage
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA-IRVINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Stargardt disease is the most common inherited macular degeneration in children that leads to progressive vision loss and eventually to legal blindness by age 50. The macular degeneration seen in Stargardt disease is caused by impaired clearance of toxic molecules that are released by light-sensitive opsin proteins found in photoreceptors when they capture light. In this proposal, I will investigate whether irreversibly blocking the ability of opsins to release these toxic species is a viable strategy to providing long-lasting protection to the retina from light-induced damage, and delay the onset and progression of macular degeneration in STGD1.

application_id: 10767328
activity_code: K08
title: Human Ocular Surface Electrophysiology
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Dry eye disease is a multifactorial disease of the ocular surface characterized by impaired tear film homeostasis accompanied by ocular symptoms that affects approximately 6.8% of adults in the USA, with a global prevalence as high as 50%. Despite this significant disease burden, there are currently just four FDA- approved therapies for dry eye disease, each targeting only the inflammatory pathway and having limited efficacy. The proposed research will advance novel dry eye disease therapeutics that promote tear fluid secretion by targeting ion transport proteins on epithelial cells lining the ocular surface.

application_id: 10896129
activity_code: K08
title: The Role of Mechanosensitive Ion Channels in Glaucoma
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: Glaucoma is a progressive, blinding disease for which intraocular pressure is the most significant risk factor, and yet, the mechanisms of how the eye senses pressure remain largely unknown. This project will investigate the role of mechanosensitive ion channels in glaucoma, and how human variants in mechanosensitive ion channel genes may be associated with glaucoma risk. These studies will provide fundamental insights into the mechanosensitive responses of retinal cells at the molecular and physiological level and will lay the foundation for the development of new neuroprotective therapies for glaucoma.

application_id: 11105771
activity_code: K08
title: Role of a craniosynostosis associated fibroblast growth factor receptor mutation in extraocular muscles
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 Apert syndrome is a devastating genetic syndrome with abnormal skull development and strabismus, or
eye misalignment. This research proposal will use a model for Apert syndrome to examine why patients have
abnormal muscles around the eye and what might be causing their abnormal vision using state-of-the-art
techniques in muscle biomechanics and genetics. Gene therapy will also be tested using this model to investigate
innovative therapies for patients with strabismus in Apert syndrome.

application_id: 11118943
activity_code: K23
title: Staphylococcus aureus Cardiac Device Infections - From the Vascular Viewpoint
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Staphylococcus aureus is the leading cause of cardiac device infections, a devastating complication that
jeopardizes the function of these lifesaving devices. This research proposal will build novel bioengineered
model systems to examine how specific patient and bacterial factors interact to influence why some people
with S. aureus bloodstream infection and cardiac devices (pacemakers, artificial heart valves) develop an
infection of their cardiac device while others do not. The results from this research can be used to develop new
diagnostics to identify patients at high risk for developing a cardiac device infection or new therapeutics to
augment a person's ability to fight these infections.

application_id: 10909950
activity_code: K08
title: Mechanisms driving endothelial angiopoietin-2 expression and vascular dysfunction during pediatric sepsis
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ALABAMA AT BIRMINGHAM
phr: Sepsis remains a significant health concern for children, with a hospital mortality rate of 25% globally. A key driver of organ injury and death in children with sepsis is vascular disease, although the discrete mechanisms driving this pathology remain elusive. This proposal will uncover mechanisms that promote vascular dysfunction to facilitate the development of successful therapies for children suffering with sepsis.

application_id: 11311274
activity_code: K08
title: Metabolic impact of Intralipid on synaptic function as a mechanism of resuscitation in local anesthetic systemic toxicity
org_name: WEILL MEDICAL COLL OF CORNELL UNIV
phr: Project Narrative
Intravenous lipid emulsions (ILEs) are an antidote to local anesthetic systemic toxicity (LAST), a life-threatening
syndrome caused by anesthetic overdose that often presents as seizures and cardiovascular instability.
Intralipid®, the prototypical ILE of long-chain triglycerides derived from soybean oil, and other emulsions reverse
local anesthetic-induced mitochondrial dysfunction in cardiac models, but this mechanism has not been explored
in the context of central nervous system dysfunction. My preliminary data demonstrates surprisingly that
Intralipid® can power neuronal synaptic function in the absence of glucose, and my proposal will systematically
investigate the metabolic effects of ILE components to identify the mixture most capable of reversing
neurotoxicity in ...

application_id: 11007260
activity_code: K08
title: Mechanisms of cardiomyocyte dysfunction in pediatric septic shock
org_name: CINCINNATI CHILDRENS HOSP MED CTR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE One in four children with septic shock die despite receiving intensive care and antibiotics, and cardiac dysfunction seems to contribute to this high mortality rate. No treatments exist for cardiac dysfunction in sepsis, and our goal is to better understand the causes of cardiac injury in children with septic shock to facilitate development of new medications to help these children recover and survive. We will study these processes using human cardiac cells and human serum, rather than animal models, to improve the ability to translate discoveries back to the bedside to improve patient care.

application_id: 10883928
activity_code: K08
title: The Role of DHCR7 in Endotoxemia
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Sepsis, or dysregulated host response to infection, is one of the most common causes of hospitalization in the U.S. with a high mortality rate. DHCR7, an enzyme involved in cholesterol synthesis, was associated with mortality in human sepsis, and fish treated with a DHCR7 inhibitor were protected from toxicity in a zebrafish model that recapitulates features of human sepsis. This proposal will clarify the mechanisms by which inhibition of this enzyme exerts a protective effect, with implications for the development of human sepsis therapeutics.

application_id: 10760239
activity_code: F30
title: Defining Inflammatory Triggers that Induce Diverse Subtypes of Gastric Metaplasia
org_name: SAINT LOUIS UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  Gastric cancer is a major cause of cancer related death worldwide and screening practices are not routine in the US, leading to late diagnosis and poor outcomes. To improve accurate diagnoses and medical intervention, different types of metaplasia need to be molecularly defined and the inflammatory signals that trigger development of potentially high-risk metaplastic cells need to be established. The proposed research aims to define types of metaplasia that 1. Arise in models of Helicobacter pylori (Hp) infection compared to models of autoimmune gastritis (AIG), 2. Arise in human Hp compared to AIG patients, and 3. Are selectively induced/expanded by inflammatory cytokines, to ultimately improve methods in gastric cancer early detection and prevention.

application_id: 11241711
activity_code: F32
title: The impact of aberrant m5C mRNA modification on translation initiation and cancer
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE:
 Aberrant mRNA modifications result from overexpressed methyltransferases in cancers,
leading to dysregulation of protein synthesis and more aggressive tumors with worse patient
outcomes. The project aims to identify aberrant m5C modifications on 5' untranslated regions of
mRNAs and determine how they impact translation initiation and protein expression. The
findings could generate new leads for development of novel targeted therapeutic strategies for
cancers with aberrant m5C methylation due to elevated expression levels of mRNA
methyltransferases.

phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Iron regulatory proteins and Argonaute-2 are RNA-binding proteins that respectively coordinate cellular iron
regulation and microRNA-mediated regulation, mechanisms essential for the proper execution of early
embryonic development. The objective of this project is to dissect the specific roles of post-transcriptional
regulation by microRNAs and iron regulatory proteins on cell fate decisions in early mammalian development
and to build in vitro and in vivo biosensor platforms to model cellular iron utilization in early mammalian
development. A comprehensive understanding of the dynamic relationships of iron regulatory proteins,
microRNAs, and their functional targets during this critical developmental window is needed and will provide a
roadmap for functional rewiring in s...

application_id: 11170500
activity_code: K08
title: Investigating the Downstream Mechanisms Contributing to Beta Cell Failure in Offspring Exposed to Maternal Obesity
org_name: INDIANA UNIVERSITY INDIANAPOLIS
phr: Project Narrative:
Children born to obese mothers are at increased risk of developing type 2 diabetes at a younger age
and preclinical models demonstrate the presence of worsened pancreatic beta cell dysfunction in
males when compared to female offspring, but the mechanistic pathways underlying this observation
are unclear. The goal of this proposal is to delineate the role of Reg3g-mediated heparan sulfate
polymerization as a mechanistic pathway amenable to treatment of sex-specific islet dysfunction in
offspring of obese mice. This proposal will address a critical gap in our understanding of the
developmental programming of offspring islet dysfunction induced by maternal obesity and has the
potential to inform novel therapeutic strategies.

application_id: 11127557
activity_code: K08
title: The development of a systematic approach to harness real-world evidence for the evaluation of medication safety and effectiveness in children
org_name: BRIGHAM AND WOMEN'S HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative
There is a paucity of data to inform safe and effective prescribing practices in children. Real-world evidence
(RWE) studies using data from routine clinical practice have the potential to generate high-quality evidence
that could guide prescribing when randomized controlled trials do not but are underdeveloped in pediatrics.
This study aims to develop a rigorous framework for RWE studies such that they can reliably address key
evidence gaps of medication safety and effectiveness in pediatrics.

activity_code: F30
title: Development of a Surgical Drug Delivery System for Enhancement of CAR T Cell Activity
org_name: BOSTON UNIVERSITY (CHARLES RIVER CAMPUS)
phr: Project Narrative This proposal describes the training and mentoring of Mr. Eric Bressler and his research project to treat solid tumors using a novel biomaterial in conjunction with a split and programmable chimeric antigen receptor (CAR) T cell system. Using this system, he describes a method for overcoming barriers to efficacy of CAR T cells in solid tumors, specifically poor T cell persistence in the tumor microenvironment, lack of specific antigens, and treatment-limiting adverse effects. The capability of this novel therapeutic system to promote CAR T cell proliferation, prevent antigen escape, and mitigate adverse events will be evaluated.

org_name: BOSTON CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) is a major health concern that affects up to 10% of all reproductive-aged women; however, the treatment of PCOS remains limited by an incomplete understanding of the pathophysiology of the disorder despite extensive physiologic and genetic studies. The goal of this study is to dissect the underlying causes of PCOS by implicating causal biological mechanisms underlying the pathophysiology of PCOS, as well as PCOS-associated features in women, men, and children. The results of this proposal will allow for the deconstruction of the causes of PCOS into distinct subgroups, which will pave the way for a precision-medicine approach to the diagnosis, risk stratification, and treatment of PCOS in women and its associated conditions in men and child...

title: Control of intracellular Shigella in human macrophages.
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: Macrophages are a crucial arm of innate immunity against bacterial infections. The goal of this proposal is: 1)
to define the mechanisms in which human macrophages kill intracellular bacteria, and 2) to define the impact

macrophages. As bacteria are increasingly resistant to available antibiotics, these studies will offer valuable
insights into bacterial pathogenesis and immune evasion and identify new targets for therapeutic approaches.

title: The effects of alcohol metabolism on hepatic and cardiac energy state and function
org_name: UT SOUTHWESTERN MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Project Narrative Alcohol metabolism may reduce the cellular energy state in the heart, which can alter cardiac function. The following proposal will make meaningful advancements in our understanding of the effects chronic alcohol consumption and abuse has on metabolic function in the heart and liver. This project will also investigate novel mechanisms that when impaired, or ablated, may mitigate the alcohol induced alterations in the cardiac energy state.

title: Comprehensive Pediatric Phenotyping for Evidence-Based Diagnosis in Genetic Disease
org_name: CHILDREN'S HOSP OF PHILADELPHIA
phr: Project Narrative
Many of over 7000 rare genetic diseases are diagnosed based partially on our understanding of their clinical
features or a set of diagnostic criteria that appear in combination. We will test whether a more rigorous scientific
approach to these criteria based on actual patient data from over a decade of clinical evaluations of children can
improve our ability to make rare disease diagnoses. From these same data, we will develop machine learning
algorithms that may one day assist non-specialists in recognizing these rare diseases in their patients.

activity_code: F30
title: Characterizing the functional heterogeneity of the mouse paralaminar nucleus
org_name: GEORGETOWN UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The goal of the research proposed in this fellowship is to determine the functional heterogeneity of neurons in the paralaminar nucleus (PL), an understudied region of the amygdala implicated in neuropsychiatric disorders. The experiments will use a mouse model to classify the diversity of mature PL neurons by linking their physiological properties, morphological features, and input/output circuitry. The results will provide crucial knowledge for understanding the role of the paralaminar nucleus in amygdala function and behavior.

application_id: 10874422
activity_code: K08
title: Cardiovascular disease, preeclampsia, and microchimerism
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The proposed studies seek to understand whether a small number of fetal cells transferred to the mother
during pregnancy (fetal microchimerism) is associated with later-life cardiovascular disease (CVD) in women
with a history of preeclampsia. In parallel, we will expand the usability of an advanced next-generation
sequencing approach for improved microchimerism evaluation. This work will modernize the techniques for
microchimerism studies while investigating a possible contributor to CVD in a unique cohort of women with the
potential to identify novel diagnostics and therapeutic interventions to limit the burden of disease.

application_id: 10807958
activity_code: F31
title: Spatial organization of phenotypically diverse cells during collective migration
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Phenotypic diversity allows pathogenic microbes and cancer cells to survive drug treatments and form permanent drug-resistance mutations, which poses a major challenge in medicine. It is not fully understood how phenotypic diversity contributes to collective biological functions, such as in biofilm formation and cancer metastasis. This project aims to address this general question by investigating bacterial collective migration, exploring how the diversity of phenotypes enables groups of bacteria to adapt across various physical environments and mediate pathogenesis.

title: IMSD: An Initiative to Maximize Student Development in Biomedical Research at MU
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MISSOURI-COLUMBIA
phr: We will develop a predoctoral training program that will provide opportunities for research training leading to a PhD degree to predoctoral graduate students from groups that are underrepresented in the biomedical workforce. title: Supplement:  Developing Nanoparticle Drug Delivery Systems for Venous Malformations
org_name: BOSTON CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE An effective therapy with reduced risks and side effects for treating venous malformations is greatly needed. application_id: 11175315
activity_code: K08
title: Population Assessments of Aggregate Genetic Risk for Dilated Cardiomyopathy
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The current proposal seeks to uncover the population genetic determinants of dilated cardiomyopathy (DCM),
the leading global cause of heart transplantation. With growing availability of genetic data, an improved
understanding of DCM genetics may permit genomic risk prognostication as part of routine clinical practice,
enabling early identification and management of patients at risk for DCM, and thereby mitigating risk – and the
potential need for heart transplantation – before the onset of disease. Furthermore, identification of putative
drug targets through genetic discovery may motivate the development of novel therapeutics capable of altering
the poor prognosis that is currently associated with DCM.

title: The regulation and function of platelet FcARIIA in sepsis
org_name: UTAH STATE HIGHER EDUCATION SYSTEM--UNIVERSITY OF UTAH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Sepsis is a common syndrome of dysregulated immune responses to infection that occurs in all ages and

potential to uncover biology to lead to therapeutic improvements for the treatment of sepsis.

application_id: 11257312
activity_code: K08
title: Mechanisms of platelet stimulation in hemolysis
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: NARRATIVE
High-mobility group box 1 (HMGB1) has a recognized role in platelet activation and thrombosis
formation, but its mechanism of platelet activation in sickle cell disease remains unclear. I propose
that circulating hemoglobin oxidizes HMGB1 to trigger an intracellular platelet signaling cascade
that potentiates platelet mitochondrial reactive oxygen species (mtROS) and platelet activation.
Investigating this novel mechanism of platelet activation will improve our understanding of the
mechanisms linking hemolysis and inflammation to platelet activation and thrombosis, and
establish a foundation for developing and applying HMGB1-inhibitors to prevent thrombotic
complications in sickle cell disease.

application_id: 11052544
activity_code: K08
title: The Role of Lung Megakaryocytes in Airway Disease after Neonatal Hyperoxia
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ROCHESTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The incidence of premature birth (delivery more than three weeks early) is increasing worldwide and many
infants born extremely preterm (less than 28 weeks' gestation) suffer long-term lung disease resulting from
injury due to oxygen therapy and mechanical ventilation. These former preterm infants experience lifelong
breathing problems, including wheezing and increased respiratory infection severity which puts a high burden
on the health care system because of the need for continued care and rehospitalization. This project will
contribute to our understanding of reasons why preterm infants and their oxygen exposures at birth leave them
vulnerable to worse respiratory infections and wheezing later in life, in attempts to alleviate this burden.

application_id: 10864939
activity_code: K08
title: Using Induced Pluripotent Stem Cells to Characterize Cystic Fibrosis
org_name: BOSTON CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative Cystic Fibrosis (CF) remains the most lethal genetic disease in the Caucasian population, caused by mutation in the CFTR gene, with a wide range of disease severity. Using cutting-edge techniques of stem cell culturing, differentiation into airway epithelium, and gene-editing, this proposal seeks to establish the role of alternative ion channels in patients with extreme disease manifestations. The findings will clarify disease mechanisms in CF, facilitate individualized treatments for patients, and advance use of stem cell- derived airway epithelium to model lung diseases.

title: Building Brain Health Research Capacity in Rwanda: Neurocognition and Neurobehavior Across the Lifespan
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Collectively, neurologic and psychiatric disorders account for over a quarter of the global burden of disease, with disproportionately high rates of illness in low-middle income countries where expert resources are scarce. application_id: 11257270
activity_code: K08
title: Long noncoding RNA-mediated regulation of T-cell alloimmunity
org_name: INDIANA UNIVERSITY INDIANAPOLIS
phr: Project Narrative
Acute graft-versus-host disease (aGVHD) is a major complication of allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell
transplantation, and improved therapies for aGVHD are needed. To develop potential new targets for
improving aGVHD, our group recently identified a novel T cell-enriched long noncoding RNA, LINC00402, that
uniquely promotes allogeneic T-cell proliferation. We now seek to determine LINC00402’s function and
molecular mechanism in both mice and humans.

application_id: 11060933
activity_code: K08
title: Mitochondrial redox regulation of adrenal steroidogenesis
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Excess adrenal steroid production is increasingly recognized as a common cause of hypertension and
cardiovascular disease, with medical therapies that remain suboptimal and biological mechanisms that remain
incompletely defined. We have developed novel methods for deeply interrogating the proteome and
bioenergetics of adrenal mitochondria and have obtained preliminary data for several redox pathways that are
uniquely enriched in adrenal mitochondria. The proposed research will test the concept that dysfunction of
adrenal mitochondrial redox pathways can be a pathogenic driver of dysregulated aldosterone and cortisol
production relevant for common hypertension and cardiovascular disease.

activity_code: K08
title: Impaired Pneumococcal Antibody Function and Exacerbations of Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ALABAMA AT BIRMINGHAM
phr: Project Narrative Exacerbations of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) are a major source of morbidity, mortality, and healthcare costs. Bacterial infections are a common cause of exacerbations, however it is not known whether individuals with frequent exacerbations have specific defects in antibody function against bacteria. Investigating antibody-mediated immune function in COPD could lead to the recognition of novel risk factors for exacerbations, newly recognized sub-populations, and targeted therapeutic interventions for individuals with immune dysfunction, with potential for broader application to other pathogen types and disease processes.

application_id: 10808050
activity_code: F30
title: Coordination of T cell-derived signals in intestinal epithelial barrier defense
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ALABAMA AT BIRMINGHAM
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Diarrheal pathogens are the cause of over 1.5 million deaths globally each year, mostly affecting children in underdeveloped countries. Barrier maintenance and host defense against diarrheal pathogens requires the complex interplay of both immune cells and intestinal epithelial cells, but how these cell types cooperate to eradicate pathogens is largely unknown. This proposal seeks to eclucidate the mechanisms by which IFN acts on intestinal epithelial cells to recruit and sustain local IL-22 delivery from pathogen-specific CD4+ T cells in the setting of intestinal infection and how this process can be manipulated for therapeutic benefit in the treatment of diarrheal illness.

activity_code: T32
title: Initiative for Maximizing Student Development at the University of California, Davis.
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA AT DAVIS
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The overarching goal of this IMSD Training Program is to increase the diversity of the biomedical research workforce by boosting the number of students from diverse backgrounds who successfully earn PhDs in biomedically related science fields. To achieve this, we aim to consistently refine and execute comprehensive intervention programs, encompassing research, education, mentoring, and community-building initiatives for graduate students. We anticipate that our carefully developed enrichment activities will enhance inclusivity and belonging for those underrepresented in the biomedical workforce and produce resilient and highly skilled scientists who are successful in their dissertation research and who will thrive in the next step of their biomedical research career.
