phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Human cytomegalovirus (CMV) is the most common viral infection to complicate heart transplantation and is a
well-established risk factor for rejection, a significant barrier to allograft function and longevity. The goal of this
project is to create a high dimensional cartography at single-cell resolution of the cardiac allograft tissue
microenvironment during cellular rejection with CMV infection to identify mechanisms of viral rejection and
immune cell signatures that track with tissue disease state in the blood. Successful completion of these proposed
studies will nominate novel, non-invasive biomarkers to predict CMV-related outcomes in heart transplant
recipients and guide the development of rejection treatment strategies that selectively mitigate viral immune

org_name: OHIO STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative The plasma membrane is essential for normal cell functioning and cell homeostasis. application_id: 11081667
activity_code: K08
title: Clonal Hematopoiesis in a Biracial Population
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ALABAMA AT BIRMINGHAM
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The proposed research seeks to study the factors associated with development of clonal hematopoiesis of
indeterminate potential (CHIP), and racial disparities in associated outcomes, in order to identify individuals at
increased risk. With the availability of low cost sequencing techniques, we expect the number of individuals with
CHIP to increase in future, and, it will be important to develop appropriate strategies to manage these individuals.
Knowledge gained from the proposed study is expected to set the stage for interventions to inform management
of CHIP and decrease the risk of associated adverse outcomes such as hematologic malignancies,
cardiovascular disease and mortality.

application_id: 11001473
activity_code: K08
title: Regulation of endothelial cell phosphatidylserine in thrombosis
org_name: BETH ISRAEL DEACONESS MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Project Narrative Phosphatidylserine is a lipid whose exposure to blood promotes clotting. This proposal uses animal models and state-of-the-art microscopy techniques to study how endothelial cells on the blood vessel wall contribute phosphatidylserine to generate thrombosis. By studying the regulation of phosphatidylserine exposure on endothelial cells, we will gain a better understanding of how blood clots form in vessels, which may allow for the development of novel therapeutics to treat thrombotic disease.

title: Personalized Postpartum Hemorrhage Prediction Using Machine Learning And Polygenic Risk Scores
org_name: BRIGHAM AND WOMEN'S HOSPITAL
phr: Since 10.7% of all maternal deaths are due to postpartum hemorrhage, a personalized prediction tool is essential for clinicians. application_id: 10771145
activity_code: K08
title: Identification of Causal T-Cell Mechanisms in Immune Checkpoint Inhibitor Induced Myocarditis
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Myocarditis, a serious inflammatory reaction in the heart causing cardiac death, can occur as a result of immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy. Immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs) are a novel class of cancer therapeutics which release the brakes on T-cell cytotoxicity against tumor cells but may also lead to T-cell mediated myocarditis. This project aims to define the precise T-cell subsets, identify molecular mechanisms potentiating T-cell activation/expansion, and uncover the culprit antigens in ICI-induced myocarditis, thus paving the way to prevention and cure of this serious side effect of ICIs and helping ensure the cardiovascular safety of cancer patients.

phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Our brains are hard-wired to prioritize the present over the future—a tendency called delay discounting that often leads us to choose immediate gratification even when we know it is not in our long-term interests. For pregnant women who smoke, the immediate rewards of a cigarette can overwhelm efforts to resist the temptation to smoke, despite most women wanting to quit for both their own and their baby's sake. Among pregnant women who smoke, this study will (1) measure delay discounting using a newly developed method that mimics the experience of addiction, (2) characterize how delay discounting affects smoking during and after pregnancy, and (3) building on evidence from animal and clinical research, test whether high-dose dietary supplementation of omega-3 polyunsatura...

title: Functional mapping of the parabrachial nucleus: from gastrointestinal topography to satiety
org_name: BETH ISRAEL DEACONESS MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Project Narrative Appropriate feeding behaviors rely on accurate estimation of future energy balance, which in turn relies on gastrointestinal signals related to stomach stretch and nutrient content. How these visceral signals are integrated to assess internal state and guide feeding behaviors is poorly understood. application_id: 10909049
activity_code: F32
title: Identifying Determinants of Borrelia burgdorferi and Peromyscus leucopus symbiosis
org_name: TUFTS UNIVERSITY BOSTON
phr: Project Narrative Lyme disease places a substantial health (affecting >450,000 US citizens per year) and financial (costing Americans >$1 billion per year) burden on the United States. This project seeks to understand how the causal agent of Lyme disease, Borrelia burgdorferi, is able to survive, propagate, and spread in nature. Understanding the symbiotic relationship between B. burgdorferi and its natural rodent reservoir could reveal strategies to substantially reduce or even eradicate the pathogen from the wild, which would prevent spread of the bacteria to humans and the development of acute and/or chronic Lyme disease.

title: Postdoctoral Training in Suicide Prevention Research
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ROCHESTER
phr: Project Narrative Suicide is the tenth leading cause of death in the U.S., second among individuals between the ages of 10 and 34 and fourth among individuals between the ages of 35 and 54. The objective of this postdoctoral fellowship is to provide scientists with the knowledge and skills to design and conduct research with the goal of reducing suicide- related morbidity and mortality across the life course.

application_id: 11016912
activity_code: F31
title: The neural basis of stereognosis and its application to neuroprosthetics
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CHICAGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE We can dexterously and precisely interact with objects using our hands, which is enabled by our ability to form robust and vivid internal representations of the shape and structure of objects. This ability, termed stereognosis, involves still unknown neural processing mechanisms that integrate tactile and proprioceptive signals from skin and muscles of the hand. In this work, we aim to characterize the neural basis of stereognosis in an effort to elucidate principles of multimodal integration using neurophysiological experiments in grasping primates and computational modelling of the same behavior.

application_id: 10762422
activity_code: K01
title: Modulating brain networks to reduce gait variability in older adults at risk of falling
org_name: HEBREW REHABILITATION CENTER FOR AGED
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE In older adults, the complex task of walking is controlled in large part by multiple brain networks that enable one to pay attention to their surroundings, process sensory information, and produce appropriate movements of the body. This career development proposal aims to determine whether a form of non-invasive brain stimulation called transcranial direct current stimulation (tDCS) can change brain network function and thus improve walking in older adults with unsteady gait. This project is expected to increase our understanding of the brain-level control of walking in older adults, and produce critical information needed to design a larger clinical trial of tDCS to enhance gait and mobility in older adults.

application_id: 10999468
activity_code: K08
title: The Role of the Bifidobacterium Gut Microbiome Guild in Pathophysiology of Chronic Heart Failure with Reduced Ejection Fraction
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The disease course of chronic heart failure, a prevalent disease with high morbidity and mortality, is highly
variable and our ability to predict progression on an individual level and prevent it is limited. Data from animals
suggests that the gut microbiome, a community of microbes living in our gastrointestinal tract, may play a role
in heart failure pathophysiology and disease progression. The goal of this project is to better understand
disturbances in the gut microbiome, its interactions with the host and their joint impact on heart failure
pathophysiology and disease progression in patients with chronic heart failure, which will identify novel
preventative and therapeutic targets and improve risk stratification in this disease.

phr: Project Narrative Pulmonary fibrosis (PF) syndromes, including adult idiopathic PF (IPF) and childhood interstitial lung disease (chILD), cause significant morbidity and mortality worldwide. PUBLIC HEALTH RELEVANCE: Obesity affects more than one-third of the US population generating a need

application_id: 11047743
activity_code: K08
title: Development of strategies to enhance titin (TTN) expression and treat dilated cardiomyopathy caused by TTN haploinsufficiency
org_name: BRIGHAM AND WOMEN'S HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative
Rare genetic variants of the essential sarcomere protein titin (TTN) is the most common genetic cause of dilated
cardiomyopathy (DCM), a disease characterized by enlargement and weakening of one or both of main
chambers of the heart. DCM often progresses to heart failure, a devastating disorder with high morbidity and
early mortality, but only limited options are currently available to treat DCM. This proposal aims to determine
how TTN gene expression is regulated, augment the amount of TTN expression via genome editing, assess
effects of increased TTN protein level on cardiomyocytes with pathogenic TTN variants, and ultimately establish
therapeutic strategies for DCM.

application_id: 10808155
activity_code: K23
title: Improving Glaucoma Care Using a Scalable Decision Support System
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF UTAH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Primary open angle glaucoma is a complex chronic disease that affects over 2.9 million people in the United States and is the 2nd leading cause of blindness in this country. Clinical decision making for glaucoma is challenging, as clinicians must incorporate large amounts of longitudinal data from many data sources, which often results in suboptimal testing and management. This study will lead to the development of a standards- based clinical decision support system that presents data at the point of care to help clinicians determine a personalized frequency for visual field testing for patients with glaucoma, which will reduce over- and under- testing and ultimately prevent vision loss.

application_id: 11080965
activity_code: K08
title: Towards Precision Medicine for Thoracic Aortic Disease: Defining the Clinical and Genomic Drivers of Bicuspid Aortopathy
org_name: UTAH STATE HIGHER EDUCATION SYSTEM--UNIVERSITY OF UTAH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
This proposal will investigate the causative clinical factors, demographic variables, and genomic variants
associated with bicuspid aortic valve aortopathy, a heritable condition with poorly understood pathogenesis. The
proposal will use artificial intelligence methods of predictive modeling to discover clinical and demographic
variables associated with BAV aortopathy as well as whole genome sequencing analysis to define the drivers of
this complex disease. Successful completion of this study will allow development of precision medicine tools to
predict aortic disease risk at the individual patient level.

application_id: 11089373
activity_code: K08
title: Comparative-Effectiveness of Procedures for Carotid Revascularization
org_name: DARTMOUTH-HITCHCOCK CLINIC
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The objective of this project is to determine the effectiveness of transcarotid artery revascularization (TCAR) to
prevent stroke in patients with carotid artery stenosis compared to carotid endarterectomy or transfemoral carotid
artery stenting. With no randomized trial of TCAR to guide treatment decisions, this study will resolve important
knowledge gaps surrounding how TCAR should be used in clinical practice. In addition, this work will serve as a
project-based learning example allowing Dr. Columbo to fill critical conceptual knowledge gaps in his training as
a comparative-effectiveness researcher, launching his career as an independent investigator.

title: Exploring the role of hyper-phagocytic microglia and SYK signaling in the aged hippocampus during COVID-19
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF VIRGINIA
phr: Project Narrative Since its emergence in 2019, SARS-CoV-2, a novel sarbecoronavirus, has infected at least half of the United States population and has left many individuals with debilitating neurological post-acute COVID-19 sequalae (neuro-PASC). Currently, the etiology of neuro-PASC remains unknown, but early clinical data suggests that neuro-PASC could be attributed to an overactive immune system. Therefore, in line with these recent findings, this proposed research project will determine whether microglia, the resident macrophages in the brain, rely upon the spleen tyrosine kinase (SYK) gene, an important regulator of microglia inflammatory responses, to perform pervasive activity in the COVID-19 brain.

application_id: 10897967
activity_code: K08
title: Progressive loading of a human dystrophic cardiomyopathy 3D model to mimic disease and evaluate therapeutic
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MINNESOTA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Duchenne muscular dystrophy heart failure is ubiquitous and is now the leading cause of death, however there
is limited mechanistic understanding of disease progression and currently no effective treatments to prevent or
limit the progression. This proposal is designed to train Dr. Forum Kamdar in cardiac tissue engineering and
extracellular matrix dynamics, to aid in her transition to an independently funded physician-scientist in
neuromuscular cardiomyopathy. In this project, she will develop a 3D heart model using dystrophic heart cells
derived from patient skin cells, using advanced physical technology to understand the molecular basis of this
devastating disease, and to test ideas for developing effective treatments.

application_id: 11167448
activity_code: K08
title: The protective role of fibroblast growth factor signaling in hypoxia-induced pulmonary hypertension
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Pulmonary hypertension (PH) induced by hypoxia is a debilitating disease that affects premature infants with
bronchopulmonary dysplasia and adults with chronic obstructive lung disease. We showed that genetic activation
of Fibroblast Growth Factor (FGF) signaling in lung endothelial cells is protective against hypoxia-induced PH.
This proposed studies will increase our understanding of the role of FGF in vascular remodeling, as well as
advance gene therapy approaches to activate FGF signaling efficiently and selectively in the pulmonary vascular
endothelium.

application_id: 11124235
activity_code: K08
title: Myelofibrosis impacts the hematopoietic niche through TNF-a
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
This proposal outlines an approach to advance our understanding of how cells that support
normal blood formation are changed in patients with a hematologic disease called
myeloproliferative neoplasms. We will test the hypothesis that changes in interactions between
cells in the bone marrow microenvironment cause low blood counts and enlarged spleen size
through tumor necrosis factor-alpha (TNF-?) and platelet derived growth factor (PDGF). This
preclinical research may lead to new combinations of therapies for clinical trials in patients with
myeloproliferative neoplasms to prevent development and worsening of disease.

title: Sex, Physiological State, and Genetic Background Dependent Molecular Characterization of CircuitsGoverning Parental Behavior
org_name: HARVARD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Parental care is an evolutionarily conserved behavior that is highly modulated by the sex, physiological state, and genetic background of the caregiver. An inability to properly display parental behaviors can lead to long- lasting detrimental effects in the infant. Thus, understanding the molecular basis for sex-, state-, and genetic- dependent expression of parental behavior is critical to understanding this essential behavior in normal and pathological contexts.

application_id: 11109594
activity_code: K08
title: Propagation of graft vs. host disease through CD4+ T-cell cognate recognition of gastrointestinal microbiota
org_name: FRED HUTCHINSON CANCER CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Graft vs. host disease (GVHD) remains a major barrier for the successful application of hematopoietic stem cell
transplant for a wide range of benign and hematologic malignancies. There is increasing evidence that the
microbiome can impact GVHD outcomes, though current understanding of how specific microbial species
contribute to GVHD pathogenesis remains largely correlative with limited mechanistic insights. The proposed
research will aim to improve the understanding of how the microbiota and microbiota-specific T cell response
impact the alloreactive response to open additional strategies aimed at treating GVHD.

application_id: 11124698
activity_code: K08
title: ADAR mediated RNA editing is a causal mechanism in coronary artery disease
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Coronary artery disease (CAD) is the leading cause of death worldwide, and from leveraging the power of
human genetics and mechanistic discovery, we are poised to understand causal mechanisms of disease and
design crucially needed therapies. In a groundbreaking discovery through complex disease genetics, RNA
editing of double stranded RNA, mediated by ADAR1, has been found to powerfully influence CAD risk. In this
proposal, I aim to understand the fundamental mechanisms of RNA editing and atherosclerosis, and further
define the potential for targeting the dsRNA sensor MDA5 for clinical therapy — allowing me to advance my
training and become an independent physician-scientist in cardiovascular medicine.

application_id: 11073092
activity_code: K08
title: Donor DNMT3a-mediated regulation of Graft-vs-Host-Disease and the Graft-vs-Tumor effect in allogeneic hematopoietic cell transplantation
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplant (HCT) remains the only curative therapy for many aggressive
malignancies and is increasingly used for non-malignant disorders, but successful outcomes are limited by graft-
vs-host-disease (GVHD) and inadequate graft-vs-tumor (GVT) activity. De novo DNA methylation catalyzed by
DNMT3a is a major epigenetic mechanism that controls the post-HCT immune response. This project will define
the DNMT3a-dependent pathways that can be targeted in the regulation and separation of harmful GVHD from
beneficial GVT activity, which remains a critical unmet need toward improving patient outcomes.

application_id: 11033574
activity_code: K01
title: Deciphering the Regulation of TGF-b3 in Driving Myofibroblast Differentiation in Systemic Sclerosis
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: NARRATIVE
While significant strides have been made in unraveling the complex pathogenesis of systemic sclerosis (SSc),
effective therapies targeting myofibroblasts, the primary cells driving fibrosis in SSc, remain elusive.
Transforming growth factor β (TGFβ) is recognized for its central role in promoting myofibroblast differentiation
in SSc. This study aims to investigate epigenetic mechanisms underlying the upregulation of TGF-β3, specifically
expressed in skin myofibroblasts, with the goal of enhancing the safety and efficacy of SSc treatment by targeting
the TGFβ signaling pathway.

application_id: 11193799
activity_code: K08
title: Optimizing hematopoietic stem cell transplantation to treat non-malignant disease
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (HSCT) with autologous gene therapy is a promising approach to
treating non-malignant blood diseases; however, optimization of stem cell mobilization, transplant conditioning
regimens, and stable engraftment of gene-edited hematopoietic stem cells is essential to maximizing the
curative potential of HSCT. This proposal seeks to overcome these major barriers by investigating novel stem
cell mobilization and antibody-based transplant conditioning strategies and leveraging these approaches to
improve therapy of sickle cell disease. These studies will yield novel insights that will advance the basic
science and translational potential of autologous gene therapy.

application_id: 10806496
activity_code: K08
title: Modulating the Microbiome to Enhance Right Ventricular Function in Pulmonary Arterial Hypertension
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MINNESOTA
phr: Project Narrative This proposal is designed to further train Sasha Prisco, MD, PhD in molecular and cellular cardiac physiology and multiple omics techniques to help in the transition to becoming an independently funded physician-scientist who studies the mechanisms of right ventricular dysfunction in pulmonary hypertension. Her project investigates if Lactobacillus supplementation modulates the gut microbiome, systemic inflammation, the microtubule cytoskeleton, and right ventricular function in pulmonary arterial hypertension. During her training, she will refine skills that will enable her to perform clinically-relevant, translational research that will hopefully allow for future development of novel therapies for this rare and devastating disease.

title: Genetic and Transcriptomic Resilience in Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease
org_name: UPSTATE MEDICAL UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE While some genes can increase the risk of disease, other genes may reduce the effect of these risk genes as well as the effect of environmental factors. In this study, we will identify genetic variants that contribute to resilience to chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) by attenuating the effects of genetic risk variants and cigarette smoking. This will result in greater understanding of the ways that genetic factors contribute to variable disease risk, and this information might be used to better predict risk for COPD and identify new treatments.

title: ALL OF US EVENINGS WITH GENETICS RESEARCH EDUCATION PROGRAM
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: To address a lack of data science expertise among researchers, which limits their ability to tackle community-based issues in research with large databases like that from All of Us, we launched training on the All of Us data and the Researcher Workbench. application_id: 10761768
activity_code: K01
title: Mechanisms linking dietary fiber, the microbiome, and satiety
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MISSOURI-COLUMBIA
phr: Relevance/Project Narrative Recent research has shown that microbes in the human gut can influence a person’s health; dietary fiber is beneficial to these microbes, and fermentation of this fiber produces byproducts, called short chain fatty acids (SCFA) that may signal the gut to make hormones that give people a feeling of fullness. The proposed study will test this idea by feeding people high-fiber diets and measuring blood levels of SCFA, hormones and brain activation in response to a food-cue fMRI task. Successful completion of this study will increase our understanding of how high-fiber diets help people feel full and help them keep their weight down which would result in less risk for the future development of heart disease.

title: Oxidative Stress and Mitochondrial Dysfunction in Chemogenetic Heart Failure
org_name: BRIGHAM AND WOMEN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Heart failure is a debilitating medical condition characterized by the inability of the heart to supply blood to the body and critical organs. The number of people developing heart failure continues to increase worldwide and, despite intense efforts to improve the treatment of heart failure, death rates remain high. Although multiple complex causes lead to heart failure, oxidative stress is a common feature and by leveraging the strength of a newly characterized animal model that allows on demand generation of oxidative stress in the heart, I aim to define how oxidative stress leads to heart failure with the long-term goal to find novel therapies for this devastating disease.

application_id: 11076301
activity_code: K08
title: CCRL2 as a Regulator of Growth and Novel Target in Myelodysplastic Syndrome
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: Narrative
 Myelodysplastic syndrome (MDS) is a frequently fatal type of blood disorder with few therapeutic options due to
challenges in distinguishing and targeting the abnormal immature cells in the bone marrow. We have discovered
that the levels of a specific receptor called CCRL2 are significantly higher in MDS cells compared to healthy cells
and this molecule promotes the growth of these abnormal cells. We propose to determine the specific
mechanisms of the growth activities of CCRL2 in MDS and develop therapies that will effectively suppress the
growth of abnormal cells in MDS patients.
.

title: Risk factors for chronic obstructive pulmonary disease exacerbations, quality of care, and outcomes in people with HIV
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
HIV infection is a risk factor for chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) and COPD exacerbations, but
risk factors for and outcomes following COPD exacerbations as well as the prescribing patterns and effect of
guideline-concordant COPD care in people with HIV (PWH) are unknown. We therefore propose to perform a
cohort study of HIV-infected and uninfected comparators with COPD using administrative and electronic health
record data from multiple health systems across the US and a causal inference framework to identify
modifiable risk factors for COPD exacerbations and poor outcomes and to investigate the links between
processes of care and outcomes in PWH. Ultimately, this research will inform efforts to develop effective
management strategies for high-quality COPD c...

application_id: 10907779
activity_code: K08
title: Role of stromal inflammatory signaling in the aging of lung resident lymphocytes
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Dysfunction of the immune system has been implicated in the pathogenesis of several age-related lung diseases, including pneumonia, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, and pulmonary fibrosis, suggesting that reversing or delaying age-related immune dysfunction may be an effective strategy for treating these diseases as a group. Our preliminary data supports a role for increases in inflammatory signaling within structural cells of the lung as a cause of aging-associated immune system dysfunction. The aim of this proposal is to understand how this inflammatory signaling induces immune system dysfunction, with the ultimate goal of identifying new potential therapeutic targets to treat age-related lung diseases.

application_id: 10897098
activity_code: R25
title: Mayo Clinic Research Education Program in Computational Autonomic Neurobiology of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases
org_name: MAYO CLINIC ROCHESTER
phr: Project Narrative The Research Educational Program in Computational Autonomic Neurobiology is being developed at the Mayo Clinic in Rochester Minnesota to train a new generation of modern biomedical scientists and physicians capable of performing, interpreting, and applying analyses of increasingly complex and large datasets. By providing participants with foundational knowledge and practical skills to apply computer-based analyses to data from impactful research experiences, the program is poised to advance understanding of diabetes and digestive and kidney diseases.

application_id: 11077259
activity_code: K08
title: Transfer RNAs as novel mediators in acute lung injury
org_name: OHIO STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Despite decades of research, there are no specific, targeted pharmacotherapies for the Acute Respiratory
Distress Syndrome (ARDS), a common and deadly problem in critically ill patients. We propose a mechanism
where host transfer RNAs (tRNAs) modulate host defense in experimental lung injury and explore regulation of
tRNAs and tRNA fragments by the tRNA methyltransferase, TRMT1. tRNA fragments are unexplored in ARDS
pathobiology and may represent novel therapeutic targets.

application_id: 10914247
activity_code: K08
title: The role of surfactant protein-A genetic variant and sex in pathogenesis of neonatal respiratory syncytial virus infection
org_name: PENNSYLVANIA STATE UNIV HERSHEY MED CTR
phr: Narrative Respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) causes significant morbidity and mortality in infants with a global disease burden of 64 million cases and 160,000 deaths with healthcare costs estimated at $365-$585 million per year. The objective of the current research proposal is to elucidate the role of human SP-A genetic variant and sex in RSV severity and to get insight into the mechanisms of host factors that govern the severity of RSV disease. This will lead to the development of novel approaches and individualized therapeutic options for severe RSV infection.

application_id: 10909298
activity_code: F31
title: Preventing Age-Associated Oocyte Aneuploidy: Mechanisms Behind the Drosophila melanogaster Centromere Effect
org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: Project Narrative During meiosis, crossing-over between homologous chromosomes plays an essential role in preventing nondisjunction of homologs, with defects in segregation being the leading cause of miscarriages and chromosomal disorders. This project will illuminate features of the centromere effect, a meiotic crossover patterning phenomenon that excludes crossovers in centromere-proximal regions, and whose mechanism remains elusive to this day. Studying the centromere effect not only contributes towards understanding a phenomenon that safeguards against age-associated meiotic defects, but also fills a gap in knowledge regarding a fundamental cellular process.

application_id: 11240260
activity_code: F31
title: Determining how a single-residue PKR mutation found in patients with familial dystonia causes aberrant PKR activation
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: Project Narrative
1. PKR is a pattern recognition receptor which binds dsRNA, and that is overactivated in a variety of debilitating
neurologic diseases (including Huntington’s disease, Alzheimer’s disease, and Aicardi-Goutières syndrome).
2. The G130R mutation in PKR leads to enhanced PKR activation both in patient fibroblasts with familial
neurologic disease as well as in our cell culture system, but the mechanism of overactivation is not known.
3. This proposal will attempt to identify the cause of PKRG130R overactivation and its role in causing
neurodegeneration, which may help identify strategies for future therapeutics for a range of different neurologic
diseases where PKR is overactive.

application_id: 11061847
activity_code: K08
title: SREBP1 as a Novel Mammalian Oxygen Sensor
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE/PUBLIC HEALTH RELEVANCE STATEMENT
Chronic hypoxia affects millions of patients with cardiopulmonary diseases such as advanced heart failure and
severe chronic lung disease, and is associated with increased morbidity and mortality. The goal of this work is
to test the hypothesis that the transcription factor, sterol response element binding protein 1 (SREBP1), is an
evolutionarily conserved oxygen sensor in mammals that mediates adaptation to chronic hypoxia by integrating
changes in lipid metabolism and low oxygen availability. This work could uncover novel therapeutic targets for
conditions that lead to long-term hypoxia, in particular cardiovascular and metabolic diseases.

application_id: 10812994
activity_code: F30
title: Life Course Developmental and Reproductive Predictors of Increased Mammographic Breast Density in Black Women
org_name: TUFTS UNIVERSITY BOSTON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Despite advances in breast cancer prevention and therapies, Black women exhibit increasing incidence and continue to have the highest breast cancer mortality rate compared to any other racial population. Mammographic density is one of the strongest breast cancer risk factors, yet, while Black women are more likely to exhibit higher mammographic density, unique predictors of mammographic density are severely understudied in this vulnerable population. Using the Black Women’s Health Study, the largest cohort of Black women in the United States, the overall goal of this proposal is to identify life course developmental and maturation risk factors for increased mammographic density.

title: Integrative Multiomics to Uncover Novel Genes and Networks in Pulmonary Arterial Hypertension
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA LOS ANGELES
phr: Project Narrative
Pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH) remains an incurable and often fatal disease characterized by irreversible
vascular remodeling. Leveraging a well-powered cohort integrating the latest omics and computational
methodologies is critically needed to identify candidate molecular drivers in PAH lungs as potential therapeutic
targets. The proposed studies will utilize a combination of cutting-edge multiomic approaches and experimental
biology to provide greater insight into a novel PAH-associated gene set derived from the largest PAH lung
biobank to date, and will lay the groundwork for future mechanistic and translational studies.

title: Mural cells contribution to vascular remodeling in flow induced pulmonary hypertension
org_name: BOSTON CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative: At present, no disease-modifying therapies exist for patients with pulmonary arterial hypertension. While it is known that increased pulmonary blood flow can lead to structural changes in the distal arterioles, the underlying mechanisms remain unclear. title: WNT Signaling in the Pathogenesis of Atrial Fibrillation
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative
Atrial fibrillation is a common arrhythmia with downstream consequences that include stroke, heart failure, and
death. Despite its growing burden, our understanding of the mechanistic basis of AF is incomplete and
therapeutic interventions are limited in efficacy, associated with adverse effects, and have not seen any
significant advances in years. This proposal aims to exploit a common signaling pathway that may underlie both
genetic and acquired forms of atrial fibrillation, with an effort towards identifying novel therapeutic targets.

title: Enzymatic Mechanism of Polysaccharide Length Control by GlfT2
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Extracellular polysaccharides play critical roles in bacteria. title: Etiology of naturally-occurring anti-ABO antibodies
org_name: BRIGHAM AND WOMEN'S HOSPITAL
phr: Narrative: ABO(H) blood group antigens were the first human polymorphisms described and corresponding anti-ABO(H) antibodies continue to be the most common immunological barrier to transfusion and transplantation. application_id: 11175437
activity_code: K08
title: Inflammatory Disease Associations in Clonal Hematopoiesis
org_name: DANA-FARBER CANCER INST
phr: Project Narrative
Clonal hematopoiesis (CH) is the pre-cancerous and age-related expansion of hematopoietic stem cells
(HSCs) often caused by acquired mutations in leukemia driver genes. While CH research has focused on risk
of developing blood cancers such as acute myeloid leukemia (AML), having CH also increases the risk of
inflammatory diseases. This project utilizes large genomic and clinical datasets to define CH-associated
inflammatory disease outcomes, model risk of inflammatory comorbidity in CH and explore the use of
hypomethylating agents as a therapeutic strategy to alleviate inflammation disease burden in CH.

application_id: 11002303
activity_code: K08
title: The Role of E3 Ligase MARCH10 in Influenza Virus Infection
org_name: OHIO STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE There are ~ 1 billion human influenza virus cases worldwide each year that cause up to 650,000 deaths. Influenza virus infection remains difficult to treat because the antiviral drugs available are usually limited to preventing influenza virus infection early in the infection and increasing drug resistance limits their effectiveness. We have identified an E3 ligase MARCH10 that targets an influenza virus virulence factor for degradation and propose in vitro and in vivo studies with the ultimate goal of developing new therapies to combat influenza virus infection.

activity_code: K08
title: A comprehensive approach to understanding the genetic causes of urinary stone disease: combining monogenic and polygenic analyses
org_name: CASE WESTERN RESERVE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Urinary Stone Disease (USD) is a widespread ailment with significant health and economic impacts worldwide. However, the mechanisms underlying the condition are still not fully understood, and effective prevention strategies are lacking. This career development grant proposes to address these issues by employing next-generation sequencing technology to systematically investigate both monogenic and polygenic genes comprehensively and develop an integrated mono-polygenic risk model to facilitate precision medicine interventions.

application_id: 11177591
activity_code: F31
title: Characterizing genes that regulate dopaminergic neuron regeneration in the planarian brain
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF GEORGIA
phr: Narrative
This proposal aims to elucidate the timeline of dopaminergic neuron specification and maturation during
homeostasis and injury. Additionally, the research in this proposal will reveal genes that promote the creation
of neurons that produce dopamine, a key signal in the nervous system. Uncovering and characterizing genes
important for dopaminergic neuron regeneration using planarian flatworms could lead to a better understanding
of successful brain regeneration and could lead to improvements in creating dopamine-producing neurons from
stem cells for human therapies.

application_id: 11291325
activity_code: K08
title: Immuno-Metabolic Regulation of Vascular Dysfunction by CD70
org_name: BRIGHAM AND WOMEN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Cardiovascular diseases comprise the leading cause of death in the United States, but mechanisms leading to
endothelial and vascular dysfunction are still poorly understood. The research outlined in this proposal will
elucidate how the protein CD70 regulates endothelial metabolism and vascular function. The long-term goal of
this Career Development Award is to provide a pathway for the PI to transition to an independent physician-
scientist while uncovering how CD70 regulates key endothelial redox and metabolic processes to influence the
development of vascular dysfunction, thereby gaining new insights into the potential of CD70 as a novel
therapeutic target for vascular diseases.

application_id: 10910052
activity_code: K01
title: Central regulation of atherosclerosis by testosterone-deficiency
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: Men with low levels of testosterone have substantially elevated risk of atherosclerosis, the leading cause of heart disease and stroke in the United States. Increasing evidence suggests that in addition to peripheral mechanisms, the brain also has the capacity to regulate risk factors of atherosclerosis. The proposed study aims to investigate hypothalamic gliosis as a pivotal mechanism through which testosterone deficiency promotes atherosclerosis.

title: A data-driven framework for mapping transcranial electrical stimulation effects on audiovisual integration and hallucinations
org_name: BETH ISRAEL DEACONESS MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  This project will examine data from a trial of transcranial direct current stimulation for the reduction of hallucinations. Stimulation targets the right superior temporal sulcus (rSTS), which plays a causal role in the manifestation of hallucinations and is associated with abnormal audiovisual integration in psychosis syndromes. Analyses will determine the accuracy of stimulation by assessing electrophysiological (EEG) biomarkers of audiovisual integration, and the efficacy of the intervention by examining clinical measures of hallucination frequency and severity.

title: Mechanisms of Complement Activation in Traumatic Injury
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF NEBRASKA MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Narrative
Traumatic injury is the leading cause of death from ages 1-44 in the United States, claiming more than 200,000
lives per year and is responsible for millions of hospital encounters at an annual cost of over $4 trillion USD.
While approximately half of trauma patient deaths occur early after injury from bleeding or brain injury, the
other half occur in a delayed fashion and frequently are related to organ failure (lungs, kidneys) that is driven,
in part, by inflammatory responses. We are performing research focused on understanding how a key
inflammatory system called complement gets activated and contributes to the organ failure and delayed deaths
observed after traumatic injury, which may eventually lead to novel therapeutic interventions to prevent death
in these predominantly ...

application_id: 11001948
activity_code: K08
title: Characterization of PHF7 function in mediating cardiac reprogramming
org_name: UT SOUTHWESTERN MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Project Narrative
Heart failure from ischemic heart disease is the leading cause of morbidity, mortality, and healthcare expenditure
worldwide. The long-term goal and the clinical significance of this K08 grant is to provide the basis for the
development of a novel therapeutic factor for the treatment of ischemic heart disease that promotes adult
fibroblast to cardiomyocyte reprogramming following myocardial infarction. The results from these studies will
deepen our understanding of mechanisms that drive reprogramming and serve as an important pre-clinical
platform for the advancement of cardiac reprogramming therapies in patients suffering from heart failure and
ischemic heart disease.

application_id: 10763003
activity_code: K23
title: Using BTK Inhibitors to Prevent Anaphylactic Drug Reactions
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Anaphylaxis is a potentially life-threatening IgE-mediated systemic allergic reaction with no known preventative therapies. Our preliminary data suggest that pharmacologic inhibitors of Bruton’s tyrosine kinase (BTK) can prevent IgE-mediated human mast cell and basophil activation in vitro and in humanized mouse models in vivo. Specific aims will determine the ability of BTK inhibitors to prevent drug-induced anaphylaxis, in addition to further defining the kinetics and degree of their efficacy as potential therapies for preventing anaphylaxis in humans.

application_id: 11094830
activity_code: K08
title: Multi-scale Analysis of the Role of Skeletal Muscle Actin Mutations in Dilated Cardiomyopathy
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: Project narrative
The heart and skeletal muscle contract when myosin protein pulls on actin filaments and mutations in these and
associated proteins can lead to dilated cardiomyopathy through different mechanisms. This study will use
methods from single atoms to whole hearts to explore a unique mutation in an actin protein that normally causes
skeletal muscle disease but, in this case, causes cardiomyopathy. The findings from this study will establish a
new way to study actin proteins in cardiomyopathy and lay the foundation for new heart failure therapies.

application_id: 11084367
activity_code: K08
title: Exploiting neointimal and vascular smooth muscle cell heterogeneity to reveal mechanisms driving venous neointimal growth and vascular remodeling in pediatric pulmonary hypertension
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Pediatric pulmonary hypertension (PPH) is a progressive, incurable disease caused by abnormal smooth muscle
growth and development of “neointimal lesions” that block blood flow through arteries and veins, with devastating
outcomes when venous disease predominates. We have no treatments that target venous lesions because we
know very little about what controls their growth and therefore, in these experiments we are 1) defining the
transcriptional signature of vein neointima across the lifespan of a lesion to understand the mechanisms driving

activity_code: K08
title: Neurogenomic Investigations of Trichotillomania and Excoriation Disorder
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Trichotillomania and excoriation disorder are disabling psychiatric conditions with limited effective treatments.
This research uses a series of complementary neurogenomic investigations to significantly advance our
understanding of the genes and neurodevelopmental pathways that contribute to these conditions. This is
relevant to public health because it advances our fundamental knowledge of the underlying mechanisms that
lead to trichotillomania and excoriation disorder, which has the potential to identify novel treatment targets to
ultimately reduce the burden of these conditions.

application_id: 11199459
activity_code: U54
title: Core B: Biospecimen Processing and Storage
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: Public Health Relevance Core B Biospecimen Processing and Storage Core 
Latino children with acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL) have a higher frequency of treatment-related toxicities 
compared to other groups. Multiple factors likely contribute to these disparities in this vulnerable population of 
children, including underlying germline genetics, tumor biology, and social determinants of health. The 
Biospecimen Processing and Storage Core (Core B) will serve as the central location where biospecimens will 
be processed and stored to support research that can provide insights into the biological factors underlying 
cancer health disparities in childhood ALL.

activity_code: K08
title: Clinical and biologic health trajectories after early life adversity
org_name: CHILDREN'S HOSP OF PHILADELPHIA
phr: Project Narrative Child maltreatment is a significant cause of morbidity and mortality in the U.S. Better understanding of proximate mental and physical health predictors after childhood traumatic stressful events (TSEs), including genetic susceptibility and early biologic embedding by epigenetic age acceleration (EAA) and increased inflammation, may facilitate targeted interventions to ameliorate the long-term health burden. Using the Philadelphia Neurodevelopmental Cohort (PNC) and Center for Applied Genomics (CAG) pediatric biorepositories at The Children’s Hospital of Philadelphia, this project will test the hypothesis that childhood TSEs are associated with a spectrum of mental and physical health consequences that are influenced by genetic susceptibility and biologic embedding.

title: Synaptic protein composition and localization in individuals with schizophrenia
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Schizophrenia is a chronic illness with no treatments that interfere with the disease process. To design treatments that will change the course of schizophrenia illness, we need to map the detailed ways in which brains of people with schizophrenia differ. title: Defining the multivariate genomic signature of pubertal markers and impact on lifespan psychopathology
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Increased rates of psychiatric symptoms during adolescence significantly impact health and well-being during
this period of life and into adulthood, and are robustly associated with variation in pubertal timing and tempo.
Identifying mechanisms underlying pubertal risk for psychopathology is complicated by multiple layers of
hormonal, genetic, and contextual influences on both puberty and psychopathology that unfold over time, and
that may operate through both sex-specific and generalized pathways. Defining the genomic architecture across
physical and hormonal markers of puberty and examining links with psychopathology across the lifespan in both
sexes will enable researchers to better disentangle multilayered mechanisms driving pubertal risk for lifecourse
psychopatholog...

application_id: 10911338
activity_code: R01
title: Research Experience and Mentorship as Critical Interventions to Advance Diversity in Biomedical Science
org_name: CLAREMONT GRADUATE UNIVERSITY
phr: Diversifying the biomedical workforce is imperative to meet the healthcare needs of the U.S. population. We will build on our established longitudinal panel of Black and Hispanic science majors to test the long-term effectiveness of two widely utilized interventions: undergraduate research experience and faculty mentorship. Results will inform best practice for these large- scale interventions in training the next generation of diverse biomedical researchers.

org_name: DANA-FARBER CANCER INST
phr: Project Narrative Lung cancer is the leading cause of cancer related deaths worldwide and many patients eventually stop responding to treatments including immunotherapies, so a better understanding of why patients stop responding will help us develop strategies to overcome this challenge. My current work uses novel mouse models of lung cancer to investigate mechanisms of acquired resistance to immune checkpoint inhibitors, whereas my postdoctoral work will focus on developing efficacious neoantigen vaccines that induce tissue resident memory T cells. org_name: NATIONAL PARTNERSHIP/ENVIRONMNTL/TECH/ED
phr: The goal of the Community College Consortium for Health and Safety Training (CCCHST) is to help the nation be better prepared to prevent and to respond to hazardous materials incidents through the training of instructors serving workers from business and industry; students from community and tribal colleges and technical schools, including first responders; transitioning military; and members of community-based and faith-based organizations in targeted communities. CCCHST will make NIEHS-approved training nationally available through over 100 CCCHST organizational members, prepared through a Train-the-Trainer model program, to offer hazardous materials and disaster preparedness instruction in nearly all states of the nation. application_id: 10850877
activity_code: U45
title: Hazardous Materials Worker Health and Safety Training
org_name: SUSTAINABLE WORKPLACE ALLIANCE, INC.
phr: The Sustainable Workplace Alliance and its alliance partners will provide model training to better protect workers, first responders and citizens in underserved communities that have been negatively impacted by pollution, blight and emergency events. These vulnerable populations are unlikely to know their rights to a safe workplace, the inherent risks of the work they do, the dangers of misuse of opioids, the hazards created by pollution or hazardous materials, and the most effective ways to protect themselves from these harms. This model training and outreach program is designed to reduce injuries and death and to place disadvantaged citizens in viable jobs in the environmental and construction industries.

application_id: 11165303
activity_code: DP2
title: Transforming systems of care to address HIV, HIV risk, and substance use disorders in prisons
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
People incarcerated in prisons face a disproportionate burden of HIV, HIV risk, and substance use disorders,
and prison healthcare is often inadequate, which sets individuals up for poor substance use and HIV outcomes
after release. This study will work toward building comprehensive systems of healthcare in prisons to reach
people who use drugs who are living with or at risk for HIV. This study will implement a novel Learning Health
System model in a state prison system, using data to guide care improvement and generate knowledge that
can inform service delivery in prisons across the country.

title: Spatial-Temporal Dissection of Stratified Host Tissue Responses to Severe acute respiratory syndrome-related coronaviruses in situ to Understand Intra-host Pathogenesis
org_name: BETH ISRAEL DEACONESS MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Little is known about the spatial features of an orchestrated host defense against a pathogen in situ. How does our immune system coordinate itself against viral infections, and why does it respond differently to diverse virus variants or from the same lineage? application_id: 10680954
activity_code: DP2
title: IDENTIFYING MICROBIAL MECHANISMS THAT REGULATE ANIMAL INSULIN SIGNALING
org_name: VAN ANDEL RESEARCH INSTITUTE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Insulin resistance and Type 2 diabetes are among the most common and impactful human diseases in existence,
and are on the rise. I discovered that environmental bacteria have novel mechanisms or factors that can regulate
animal insulin signaling. To launch my independent research career, I will perform the first-ever, large-scale
screen for bioactive bacteria that modify animal insulin signaling and identify the mechanisms bacteria have
evolved to do so, which will provide the field with a fundamentally new strategy to identify novel therapies or
treatments for insulin resistance.

application_id: 10909556
activity_code: DP2
title: Genomic encoding of heterogeneity
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Cell-to-cell differences in gene expression (heterogeneity) underlies stem cell function and cancer,
though we lack understanding of its origins. Here we propose to identify fundamental mechanisms by
which cell systems encode heterogeneity using genomics and systems biology approaches. By
identifying the principles by which cells encode gene expression heterogeneity we can unlock new
therapeutic strategies to engage stem cells in regenerative medicine and to overcome treatment failure
in cancer.

application_id: 10685715
activity_code: DP2
title: How do animals learn the structure of their natural environment?
org_name: CORNELL UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
The goal of this project is to identify the neural circuit mechanisms of spatial and social structure learning using rats as a
model. By employing a multidisciplinary combination of cutting-edge neurophysiological, computational and behavioral
approaches, it would be able to test how hippocampo-cortical neuronal sequences support structure learning and inference
in naturalistic environments. These experiments have the potential to provide a long-sought answer about the neural basis
of adaptative behavior in natural conditions.

Cancer remains the second leading cause of death in the United States, and while cell-based therapies have
shown great success in treating blood cancers, they have unfortunately fallen short for treating solid tumors like
lung, breast, prostate, and colorectal cancer, the leading causes of cancer-related deaths. This research
proposal seeks to unravel the molecular and cellular biology of a specialized type of immune cell, called natural
killer (NK) cell, and to harness it as a therapy to eradicate solid tumors by applying state-of-the-art methods and
innovative synthetic biology and bioengineering strategies. Our goal is to create the next generation of cell-based
therapies that maximize the effectiveness of NK cells against solid tumors, potentially transforming cancer
...

application_id: 10685017
activity_code: DP2
title: Live-Cell Chromatin Imaging and Biology: Application to Extrachromosomal DNA
org_name: CLEVELAND CLINIC LERNER COM-CWRU
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Extrachromosomal DNA (ecDNA) represents a new cancer hallmark and Cancer Grand
Challenge as we currently lack experimental tools and quantitative methods by which to dissect
mechanisms of ecDNA regulation in the cancer genome at a single cell level. In this project, we
will develop innovative live-cell chromatin visualization, manipulation, and profiling tools, which
will allow us to probe the fundamental rules of cancer ecDNA regulation. Understanding these
fundamental ecDNA regulatory principles is essential to design novel strategies aimed at
improving the treatment of ecDNA-associated cancer.

title: Baylor Research Education Program in Neurosurgery
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: Baylor Research Education Program in Neurosurgery Narrative The primary goal of this research education program is to provide neurosurgery residents with the opportunity to complete a cohesive, significant research project during their residency training. The program will mentor residents through the entire research process, from project conception to experimental design, data analysis and interpretation, to publication of results and finally to transitioning to a junior faculty position. This hands-on approach is the most effective way to prepare the next generation of young clinician scientists for a productive research career in neurosurgery.

application_id: 10687588
activity_code: DP2
title: Decoding natural protective mechanisms during diapause and longevity to counter aging
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: Project Narrative
Aging is the primary risk factor for various diseases, including cancer, heart disease, and neurological
disorders such as Alzheimer’s. Strategies to slow or pause aging have immense potential to delay the onset of
diseases and extend the healthy periods of our lives. In this work, we will study naturally evolved strategies to
slow or pause aging in killifish, identify new genes and pathways for healthy lifespan extension, and pave the
way for future therapeutics to extend human health.

title: Multidisciplinary Characterization of the Gut-Brain Axis in Addiction
org_name: RUTGERS, THE STATE UNIV OF N.J.
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Addiction is a chronic, relapsing disorder that affects millions of people in the United States and worldwide, and
yet no successful treatments have been developed. In the past years, the composition and type of bacteria that
inhabit our gut (the gut microbiota) has emerged as a crucial regulator of brain maturation, function, and response
to different diseases, including addiction; however, the mechanisms behind this communication remain mostly
unknown. This project proposes a novel multidisciplinary approach directed to characterize clear and
mechanistically defined interactions between the gut microbiota, psychostimulant use, and addiction, which can
lead to the development of more successful evidence-based systemic interventions for treating this debilitating
psychopa...

activity_code: DP2
title: Decoding the Spatial Grammar of Developmental Signaling
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: Project Narrative
A defining goal of regenerative medicine is to build individualized tissues from patient-derived stem cells,
However, current approaches do not offer flexible and precise control over stem cell differentiation in space,
limiting our ability to rationally design tissue structures. Here, we propose a new method that will overcome this
limitation by using spatially-resolved optogenetic stimulation to communicate precise instructions to developing
stem cells.

application_id: 10686627
activity_code: DP2
title: A biochemical approach towards subcellular, label-free molecular imaging
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ILLINOIS AT CHICAGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The proposed study is highly relevant to public health as it introduces a new label-free molecular imaging
modality capable of spatially mapping intact biological and clinical samples at subcellular resolution. This new
approach will allow for investigation of subcellular anatomy and pathology with conventional mass
spectrometry instrumentation. Furthermore, it will enable multiplexed analysis of biomarkers as well as high-
precision diagnosis of diseases in existing laboratories.

org_name: UNIV OF MARYLAND, COLLEGE PARK
phr: Project Narrative
Antibiotic-resistant (AR) bacterial infections are a growing public health threat, and studies have identified
environmental sources of AR bacteria, such as wastewater. Sanitary sewer overflows (SSOs) and basement
backups – when wastewater overflows into homes and waterbodies untreated due to aging sewer
infrastructure and frequent extreme precipitation events – are chronic, under-studied public health threats
disproportionately impacting low-income and minority populations that may also place them at greater risk for
AR bacterial infections. This proposal would create a community-driven Water Emergency Team (WET) to
respond to basement backup and SSOs as they occur, employ surveys and interviews to evaluate health risks
caused by these events, and inform a more effective...

application_id: 11187073
activity_code: DP1
title: A single-cell approach to developing non-traditional antibiotics
org_name: PRINCETON UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative:
Due to rising rates of resistance to traditional antibiotics, we are in critical need of novel types of
approaches for combatting life-threatening bacterial infections. Here I propose a new direction
for my lab that harnesses our newfound abilities to monitor gene expression in single bacteria,
mammalian cells, and phages, as well as to manipulate host gene expression in a cell-type-
specific manner. We will combine these new methods to understand bacterial pathogenesis and
phage therapy dynamics at unprecedented single-cell resolution and to engineer new types of
nontraditional phage therapies and host-directed antibiotics to combat the rising antibiotic
resistance crisis.

application_id: 10905368
activity_code: R36
title: Real-Time Monitoring of Relapse in Eating Disorders
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF LOUISVILLE
phr: Project Narrative  Eating disorders are an impairing and deadly psychiatric disorder with high rates of relapse. There is a need to improve our understanding of relapse to inform the design of more efficacious treatments and relapse prevention efforts; however, current research is limited by examining relapse as an outcome longitudinally (i.e., across 1 year) rather than a process that unfold through moment-to-moment choices engagement in ED behavior (i.e., lapses). The current study – informed by existing theory of relapse borrowed from substance use disorders15 – will capture the process at the momentary level, with the ultimate goal of identifying mechanisms of relapse that can be addressed through existing and new treatments.

activity_code: DP2
title: Cross-regulation between loop extrusion, chromatin fiber structure and chromatin-associated RNAs
org_name: ROCKEFELLER UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Recent advances in DNA sequencing-based methods have begun to reveal the molecular factors that explain
how the three-dimensional organization of the human genome is disrupted in both cancer and developmental
diseases. In this proposal, we study how the cohesin complex, one such factor mutated in cancer and
developmental diseases called cohesinopathies, interacts with DNA as it is packaged within cells – wrapped
around histone proteins and associated with RNA molecules. We hope that our results will inform how the
cohesin complex interacts with other factors that shape the packaging of DNA into chromatin, revealing new
strategies for treating dysregulation of genome organization in cancer and cohesinopathies.

application_id: 11238581
activity_code: DP2
title: Scalable Development of Custom Genome Editing Technologies
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Genome editing technologies that permanently modify DNA sequences offer hope for patients suffering from
genetic disorders. However, identifying optimal CRISPR-Cas enzymes that are safe, effective, and optimally
suited to correct disease-causing mutations remains laborious, costly, and low-throughput. To close these gaps
in knowledge and unlock the promise of editing in the clinic, we are developing scalable experimental and
computational approaches to engineer a comprehensive catalog of bespoke CRISPR enzymes that overcome
the limitations of existing tools, which collectively will accelerate the development of new genetic therapies.

title: Bacterial disruption of neuroimmune pathways in a transparent brain
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
This project seeks to understand how infection causes inflammation and injury in the brain. This
work is relevant to public health because many kinds of infections activate the brain's immune
system, which can disrupt blood flow or alter the way neurons communicate with each other. By

needed information to develop treatments that limit brain inflammation, which contributes to the
development of many common neurological conditions in humans.

application_id: 11134802
activity_code: DP2
title: Innovations and mechanisms in tumor subcellular metabolism
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Cancer cells employ lysosome-mediated nutrient acquisition strategies to survive limited nutrient availability in
the harsh tumor microenvironment. Here, we will develop innovative technologies to profile the lysosomal
molecular content at cell-type-specific resolution in tumors in vivo. Our work will enable the discovery of how
cells adapt to fluctuations in nutrient availability and uncover cancer-specific metabolic vulnerabilities that can
be exploited to treat human malignancies.

application_id: 11238577
activity_code: DP2
title: A phylodynamic time machine for solid tumors
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: Project Narrative:
Solid tumors progress towards metastasis as their cells diversify in space and genotype, but by the time nearly
all solid tumors are discoverable, the dynamics governing this diversification are obscured. To overcome this
limitation, I will develop methodology to construct a tumor ‘time machine,’ which enables both the
reconstruction of evolutionary dynamics throughout a tumor’s past and robustly predicts which parts of a tumor
will most rapidly progress in the future.

application_id: 11297096
activity_code: DP2
title: Causal Representation Learning for the Spatial Analysis of Transcriptomic and Imaging Data in Tissue Contexts
org_name: BROAD INSTITUTE, INC.
phr: NIH New Innovators Award
Project Narrative
With the foreseeable explosion of spatial transcriptomic datasets in the coming years, it is of
paramount importance to develop a computational framework that can complement the
experimental revolution that made such datasets possible. In this proposal, we will develop a
causal representation learning framework to obtain representations of the data that elucidate
spatial processes underlying tissue homeostasis and disease. While broadly applicable, we will
demonstrate our framework in three functional contexts, namely inflammation/fibrosis in the gut,
Alzheimer's disease, and classic Hodgkin lymphoma, where understanding how cells regulate
each other to modulate the microenvironment could provide novel therapeutic avenues.

application_id: 11066034
activity_code: DP2
title: Dissecting the mechanism of herpesvirus genome packaging
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Herpesviruses are important human pathogens that can have devastating effects in neonates and immunocompromised individuals, yet there is no cure for any herpesvirus and treatment options are lacking. Here, I dissect the mechanism of an essential feature of the herpesviral lifecycle - viral genome packaging - by exploring the role of a unique accessory factor required for packaging and by developing an assay to monitor packaging in vitro. Detailed mechanistic insight is essential to understand the mechanism of current antiherpesviral drugs and to facilitate the identification of new targets for antivirals.

molecular mechanism(s) through which lifestyle risk factors may modulate the accumulation of and functional

application_id: 11123317
activity_code: DP1
title: Mitochondrial signaling dynamics in cocaine use disorder
org_name: RESEARCH TRIANGLE INSTITUTE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The studies in this proposal aim to develop therapeutics to normalize mitochondrial function in cocaine use
disorders. Using viral strategies to knockout specific mitochondrial genes and fluorescent sensors to measure
aspects of mitochondrial function in awake, behaving mice, we will be able to validate currently available
mitochondrial therapeutics, as well as develop a pipeline for testing future mitochondrial drugs. These studies
will significantly advance the chemistry and pharmacology of substance use disorders by exploring a novel
treatment target and expanding the tools to dissect mitochondrial function.

application_id: 10896335
activity_code: DP1
title: Integrative approaches to dissection of endocrine communication
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA-IRVINE
phr: Project Narrative  Endocrine interactions have been demonstrated as causal and contributory to most common, most notably obesity, type 2 diabetes and resulting cardiovascular complications. The goal of this proposal is to closely bridge computational and experimental methods to systematically dissect mechanisms by which tissues communicate and how these interactions are perturbed in disease settings. Collectively, we will begin with newly developed and unbiased computational approaches which survey population data, validate using high-throughput in vitro assays and evaluate therapeutic potential of new endocrine interactions using mouse models of metabolic disease.

application_id: 11213107
activity_code: DP2
title: Decoding multidrug-resistant pathogen dynamics for clinically-relevant wastewater surveillance
org_name: CHILDREN'S HOSP OF PHILADELPHIA
phr: New surveillance strategies are urgently needed to track and combat the emergence and spread
of multidrug-resistant bacteria (MDRB). We will develop novel techniques to (1) establish the use
of hospital wastewater testing to monitor longitudinal dynamics of MDRB, (2) study the emergence
of novel MDRB genotypes, and (3) adapt wastewater testing to diverse healthcare settings. This
work will significantly expand current applications of wastewater surveillance and our
understanding of MDRB dynamics within hospital wastewater environments, ultimately enabling
more comprehensive infection prevention and control practices.

application_id: 11015862
activity_code: DP2
title: Engineering synthetic cellular crosstalk for transplantation tolerance
org_name: CHILDREN'S HOSP OF PHILADELPHIA
phr: Acute and chronic alloimmune reactions against allografts are the major causes of transplantation rejection. Developing safe and effective immune suppressive treatments to achieve long-term transplantation tolerance has been difficult because we don’t have strategies to specifically intervene with multiple components of the alloimmune reactions without compromising the entire host immune system. Here we will develop an orthogonal vaccine to synthetically bridge the crosstalk between antigen presenting cells and engineered regulatory T cells to simultaneously and specifically suppress alloimmune reactions at both the effector site (allograft) and the priming site (allograft draining lymphoid tissue) for long-term transplantation tolerance.

application_id: 11036352
activity_code: DP1
title: The neuropharmacology of brain activation during stages of drug abuse
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: Narrative Dopamine signaling is key to the rewarding effects of stimulant drugs, but much less is known about the involvement of other neurotransmitters and receptors in response to repeated drug exposure. Whole-brain multimodal imaging will be used to non-invasively measure the interaction between neurotransmitter systems, specifically neuroadaptations occurring at dopamine-glutamate targets. This study will reveal the importance of multi-receptor interactions and the potential role of glutamate and other targets in the treatment of drug addiction.

application_id: 10949819
activity_code: DP2
title: Structural characterization of the elongasome and divisome in mycobacteria
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: Project Narrative
Mycobacterium tuberculosis (Mtb), the causative bacterial pathogen of tuberculosis, stands as the foremost
contributor to mortality resulting from infectious diseases. On a cellular level, Mtb exhibits asymmetrical growth
and division that lead to subpopulations of cells that can survive antibiotic therapy. We will study the protein
systems that facilitate asymmetric growth and division in mycobacteria at the molecular and structural level,
which may aid in the development of new antibiotics and therapies for tuberculosis.

application_id: 11212577
activity_code: DP2
title: Functional genomics of microglial phagocytosis for precision neuroimmune therapeutics
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: Project Narrative:
This project aims to develop the first in vivo CRISPR KO genetic screening platform to identify genetic
regulators of phagocytosis in microglia, the brain resident macrophage, in the context of Alzheimer's disease.
By leveraging advanced hematopoietic stem cell gene editing, barcoding, and transplantation into disease
models, this research will uncover novel therapeutic targets to enhance microglial function and mitigate
cognitive decline in AD. The findings and methods developed here will have broad implications for
understanding the basic biology of tissue resident macrophages and diseases involving tissue-resident
macrophage phagocytosis, such as cancer and atherosclerosis.

application_id: 11136529
activity_code: DP2
title: Inducing Off-pathway Assembly of HIV Gag Polyprotein with Computationally Designed Peptides
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ILLINOIS AT CHICAGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Viral replication requires the formation of infectious particle through the bottom-up self-assembly of
each individual virion. Cellular mechanisms are commonly used to guide the process toward the
correctly assembled structure. This project aims to exploit this inherent vulnerability in the viral life cycle,
and will yield innovative therapeutic strategies to drive the formation of aberrant viral structures using
peptides that mimic native initiators of self-assembly.

title: Transfer learning leveraging large-scale transcriptomics to map disrupted gene networks in cardiovascular disease
org_name: J. DAVID GLADSTONE INSTITUTES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Mapping the network of genes disrupted in human disease allows us to design treatments that correct the network back to the normal state to treat the core disease process rather than only managing symptoms. However, mapping the network requires large amounts of data to learn the connections between genes, and in many human diseases we do not have enough data to confidently map the network, especially in rare diseases and diseases that affect tissues in the body that are difficult to reach to obtain samples to study, like the heart. In this research, we develop a new method for computers to learn connections between genes from tissues where we do have a large amount of data, serving as a knowledge base so that the machine can then confidently map the networks disrupted eve...

application_id: 11213361
activity_code: DP2
title: Gut Microbial Protein Metabolism in Food Allergy and Oral Immunotherapy Efficacy
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MARYLAND BALTIMORE
phr: Project Narrative
Peanut allergy, triggered by specific protein epitopes in peanuts, affects approximately 4.6 million people in the
United States. Peanut Oral Immunotherapy holds therapeutic promise for peanut allergy, though the treatment
is protracted and successfully induces remission in only a small subset of patients. This proposal outlines a
systematic approach to investigate the role of gut microbial protein metabolism in Peanut Oral Immunotherapy
efficacy and the development of a novel engineered probiotic-assisted peanut protein immunotherapy.

application_id: 10951348
activity_code: DP2
title: Exploiting the microbiota-stromal cell axis for microbiota-targeted medicine
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE In response to the pressing need for improved vaccination effectiveness, this proposal sets out to unlock the potential of the microbiota-stromal cell axis, a relatively uncharted territory due to stromal cell complexity and research limitations. Recent evidence highlights the critical role of stromal cells in shaping the local immune environment through the release of chemokines and cytokines. Building upon a groundbreaking prior study by the Principal Investigator demonstrating stromal cells' unique ability to recognize bacterial signals and trigger distinct immune responses, this proposal aims to harness this axis to develop microbiota-targeted therapies, promising a transformative approach to enhancing vaccine-induced immunity and ultimately advancing global health.

application_id: 11162335
activity_code: DP2
title: Dissecting the impact of immune environment on Candida albicans pathogenic potential in the gut
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Candida albicans is a commensal fungus that colonizes the intestine of most healthy people but has the
capacity to cause infections and exacerbate inflammation in susceptible people. Immune status determines
risk for C. albicans-associated disease, but the impact of immune environment on C. albicans inherent
pathogenic potential is not well understood. This proposal aims to comprehensively define the impact both
homeostatic and pathogenic immune environments on C. albicans pathogenic potential.

title: Lipid Antigen Presentation as a Driver of T2D Inflammation
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA-IRVINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
This proposal will increase our understanding of how the immune system causes disease when it incorrectly
recognizes and responds to self-molecules as a threat. By providing understanding of autoimmunity to lipids or
“fats” the outcome of this proposal has the potential to redefine Type 2 Diabetes as an autoimmune disease.
This will provide new strategies for management of Type 2 Diabetes and new targets for therapeutic
development.

title: Harnessing Broad-Specificity Phage Recombinases for Universal Bacterial Editing platforms
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ROCHESTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
This research aims to identify broad-specificity phage-recombination systems that can facilitate straightforward
and efficient genome editing across a diverse spectrum of human pathogens. This approach offers an innovative
means of pinpointing the genetic determinants of bacterial pathogenicity. By comprehending how a bacterium's
genetic makeup determines its capacity to cause disease, we can uncover novel strategies to counteract
bacterial threats to human health.
