application_id: 11142564
activity_code: DP2
title: High-spatial-resolution multi-omics sequencing of brain lesions in multiple sclerosis
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: Project Narrative
This project aims to develop a scalable and transformative technology for simultaneous profiling of multi
epigenomic modalities, whole transcriptome, and a panel of proteins at tissue scale and cellular level in a
spatially resolved manner. It will be applied to brain lesions in multiple sclerosis to generate detailed spatial map
of relevant cells, including interactions among them, and to identify cellular and molecular mechanisms
operating in MS brain lesions. It will improve our understanding of the relationship between tissue organization,
function, and gene regulatory networks in MS.

phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The goal of this project is to understand a significant public health concern, specifically prematurity and its
disproportional impacts on Black and lower income families, and the role social determinates of health, namely
work, may play in maintaining or disrupting the downstream consequences of prematurity through the infant’s
first year of life. One aim is to determine how work factors (including paid leave, work hours, and schedule
flexibility) are related to parents’ involvement in the NICU and their mental health; the second aim is to establish
how these factors are related to infants’ short (NICU discharge) and longer-term (12 month) neurodevelopmental
outcomes in a diverse sample of 250 NICU families. Findings from this study will hopefully elucidate understudied

title: Effects of the oral environment on KSHV shedding in Ugandan adults living with HIV
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: Project Narrative In sub-Saharan Africa, where Kaposi’s sarcoma-associated herpesvirus (KSHV) seroprevalence and HIV incidence are highest in the world, the effects of the oral environment on oral KSHV reactivation and shedding in people living with HIV (PLWH) before Kaposi’s sarcoma (KS) development are unknown. application_id: 10906181
activity_code: DP2
title: Identification of mosquito brain neurons that drive blood-feeding behavior
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: NARRATIVE Mosquitoes spread pathogens to humans through their behavior of blood feeding and identifying and understanding the neurons that control this behavior would enable new strategies to prevent it. Here, I describe an approach using genomics, transgenic mosquitoes, and behavior studies to identify the neurons that are required for mosquitoes to seek out and bite humans. The identification and genetic targeting of these neurons will create a way to understand what causes the attraction of mosquitoes and open new avenues for its prevention.

title: The Molecular Basis of Caste Development and Evolution in Ants
org_name: HARVARD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  The goal of the proposed research is to produce an integrative study of the epigenetic process of caste development in ants. The research will advance our understanding of this cell-type-like differentiation process using three complementary scientific approaches: laboratory, field, and museum studies. Caste is one of the most exaggerated and robust examples of phenotypic plasticity in nature, and this research important advances in understanding the mechanisms and evolution of animal development.

application_id: 10914088
activity_code: DP5
title: Advancing CAR T cell therapy for solid tumors
org_name: ICAHN SCHOOL OF MEDICINE AT MOUNT SINAI
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE CAR T cell therapy for solid tumors remains challenging in part due to inhibitory tumor microenvironments (TME) that are seen even at the earliest stages of cancer. We hypothesize that targeting the TME can mitigate these immunosuppressive effects and enhance CAR T cell killing of lung tumor. Our research strategy will develop a new immunocompetent mouse model that faithfully recapitulates the immunosuppressive TME to test novel therapeutic approaches to overcome these suppressive effects to ultimately advance CAR T cell therapy for solid tumors.

activity_code: DP5
title: A machine-learning platform to illuminate the chemical dark matter in mass spectrometry-based metabolomics
org_name: PRINCETON UNIVERSITY
phr: 8. PROJECT NARRATIVE
––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Whereas scientists can now readily measure the DNA, RNA, and protein content within any biological sample,
measuring the small molecules in that same sample has remained more challenging. This project aims to
develop a suite of machine-learning tools that will allow us to more comprehensively measure both known and
unknown small molecules in any biological sample by analyzing mass spectrometry data. The possibility of
identifying unknown small molecules in high-throughput would increase the scope and efficiency of many
publicly funded studies and open up new perspectives on the chemical world around us.

application_id: 11187698
activity_code: DP5
title: Chemoproteomics and Lipidomics for Identifying Dysregulated Enzymes and Lipids in Cutaneous Disorders.
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
This project aims to advance the treatment of atopic dermatitis (AD) by identifying novel drug targets using the
innovative chemoproteomics platform, Profiling of Active Cutaneous Enzymes (PACE), in combination with
global lipidomics. By analyzing skin samples from both human patients and pre-clinical animal models, we will
discover dysregulated enzymes and develop selective inhibitors, leading to new therapeutic strategies. The
expected outcomes include a deeper understanding of skin lipids and enzymes in health and disease, the
identification of new therapeutic targets, and the potential for applying this platform to other skin diseases.

phr: Project Narrative:
Skin disease affects 1 in 4 Americans, and doctors base diagnosis and management decisions on changes in

application_id: 10914101
activity_code: DP5
title: Investigating the contribution of non-coding genetic variation to rare disorders
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Genetic testing is rapidly emerging as a cornerstone of medicine, enabling targeted therapies for patients with
both common and rare disorders, and empowering patients, families and communities with knowledge about
their condition. However, despite significant advances over the past decade, including the introduction of
clinical whole genome sequencing, clinical genetics remains largely limited to the study of less than 1% of the
genome, the exome. Here we leverage a novel approach for simultaneously mapping the genetic and
epigenetic landscape of a sample to elucidate the functional impact of rare non-coding sequence and structural
variants on gene regulatory patterns and monogenic human disorders.

application_id: 10914694
activity_code: DP5
title: Life After Sport: Prior Injury and Sedentary Behavior as Mechanisms of Later Poor Health
org_name: MARQUETTE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Approximately 8.5 million Americans participate annually in competitive high school or collegiate sport, greatly elevating their risk for musculoskeletal injury and perhaps positioning them for poor long-term health and function. The proposed DP5 research will evaluate the impact of prior knee injury and sedentary behavior as two potential mechanisms of later poor health and reduced function. The findings will inform the way we rehabilitate, counsel, and educate athletes for long-term health and lead to future longitudinal and intervention studies that will ultimately decrease chronic disease in mid-life, enhancing healthspans.

application_id: 11181969
activity_code: DP5
title: Novel high-throughput approaches to discover and dissect human transcriptional protein interactions
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Gene regulation is fundamental to biology and a predictive understanding of how protein specificity impacts
transcription has far-reaching impacts on both medicine and technology. While we know where transcription
factors bind in the human genome, we still do not have a predictive understanding of what happens after DNA
binding; we do not know which cofactors they bind and what effect that has on gene expression. Here, we will
develop and deploy a suite of novel high-throughput technologies designed to identify and understand how
protein-protein interactions between transcription factors and cofactors specify transcription, generating critical
new tools and quantitative data that could be used for next-generation drug discovery.

application_id: 10686570
activity_code: DP2
title: Illuminating neurodegenerative tauopathy from somatic genomic landscapes of single human brain cells
org_name: BRIGHAM AND WOMEN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 Alzheimer’s disease (AD) and other neurodegenerative diseases involve a loss of brain function and brain
cells over time and eventually cause death, affecting a substantial amount of the population as we age. Recent
research has found that brain cells build up new mutations in the DNA (known as somatic mutations) in AD,
which appears to harm the brain cells. This proposal will test for somatic mutations in another
neurodegenerative disease, primary tauopathy, using advanced studies of single cells to look at how mutations
relate the buildup of the misfolded protein tau.

application_id: 11171270
activity_code: DP2
title: Neuroplasticity and cerebral blood flow as key resilience mechanisms in the brains of fast-moving SuperAgers
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MINNESOTA
phr: Project Narrative: Neuroplasticity is facilitated through physical movement and may provide a key
neuroprotective mechanism by which some exceptional older adults maintain extremely high function well into
the advanced stages of aging. This proposal will use an innovative and multimodal approach to characterize
neuroplasticity and brain vascular function in a cohort of 75+ year-olds who walk at exceptionally fast speeds
and are free of cognitive decline and dementia, and whom we have termed “fast-moving SuperAgers.” This
proposal aims to substantially increase our current scientific knowledge of brain resilience in the face of
advanced aging, with the goal of helping older adults maintain cognitive and physical function and prevent
aging decline and disease.

application_id: 11145460
activity_code: DP2
title: Building a Systems Immunopeptidomics Framework to Challenge the Current T Cell Activation Paradigm
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The human immunopeptidome, consisting of thousands of cell surface peptides, is crucial for T cells to
distinguish 'self' from 'nonself' (foreign invaders). Traditionally thought to be ignored by T cells in most tissues,
we propose that self-peptides collectively influence T cell activation to fight threats. We will build an innovative
systems immunopeptidomics framework to demonstrate the regulatory power of the self-immunopeptidome
within the organism, which may radically shift our understanding of the immune system, lead to new therapies,
and lay the foundation for a Human Immunopeptidome Project.

application_id: 10687590
activity_code: DP2
title: Identifying neurons for interoception using simultaneous profiling of activity- and projection- specific populations
org_name: MAX PLANCK FLORIDA CORPORATION
phr: Project Narrative
Interoception, the body’s ability to sense its own internal state and bodily sensations, is important for maintaining
homeostatic set points, but its dysfunction is also hypothesized to underlie both addictive behaviors and
psychiatric disorders. This proposal seeks to uncover the identity of “interoception neurons” in the insular cortex
through the examination of context-dependent valence, as well as the validation of a new molecular profiling
technique to simultaneously identify activity-dependent and projection-specific neuronal populations. By
implementing a strategy to study interoception in non-verbal subjects, we will gain insight into the neural
underpinnings of complex and maladaptive behaviors that underlie anorexia, addiction, and other psychiatric
disorders.

People who use opioids are at increased risk of poor retention in opioid use disorder treatment and HIV care

activity_code: K18
title: Spatial Science Approaches to Enhance Gun Violence Surveillance and RelatedDeterminants
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: Public Health Relevance Section
Little is known about how social determinants of health (SDoH), including neighborhood context and alcohol outlet operating hours, influence the occurrence and risk of gun violence. This project will apply advanced spatial science methods to examine the spatial and temporal dynamics of gun violence in Washington, D.C. neighborhoods from 2014 to the present. Findings from this research will enhance our understanding of how neighborhood-level SDoH and environmental factors contribute to patterns of gun violence, with the goal of improving gun violence reduction programs and policy.

application_id: 10687558
activity_code: DP2
title: Vascular mural cells in the development of the blood brain barrier
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 Fundamental questions remain about the origins, heterogeneity, and functions of brain
vascular cells. This project will define evolutionarily conserved mechanisms that regulate mural
cell development and their contribution to the blood brain barrier. By defining mechanisms of
vascular development in the brain, we can then understand and ameliorate neonatal
neurovascular diseases which currently lack any treatment strategies.

application_id: 11394015
activity_code: DP5
title: The molecular basis of caste development and evolution in ants
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF GEORGIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 The goal of the proposed research is to produce an integrative study of the
epigenetic process of caste development in ants. The research will advance our
understanding of this cell-type-like differentiation process using three complementary
scientific approaches: laboratory, field, and museum studies. Caste is one of the most
exaggerated and robust examples of phenotypic plasticity in nature, and this research
important advances in understanding the mechanisms and evolution of animal
development.

title: Investigating the Role of KIBRA-Dependent Synaptic Function on Hippocampal and Cortical Network Mechanisms Underlying Complex Cognition
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative The KIBRA gene is associated with natural variation in human memory and the complexes it organizes are associated with multiple neuropsychiatric disorders, but the role of KIBRA in regulating circuit dynamics is unknown. activity_code: K00
title: Modulation of alpha-7 nicotinic acetylcholine receptor in HIV-infected microglia and brain organoids
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PUERTO RICO MED SCIENCES
phr: Project Narrative Cathepsin B is a lysosomal protease that plays a significant role in the neurotoxicity caused by HIV-infected macrophages, which are the main reservoirs of HIV in the brain. Cannabinoid receptor type 2 (CB2R) activation by agonists decrease cathepsin B secretion, neurotoxicity, HIV-1 replication, and inflammation in macrophages. Therefore, understanding the mechanisms of CB2R activation in macrophages and cathepsin B neurotoxicity will permit the development of alternative strategies against HIV-associated neurocognitive disorders (HAND).

activity_code: K00
title: Optimizing adherence to lung cancer screening: Applying theory and implementation science to participant engagement
org_name: HACKENSACK UNIVERSITY MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Lung cancer screening with low-dose CT is the only available method to effectively diagnose early-stage disease, holding tremendous potential to change lung cancer outcomes. Initial reports of real-world screening implementation show adherence to annual screening guidelines are less than 50%, demonstrating a critical need for improvement to optimize the individual and population health benefits of lung cancer screening. This research will utilize a mixed methods approach to triangulate data exploring program level challenges and facilitators of LCS adherence, further develop the content and imagery of the adherence reminder message system, and conduct preliminary implementation testing to inform implementation strategies for future pragmatic LCS work.

title: Metabolic regulation of oxidative phosphorylation in pediatric AML relapse
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Relapse is a leading cause of death in pediatric acute myeloid leukemia (AML), and new therapies are needed for relapsed disease. AML relapse is mediated by leukemia stem cells (LSC), which undergo unique metabolic adaptations in order to survive chemotherapy. Emerging evidence shows that LSC require lipid metabolism to mediate relapse. The purpose of this work is to determine how lipid metabolism supports LSC mitochondria and the process of oxidative phosphorylation. This will be done by studying lipid metabolism in AML cell lines as well as using a high-fat diet to drive AML relapse in mice engrafted with pediatric AML. title: Development of a Multiplex Quantitative PCR Assay for HIV and Hepatitis B Virus, for Low- and middle Income Countries
org_name: UNIV OF SCIENCES, TECH & TECH OF BAMAKO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
This project seeks to develop a multiplex real time test that can detect both HIV and Hepatitis B infection
with a single test. The test will be able to detect types of HIV and Hepatitis B viruses that are unique to Mali
and West Africa. The test will also be adapted to the low resources, limited equipment, supplies, and
technicians in a low- and middle-income country (LMIC), such as Mali. I want to become a leader in the
development of tests to diagnose and monitor infections but are adapted for use in LMICs.

application_id: 10781933
activity_code: K08
title: Optimizing outcomes for children and young adults with relapse of B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia after CD19-targeted chimeric antigen receptor T-cell therapy
org_name: CHILDREN'S HOSP OF PHILADELPHIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Chimeric antigen receptor T-cell therapy targeting CD19 (CAR19) has revolutionized the treatment landscape for children and young adults with relapsed or refractory B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia. Unfortunately, however, approximately 50% of children relapse after initial response to CAR19 and their subsequent survival is dismal. This proposal aims to optimize outcomes after post-CAR19 relapse through two mutually informative approaches: 1) A phase 1/2 clinical trial testing the delivery of a novel, dual-antigen targeted CAR designed to overcome the primary mechanisms of CAR19 failure; and 2) An advanced comparative effectiveness study to determine optimal existing salvage treatment approaches for post-CAR19 relapse.

application_id: 11221427
activity_code: K00
title: Identifying vulnerabilities of ovarian cancer persister cells through integrated single-cell analyses
org_name: HARVARD MEDICAL SCHOOL
phr: Mechanisms of residual cell survival following initial therapy, one of the leading causes of cancer recurrence, remain under- studied, in part due to the scarcity of tools and models to faithfully study the evolutionary dynamics of residual disease. My postdoctoral work aims to integrate molecular biology and multi-spectral imaging approaches to extensively characterize transcriptomic landscapes, architecture, cell states, and population dynamics of High Grade Serious Ovarian Cancer tumors that have undergone standard-of-care therapy. This synergistic approach will provide a holistic understanding of the behaviors of residual tumors, guiding the development of targeted therapies aimed at eradicating residual disease to reduce or delay recurrence.

title: Multi-target approach to rational design of novel therapeutics for human African trypanosomiasis
org_name: AHMADU BELLO UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Human African trypanosomiasis causes and estimated 1.35 million loss of disability-adjusted life year (DALY) annually, and 65 million people are at risk infection. In addition, the region records an annual loss of 4. 5 billion US dollars resulting from millions of cattle, camels, and small ruminants being killed by animal trypanosomiasis. To alleviate such heavy burden created by this disease on Africa, this project is designed to find novel compounds that ablate energy production in the disease pathogens and killing them. The success of this project will generate new classes of safe and effective anti-Trypanosoma drug candidates and contribute to health and economic development of Africa.

application_id: 11127542
activity_code: K01
title: Glucagon and insulin act cooperatively in the regulation of prandial hepatic glycogen metabolism
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Glucagon and insulin are considered to be opposing factors for regulating glucose metabolism, yet they are co-
secreted in response to a mixed nutrient meal. Our preliminary data suggests that glucagon and insulin
coordinate, rather than oppose, each other to regulate hepatic glycogen metabolism. In this proposal, we will
test the hypothesis that glucagon and insulin generate a unique hepatic signature that leads to optimal systemic
glucose utilization.

title: Longitudinal microbiome-host interactions and clinical outcomes in drug-resistant tuberculosis patients
org_name: STELLENBOSCH UNIVERSITY
phr: NARRATIVE Host microbial communities (microbiota) profoundly affect our health and are changed by antibiotics. However, microbial disturbances during treatment for drug-resistant tuberculosis (a leading cause of death for which tens of millions of antibiotics doses are prescribed annually) remain uncharacterized and may help explain clinical outcomes and long-term overall health, including post-treatment sequalae. Through leveraging of an already- funded observational cohort of drug-resistant TB patients on treatment, we will evaluate microbiota changes before, during, and after treatment (including how these changes correspond to drug absorption), how specific taxa correspond to microbial metabolic end-products, and the microbiota’s association with treatment response. activity_code: K99
title: Neurobiological mechanisms underlying changes in PTSD-like symptoms after cannabis use
org_name: MEDICAL UNIVERSITY OF SOUTH CAROLINA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The prevalence of comorbidity between PTSD and CUD and the increasing legal status of cannabis support the
need to understand how stress and cannabis interact in the brain. My recent discovery suggests that cannabis
use worsens the severity of key PTSD symptoms. The experiments described in this proposal aim to identify
brain circuits and molecular mechanisms underlying the synergisms between stress and cannabis that can be
exploited as targets for treating the high incidence of comorbid PTSD and CUDs.

activity_code: DP5
title: A preconception cohort study on oil and gas development, fertility, and pregnancy
org_name: BOSTON UNIVERSITY MEDICAL CAMPUS
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Residential segregation, one example of structural racism, has long been recognized as a root cause of disparities in the U.S. and imposes inequitable burden on marginalized communities. The proposed project aims to assess the extent to which residential segregation influences oil and gas development and fertility among couples attempting pregnancy, which will be the first to characterize and evaluate residential segregation during the preconception period. This work seeks to identify opportunities for environmental and reproductive intervention, which will inform the development of targeted interventions to improve population health.

application_id: 11165701
activity_code: F31
title: Quantifying medical exposures to radiation in children with congenital heart defects using advanced Monte Carlo methods and patient-inspired anatomical models
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF FLORIDA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
A congenital heart defect is the most common type of birth defect and medical care for them can span a child’s
entire lifetime. In treating these defects, clinicians often use many imaging modalities involving ionizing radiation,
potentially exposing the child to high cumulative radiation doses and increasing their risk of developing radiation-
induced malignancies. This study proposes a deployable, clinically integrated software system that will compute
organ radiation doses from each imaging procedure a child undergoes in the treatment of a congenital heart
defect using patient-inspired anatomic models for both the establishment of low-dose epidemiology cohorts and
assisting physicians in cancer surveillance programs.

application_id: 11180437
activity_code: DP5
title: Functional ontogeny of pair bonding neural circuits
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO
phr: Project narrative
The mesolimbic dopamine system plays a crucial role in our ability to form close bonds with loved ones, and
atypical activity of this circuitry is a common feature of several neuropsychiatric diseases that feature an onset
during adolescent development. Social experiences play a crucial role in scaffolding brain development, but
the ways in which changing adolescent reward circuits are shaped by early social interactions to facilitate adult
bonding remains unknown. I will leverage the use of the socially monogamous prairie vole system to discover
the activational, functional, and transcriptional features of dopaminergic reward circuit development, furthering
our understanding of the pathophysiology of diseases that involve impaired social bonding behaviors.

application_id: 11244132
activity_code: F31
title: Decoding adaptive functions of STING in cancer progression
org_name: WEILL MEDICAL COLL OF CORNELL UNIV
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 The protein STimulator of INterferon Genes (STING) plays different roles based on its context, as
illustrated by its ability to both impede early tumor progression and later promote metastasis. I am studying the
emergent downstream functions of STING that contribute to its pro- and anti-metastatic effects using a saturation
mutagenesis library our lab has developed along with proteomics to determine if binding partners that interact
with distinct functional domains are responsible for STING’s downstream functions. The long-term goal of my
work is to better understand the mediators of STING’s functions so that its dysregulation in cancer can be better
understood and more effectively targeted with therapeutics.

org_name: NORTHWESTERN UNIVERSITY AT CHICAGO
phr: Project Narrative Despite the prevalence of heart failure with preserved ejection fraction (HFpEF), existing therapies are ineffective and demonstrate the desperate need for a novel therapeutic target. application_id: 11072018
activity_code: K43
title: Use of Peripheral Blood Mononuclear Cells Mitochondrial DNA Content to Detect Hepatocellular Carcinoma in Hepatitis B Virus Infected Nigerians.
org_name: COLLEGE OF MEDICINE, UNIVERSITY OF LAGOS
phr: Project Narrative
It is proposed in this application that the use of a minimally invasive quantitative molecular based
mitochondrial DNA content of Peripheral Blood Mononuclear Cells (PBMC) as diagnostic biomarker to screen
chronic hepatitis B (CHB) infected persons in Nigeria will promote early detection of hepatocellular carcinoma
(HCC), minimize hazards and cost of investigation. I will leverage on the access provided by the K43 career
development award to effectively utilize resources for the proposed research and training activities as well as
the support and guidance provided by my mentors and co-mentors in US, India, and Nigeria for the needed
enablement to transition to an independent researcher and translate the research outcomes to beneficial
public health policy and practice.

title: Targeting a Novel Pocket on ITGAV

phr: Project Narrative The proposed research aims to fill an unmet clinical need in treating aggressive cancers such as colon, pancreatic, and breast. application_id: 11072000
activity_code: K00
title: Understanding Quality of Life and Physical Activity in Black Breast Cancer Survivors
org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: Project Narrative Despite the improvements in breast cancer survival, black women experience suboptimal quality of life, which is why it is important to evaluate their experiences to improve quality of life (QOL) and target healthy behaviors that foster a longer life among racial/ethnic minority breast cancer survivors. Physical activity (PA) is a modifiable behavior with the potential to help reduce racial disparities in QOL. This fellowship will help to establish the relationship between PA and QOL among black women, generate new knowledge and insights to enhance QOL among black women, cultivate community partnerships, and empower other researchers to increase the representation of racial/ethnic minorities in studies focusing on behavioral medicine to meet the needs of this community.
